name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page for finding local family activities using global search, date shortcuts, section navigation, activity highlights, and quick access to personal tools.

core_features: Global header search that lets users search activities by keyword, specific date or date range, and location, and then navigates to browse.html with the appropriate URL parameters.

Date shortcut controls (upcoming Saturday, upcoming Sunday, this weekend, next month) that use getDateShortcuts and navigate directly to browse.html with pre-filled date or date range parameters for the Events category.

Homepage section tiles sourced from getHomeSections that link users into the main content groups (Events, Classes & Programs, Workshops & Camps, Seasonal & Holiday, Memberships & Passes, Map view) using the required navigation rules.
Homepage activity highlights (e.g., featured or popular activities) powered by getHomepageActivityHighlights, linking into activity_detail.html with the activity id in the URL.
Quick summary of user tools (Day Plan, Favorites, Watchlist, Cart) using getUserToolSummaries, giving users one-click access to those tools from the homepage.
Location awareness via getUserDefaultLocation, used to pre-fill the location field in the global search and to show distance information within activity highlights when available.
user_workflows:
Find activities via global search:
1) User opens Home.
2) Page calls getUserDefaultLocation to initialize the location input and getHomeSections to populate the category/section selector in the header search.
3) User enters a keyword, optionally picks a single date or date range, and can keep the default location or change it.
4) User optionally selects a section (e.g., an events-related section) which determines the categoryId to be sent.
5) On search submit, the page builds a URL to browse.html including keyword, date or dateStart/dateEnd, locationId (if available), and categoryId (if a section is selected) and navigates there.
6) browse.html then uses these URL parameters to show matching results.

Jump to time-based event listings with date shortcuts:
1) On load, the page calls getDateShortcuts to obtain upcoming_saturday, upcoming_sunday, this_weekend_start_date, this_weekend_end_date, next_month_start_date, and next_month_end_date.
2) User clicks one of the shortcuts (Upcoming Saturday, Upcoming Sunday, This Weekend, Next Month).
3) The click handler maps the shortcut to the correct URL parameters and navigates to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and the appropriate date or dateStart/dateEnd parameter.
4) browse.html displays events for that time frame.

Browse by main sections from the homepage:
1) On load, the page calls getHomeSections and renders a tile for each returned section.
2) User clicks a section tile.
3) If the section corresponds to an Activity.content_group (e.g., events, classes_programs, workshops_camps, seasonal_holiday), the handler navigates to browse.html with categoryId set from the section’s content_group.
4) If the section represents memberships or map view, the handler navigates to memberships.html or map.html respectively (no additional query parameters required from Home).

Explore featured or popular activities from Home:
1) On load, the page calls getHomepageActivityHighlights at least once (optionally multiple times with different variants or content_groups) to retrieve highlight items.
2) Highlights are rendered as cards showing title, subtitle (if provided), price_summary, next_start_datetime, age range, rating data, location_name, neighborhood, and distance_miles when present.
3) User clicks an activity card.
4) The click handler navigates to activity_detail.html, passing the card’s activity_id as id in the URL.

Use personal tools from Home (Day Plan, Favorites, Watchlist, Cart):
1) On load, the page calls getUserToolSummaries to obtain counts for favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
2) The page displays these counts adjacent to each tool entry point.
3) User clicks on one of the tool items (e.g., Favorites).
4) The click handler navigates to the respective tool page (routes defined globally), without relying on local client storage; any tool-specific state is handled by those pages.

Leverage default location in search and highlights:
1) On load, the page calls getUserDefaultLocation.
2) If a location is returned, its label/city/state are displayed in a location indicator and used to pre-select or pre-fill the location field in the global search.
3) getHomepageActivityHighlights may return distance_miles for each activity, already computed relative to the default location; this value is shown on highlight cards when provided.
4) When the user runs a search without changing the location, the locationId parameter in the browse.html URL is set to the default location’s location_id.
interactions: Typing in the global search keyword field updates an internal keyword state; no API call is made until form submission.
Selecting a date in the global search sets a single-date state and clears any previously selected end date, ensuring either a single date or a range is active but not both simultaneously.
Selecting a date range (start and end date) in the global search updates dateStart/dateEnd state and clears any previously selected single date.

Changing the location input in the global search overrides the default location for this search only; on submit, if the chosen location corresponds to a known location_id it is included as the locationId parameter in the browse.html URL.

Choosing a section in the search (derived from getHomeSections) sets an internal selection that maps to a categoryId value from the section’s content_group; if no section is selected, categoryId is omitted from the resulting URL.

Submitting the global search form triggers client-side validation (e.g., ensuring at most one of date versus dateStart/dateEnd is present) and then navigates to browse.html with the appropriate query parameters set from the search state.

Clicking a date shortcut button reads the corresponding date value from getDateShortcuts, constructs a browse.html URL with categoryId set to the events content group and the appropriate date or dateStart/dateEnd parameter, and performs a navigation.

Clicking a homepage section tile reads the section.id and/or section.content_group to determine whether to navigate to browse.html (with categoryId) or to a dedicated page such as memberships.html or map.html, and then navigates accordingly.
Clicking an activity highlight card reads the card’s activity_id and navigates to activity_detail.html with id set to that value as a URL parameter.

Clicking any user tool summary entry point (Day Plan, Favorites, Watchlist, Cart) navigates to the corresponding tool page, which is responsible for further loading and state using its own URL parameters.

If any API (getHomeSections, getHomepageActivityHighlights, getDateShortcuts, getUserToolSummaries, getUserDefaultLocation) fails or returns empty data, the page shows minimal fallback states (e.g., empty lists or disabled shortcuts) without substituting hardcoded sample content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page itself does not depend on incoming URL parameters; its initial state is derived solely from the assigned interfaces. All outward navigation encodes state exclusively in URL query parameters, not in localStorage or other client persistence. For searches: submitting the global search navigates to browse.html with a URL of the form browse.html?keyword={keyword}&date={singleDate}&dateStart={startDate}&dateEnd={endDate}&locationId={locationId}&categoryId={categoryIdFromSection}. Only parameters that have values are included. If a single date is used, dateStart and dateEnd are omitted; if a date range is used, date is omitted. The categoryId parameter is derived from the selected section’s content_group (when the user uses that selector). For date shortcuts: clicking 'Upcoming Saturday' navigates to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and date set to upcoming_saturday from getDateShortcuts; 'Upcoming Sunday' uses upcoming_sunday; 'This Weekend' uses this_weekend_start_date and this_weekend_end_date mapped to dateStart and dateEnd; 'Next Month' uses next_month_start_date and next_month_end_date mapped to dateStart and dateEnd. For section tiles: clicking an Events, Classes & Programs, Workshops & Camps, or Seasonal & Holiday section navigates to browse.html with categoryId set from that section’s content_group. Clicking the Memberships & Passes tile navigates to memberships.html without additional query parameters from Home; clicking the Map view tile or the header Map link navigates to map.html without additional query parameters. For activity highlight cards: clicking a card navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activity_id}. Home does not store any user-specific state in the URL beyond that used in outgoing navigation to other pages; any persistent user state is assumed to be managed server-side and exposed via the provided interfaces.

components.functionality: Provides a global search entry point allowing users to specify keyword, date or date range, location, and an optional section/category selector, then navigates to browse.html with the appropriate query parameters.
data_binding: getHomeSections (used to populate a selectable list of content sections/categories for categoryId derivation)
getUserDefaultLocation (used to pre-fill location information in the search form)
Activity (conceptual target of search results in browse.html)
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomeSections and getUserDefaultLocation, then populate section selector options and default location in the form.
onKeywordChange: update internal keyword state only.
onDateChange: when a single date is selected, set internal singleDate state and clear any existing dateStart/dateEnd state.
onDateRangeChange: when a start or end date is selected, update internal dateStart/dateEnd state and clear any existing singleDate state.
onLocationChange: update internal location selection; if mapped to a known location_id, store it for use as locationId.
onSectionSelect: store the selected section and derive the categoryId value from the section’s content_group for later use.

onSubmit: validate that either singleDate or dateStart/dateEnd is used (not both), assemble a browse.html URL including keyword, date or dateStart/dateEnd, locationId, and categoryId (only where values exist), and navigate to that URL.

components.functionality: Displays quick date-based shortcuts (such as upcoming weekend and next month) that, when clicked, navigate directly to browse.html with pre-configured date filters for events.
data_binding: getDateShortcuts (provides upcoming_saturday, upcoming_sunday, this_weekend_start_date, this_weekend_end_date, next_month_start_date, next_month_end_date)
ActivitySession (conceptually linked by date filters in browse.html)
event_handlers: onInit: call getDateShortcuts and store the returned dates for each shortcut button.
onClickUpcomingSaturday: build and navigate to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and date set to the upcoming_saturday value.
onClickUpcomingSunday: build and navigate to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and date set to the upcoming_sunday value.
onClickThisWeekend: build and navigate to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and dateStart/dateEnd set from this_weekend_start_date and this_weekend_end_date.
onClickNextMonth: build and navigate to browse.html with categoryId set to the Events content group and dateStart/dateEnd set from next_month_start_date and next_month_end_date.

components.functionality: Shows the main homepage sections as interactive tiles, each explaining and linking into a different content group or tool (events-related categories, memberships, and map view).
data_binding: getHomeSections (provides sections with id, label, content_group, and description)
Activity (for sections tied to Activity.content_group values)
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomeSections and render one tile per returned section using its label and description.

components.functionality: Displays a set of highlighted activities (e.g., featured or recommended) with essential summary information and links to each activity’s detail page.
data_binding: getHomepageActivityHighlights (called with a variant indicating a featured/highlight mode, optional content_groups filter, and a limit to control number of items)
Activity (underlying entity being summarized in the highlights)
Location (location_name, neighborhood, distance_miles surfaced through the interface)
ActivitySession (via next_start_datetime for the next upcoming session)
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomepageActivityHighlights with a configuration for this component (variant, content_groups, limit) and render the returned items as cards.
onActivityCardClick: navigate to activity_detail.html with id set to the card’s activity_id.
onRefresh: optionally re-call getHomepageActivityHighlights (e.g., on user-triggered refresh) and re-render the grid without caching static content.

components.functionality: Optionally shows another set of highlighted activities (e.g., popular or trending) to complement the featured list, also linking to activity_detail.html.
data_binding: getHomepageActivityHighlights (called with a different variant and/or content_groups configuration from the featured component, plus a limit)
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomepageActivityHighlights using a distinct configuration from homepage-featured-activities to retrieve a different highlight set and render cards.
onActivityCardClick: navigate to activity_detail.html with id set to the clicked activity_id.
onVariantChange: if the component supports switching variants (e.g., via a tab), re-call getHomepageActivityHighlights with the new variant and update the grid.

components.functionality: Provides compact counters and entry points for Day Plan, Favorites, Watchlist, and Cart so users can quickly see how many items they have and navigate into those tools.
data_binding: getUserToolSummaries (provides favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count)
event_handlers: onInit: call getUserToolSummaries and display the counts next to each tool label.
onClickFavorites: navigate to the Favorites tool page (URL defined in global navigation, without additional query parameters from Home).
onClickDayPlan: navigate to the Day Plan tool page (URL defined in global navigation).
onClickWatchlist: navigate to the Watchlist tool page (URL defined in global navigation).
onClickCart: navigate to the Cart page (URL defined in global navigation).

components.functionality: Shows the user’s default location (when available) and clarifies that distances and search defaults are based on this location.
data_binding: getUserDefaultLocation (provides location_id, label, city, state, latitude, longitude)
Location (underlying place entity referenced by the default location)
event_handlers: onInit: call getUserDefaultLocation and display its label and/or city/state when present.

onClickChangeLocation: focus or open the location input within the header-search-form component so the user can modify the location used for future searches; no persistent change is stored by Home itself.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Preserve the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a centered, fixed-width page with the global header at the very top (logo + nav, assumed), the default-location-indicator sitting in that header block beneath the logo, and then a large, single white content card centered on a light gray background. Inside this card, the top band uses a split layout anchored to a 12-column grid: an 8-column main hero area on the left and a 4-column vertical sidebar on the right. The left hero area is repurposed as the primary search-and-discovery zone (header-search-form followed by date-shortcuts-strip), while the right sidebar becomes a compact tools/status panel (user-tools-summary-bar). Below this split hero band, the layout collapses to single-column, full-width bands inside the same card: first home-sections-tiles, then homepage-featured-activities, then homepage-popular-activities, all stacked with consistent vertical spacing.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Preserve the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a centered, fixed-width page with the global header at the very top (logo + nav, assumed), the default-location-indicator sitting in that header block beneath the logo, and then a large, single white content card centered on a light gray background. Inside this card, the top band uses a split layout anchored to a 12-column grid: an 8-column main hero area on the left and a 4-column vertical sidebar on the right. The left hero area is repurposed as the primary search-and-discovery zone (header-search-form followed by date-shortcuts-strip), while the right sidebar becomes a compact tools/status panel (user-tools-summary-bar). Below this split hero band, the layout collapses to single-column, full-width bands inside the same card: first home-sections-tiles, then homepage-featured-activities, then homepage-popular-activities, all stacked with consistent vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header with logo, default location, and navigation occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport height on desktop and remains visually light with generous vertical spacing. The main white content card sits below with ample light gray framing (40–64px margin from viewport edges). Inside the card, the upper 45–55% of the card’s height is dedicated to the two-column hero band: approximately 65–70% of the card width for the left hero (search + date shortcuts) and 30–35% for the right sidebar (user tools). The remaining vertical space inside the card is used for three stacked, full-width sections (sections tiles, featured activities, popular activities), each separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. Each section inside the card has about 24px inner padding on all sides, following the base 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious, editorial feel. The hero search area is visually rich but not cramped: the search bar is large and prominent, with ample whitespace above and below. Date shortcuts appear as a compact but readable horizontal strip with small gaps between buttons. The sidebar tools panel is denser than the hero but still consistent with card padding and line spacing, mirroring the compact yet readable Today’s Events list from the mockup. The lower sections (sections tiles, featured and popular activities) use card-based grids with 16–24px gaps between tiles and consistent 24px padding inside each tile. Overall, whitespace and consistent rhythm are prioritized over fitting maximum items on screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey follows the mockup: the eye first catches the colorful logo (assumed existing), immediately followed by the centered default-location-indicator just beneath it, then the horizontal nav. From there, the eye drops straight down into the large central white card. Within the card, attention is first pulled to the left hero search area (large search box at top, then date shortcuts immediately beneath it), then drawn rightwards to the contrasting, vertically-framed user-tools-summary-bar. After interacting with search and tools, the eye naturally continues downward in a linear scan through the full-width bands: first scanning the home-sections-tiles (quick access to content types), then scrolling into the more editorial-style rows of homepage-featured-activities, and finally into the denser but still ordered homepage-popular-activities list.

layout.overall_description: The page sits centered on a soft light gray background, with a fixed-width content column framed by generous side margins on desktop. At the top, the brand logo occupies the visual apex, with the default location label centered directly underneath it on its own line, followed by a single-line navigation bar. This header region feels airy and uncluttered. Below it, separated by about 32px of vertical space, a large white card stretches across the central width of the page, its rounded edges and 24px internal padding giving it a magazine-style, editorial character. The top half of this card is divided into two columns: the left two-thirds form a hero search area where a bold, wide search bar sits prominently at the top, and just beneath it, a tight horizontal strip of date shortcuts runs edge to edge within that left column. On the right, occupying the remaining third of the width, a vertical sidebar panel with a colored header bar hosts the user tools summary; it reads almost like an information column on a magazine cover. Below this hero band, the card transitions into a single-column stack: first a grid of home section tiles (categories such as Events, Activities, Guides), then a visually prominent featured activities grid, then a slightly denser popular activities grid. Each section is clearly separated by consistent vertical spacing and subtle subheadings, so scrolling down feels like moving through distinct editorial sections of a curated guide rather than an app dashboard.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is placed at the very top of the main white content card, anchored within the left hero column. On desktop, it spans roughly 8 out of 12 columns (about 65–70% of the card’s width), aligned to the left but visually balanced so that its right edge vertical line aligns with the gap between left and right hero columns. The search field itself runs nearly full width within this 8-column space (about 90% width, leaving small side padding for breathing room), with a tall input height and prominent placeholder text to give it hero-level presence. There is about 24–32px of whitespace between the bottom edge of the top card padding and the search box, echoing the mockup’s generous hero spacing, and another 16–24px of space beneath the search bar before the date-shortcuts-strip. On large screens, the search is horizontally centered within the page’s max-width container because the entire card is centered, making the search feel like the main entry point. On tablets, the card width contracts but the search still occupies the full top portion, maintaining its dominance. On mobile, the two-column card collapses into a single column: the search form becomes full-width inside the card, sitting at the top, with equal padding (about 16–24px) on the left and right and clear spacing above the next elements. The search is visually treated as a primary hero, analogous to the carousel in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow but responsive: on desktop/tablet it is constrained to the left hero column; on mobile it automatically spans the full width of the card as the layout stacks. It does not stick on scroll by default, but because it is high on the page and large, it remains the first interaction users see.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-shortcuts-strip sits directly below the header-search-form within the same left hero column, forming a tight, functional band that echoes the mockup’s compact sub-elements under the main hero. On desktop, it matches the width of the search form (about 8 columns) and sits approximately 16–24px beneath the bottom of the search bar. The strip is a horizontal row of pill-style buttons or simple text links representing date options (Today, Tomorrow, This Weekend, etc.), centered vertically within a low-height band (about 48–56px tall). Buttons have 8–12px of left/right padding and 4–8px vertical padding, with 8–12px gaps between them, mirroring the moderately compact spacing of the Today’s Events list. The strip’s left edge aligns with the search bar’s left edge, and its right edge aligns with the split between the hero and sidebar columns, creating a clean vertical boundary. Below the strip, there is about 24–32px of vertical space before the content transitions into the next full-width section (home-sections-tiles). On tablets, the strip remains a single row if space permits, otherwise wraps gracefully to two lines while preserving 8px-based spacing. On mobile, it spans 100% of the card width, and the individual shortcut chips may scroll horizontally within the strip if they exceed screen width, keeping the vertical footprint compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero column and responsive: it follows the search form as a secondary control band. On mobile, it may enable horizontal scrolling within its own container to prevent excessive vertical stacking, while maintaining its position immediately beneath the search box.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-sections-tiles component starts just below the two-column hero band and stretches the full internal width of the main white card. Visually, it reads as the first major section after the hero, similar to a featured content row in a magazine. There is a clear vertical gap of about 32px between the bottom edge of the date-shortcuts-strip (or right sidebar tools panel, whichever ends lower) and the top of this section. A section heading (e.g., “Explore by Section”) sits at the top-left of this band with about 8–16px spacing above the grid of tiles. The tiles themselves are arranged in a responsive grid based on the 12-column system: on large desktop screens, use 3–4 tiles per row (e.g., each tile spanning 3 or 4 columns) with 24px horizontal and vertical gaps; on tablets, 2–3 tiles per row; on mobile, a simple single-column stack with each tile spanning the full width. Each tile is a card with 16–24px internal padding and a clear border or shadow, echoing the card treatment used elsewhere. The horizontal padding of the tile grid aligns with the card’s overall 24px left and right padding, so the tiles sit neatly within the white card’s bounds. Vertically, the section takes up enough height to show at least one full row of tiles above the fold on most laptop viewports, reinforcing its role as a quick path to deeper sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the single-column content flow, expanding and contracting with the card’s width. The grid layout reflows responsively (adjusting tile count per row) while maintaining consistent 8px-based gutters and alignment with the global 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-featured-activities section sits directly below the home-sections-tiles, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px, and also spans the full width of the main white card. It is treated visually like an editorial feature band, akin to a spread of highlighted stories. A left-aligned section title (e.g., “Featured Activities”) sits at the top with 8–16px spacing above a grid of activity cards. On desktop, this grid can present 3 cards per row (each spanning 4 columns of the 12-column grid) with 24px horizontal gaps and 24–32px vertical spacing between rows, giving a balanced yet roomy feel. Each activity card includes an image thumbnail at the top, followed by title and brief meta information, with approximately 16px internal padding and 8–12px spacing between text lines. The overall content density is moderate, slightly richer than the section tiles but still airy. On tablets, the grid adjusts to 2 cards per row; on mobile, the cards stack in a single column. Horizontal alignment is consistent with the other sections: the card grid’s left and right edges align with the inside padding of the main white card. The section height can extend down the page as needed, but it remains visually separated from the following popular activities by maintaining a consistent section gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the scroll flow, functioning as a large editorial band. The grid reflows responsively but maintains fixed internal padding and consistent card sizing relative to the 12-column grid. No sticky or floating behavior is applied here.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-popular-activities section follows the featured activities band, separated by another 24–32px of vertical whitespace, and also spans the full width of the main white card. It is slightly denser in content than the featured section, evoking a curated list-style editorial area. At the top, a left-aligned heading such as “Popular Right Now” introduces the section. Below, activities can be presented either as a tighter grid of smaller cards (e.g., 3–4 items per row depending on available width) or as a two-column list on desktop, with 16–24px horizontal gaps and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. Cards in this section may be slightly more compact than featured cards (e.g., slightly smaller image or less vertical padding), in line with the mockup’s balance between hero content and supporting lists. On tablets, the layout drops to 2 items per row; on mobile, it collapses into a vertical list of full-width cards with consistent 16px internal padding. The left/right bounds of the layout line up perfectly with the sections above, maintaining a clean vertical column. This section can extend further down the page, forming the bottom portion of the main card; below it, a final 32–40px of bottom padding inside the card provides breathing room before the card’s end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow. It responds to the viewport by adapting the number of items per row but does not change its relative position or become sticky. It forms the primary scrolling content at the bottom of the homepage card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar occupies the right-hand hero sidebar, mirroring the location and treatment of the Today’s Events panel in the mockup. On desktop, it spans approximately 4 columns of the 12-column grid (about 30–35% of the card width), aligned flush to the right edge inside the main white card. From the top edge of the card, there is about 24px of padding before the bar begins. The component is contained within its own vertical card, with a colored header strip (e.g., pink, similar to the TODAY’S EVENTS label) at the top containing a concise label such as “Your Tools” or “My Stuff,” followed by a stacked list of concise rows summarizing key user items (saved activities count, upcoming RSVPs, profile link, etc.). Each list row has 8–12px vertical padding with small gaps between items, echoing the compact but readable list in the mockup’s events sidebar. The bar’s total height fits comfortably within the hero area, roughly aligning its bottom edge with or slightly below the bottom of the date-shortcuts-strip, without extending too far into the sections below; if content exceeds this, it can scroll internally. There is 24px of padding between the right edge of this sidebar card and the outer right edge of the main content card. On tablet widths where two columns are still viable, this sidebar remains to the right but may widen slightly (4–5 columns). On mobile, it stacks beneath the search and date shortcuts, spanning the full width of the card and preserving its internal card style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the hero area on desktop and larger tablets, the user-tools-summary-bar can be made sticky relative to the top of the main card: as the user scrolls within the hero, it stays pinned until the main card’s top scrolls out of view. Below that threshold, it scrolls normally with the content. On mobile, where the layout collapses to a single column, it behaves as a standard static block beneath the search and date shortcuts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The default-location-indicator is positioned in the global header area above the main white content card, mirroring the location label placement in the mockup. It sits centered horizontally on the page, directly beneath the brand logo block and above the main navigation bar. There is approximately 8–16px of vertical space between the logo and the location indicator, and another 8–16px between the location indicator and the nav, maintaining the header’s airy feel. The indicator itself is a single line of text (optionally with a small location pin icon) with modest font size, giving it clear visibility without overpowering the logo or nav. It does not extend across the full width; instead, it has a natural text width, creating a tight, focused line of information in the center of the header column. On narrower screens, it remains centered, with the logo stacked above and the nav or menu icon below, maintaining roughly the same vertical spacing. The presence of this indicator helps anchor the sense of locality before users reach the search and main content card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header stack. It sits between the logo and navigation and scrolls away with the header content when the user scrolls down the page. If the header is configured to be sticky globally, the location indicator remains visible as part of that sticky header; otherwise, it behaves like any other header element.

name: Browse Activities
title: Browse Activities
description: Browse and filter local family activities (events, classes, workshops, seasonal activities) by date, age, price, time, distance, and other criteria, in list or calendar view.

core_features: Load and apply context-specific defaults based on the categoryId URL parameter (e.g., events, classes_programs, workshops_camps, seasonal_holiday) to preconfigure filters and labels.

Retrieve and display dynamic filter options (age ranges, price ranges, time of day, day of week, distance, categories, schedule/recurrence, rating, tags, accessibility, sort options) via getActivityFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.
Use getDateShortcuts to offer quick-select date and date range options (e.g., upcoming weekend or next month) within the date range picker for browsing activities.
Use getUserDefaultLocation to initialize distance-based filters and to support distance sorting and display for activity results.

Search and paginate activities via searchActivities using current URL parameters (keyword, date/date range, age, price, time, distance, categories, tags, accessibility, schedule, rating, sort, page) and display results in list or calendar view.
Allow switching between list and calendar presentation modes using the view URL parameter while preserving all filter and sort URL state.
Enable navigation to activity_detail.html from any activity card, including passing a returnUrl parameter so users can return to the same filtered browse state.
Enable navigation to map.html with all current filter and date parameters translated into the appropriate query parameters for the map view.
Show live counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, cart items) by calling getUserToolSummaries and expose them as status indicators on the page.
Allow users to add activities to a favorites list using addActivityToFavorites and update both the local UI state and the user tools summary counts.
Allow users to add applicable activities to a watchlist using addActivityToWatchlist (especially for ongoing classes/programs) and update both the local UI state and the user tools summary counts.
Maintain all browsing state (filters, sort, view, pagination) purely via URL parameters so that navigation, sharing, and back/forward browser actions restore the same results.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from header navigation with a content context
1. User clicks a header link such as 'Events', 'Classes & Programs', 'Workshops & Camps', or 'Seasonal & Holiday'.
2. The site navigates to browse.html with categoryId set to the corresponding content_group and may omit other filters.
3. On page load, page-init-controller reads categoryId and calls getActivityFilterOptions using this context/content_group.
4. page-init-controller also calls getDateShortcuts and getUserDefaultLocation to prepare date shortcuts and distance context.
5. page-init-controller derives initial searchActivities parameters from URL (or sensible defaults when parameters are absent) and calls searchActivities.
6. The activity-results-list (or activity-calendar-view if view=calendar) renders the first page of results.
7. context-header highlights the active content_group based on categoryId, and filter-sidebar initializes controls from URL parameters and the filter options data.

Workflow: Refine results using filters and date controls
1. User adjusts a filter in filter-sidebar (e.g., selects an age range, enables free-only pricing, changes distance, selects categories/tags/accessibility) or updates the date/date range in date-range-picker.
2. The respective component converts the user’s selection into URL parameter values (e.g., ageRange id to minAge/maxAge, freeOnly boolean, distanceMax number, categories/tags/accessibility CSV strings, date/dateStart/dateEnd).
3. The component updates the browser URL query string (replacing or adding the changed parameters, resetting page=1) without reloading the entire page if possible.
4. page-init-controller listens for URL changes, recomputes the searchActivities parameters, and calls searchActivities.
5. When the new results arrive, activity-results-list or activity-calendar-view updates to display them, and pagination-controls recalculates page counts.
6. The active filters are visually reflected in filter-sidebar and possibly as removable filter chips, all derived from current URL parameters.

Workflow: Search by keyword within a context
1. User types a search term into activity-search-bar and submits the form.
2. activity-search-bar updates the keyword URL parameter (and resets page=1) while preserving other filter parameters.
3. page-init-controller reacts to the updated URL, constructs searchActivities parameters including the keyword, and calls searchActivities.
4. activity-results-list/activity-calendar-view updates with keyword-filtered results.
5. If the user clears the search field, activity-search-bar removes or empties the keyword parameter and triggers a new search with all other filters remaining intact.

Workflow: Switch between list and calendar views
1. User clicks a toggle in sort-and-view-bar to change from list to calendar or vice versa.
2. sort-and-view-bar updates the view URL parameter accordingly while keeping all filter, sort, and pagination parameters.
3. page-init-controller checks the new view value and determines which presentation component to display: activity-results-list for list view or activity-calendar-view for calendar view.
4. The inactive view component is hidden or not rendered; the active component uses the current searchActivities results to render activities structured either as a linear list or grouped by date/time.
5. The user can continue to adjust filters, date, or sort; only the visible view component updates, but the shared results data pulls from the same searchActivities call.

Workflow: Sort results and paginate
1. User changes the sort order via a control in sort-and-view-bar (e.g., selects a different sort option value from getActivityFilterOptions.sort_options).
2. sort-and-view-bar updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page=1.
3. page-init-controller calls searchActivities with the new sort_by value.
4. Results refresh in the active view component with the new sort order.
5. User uses pagination-controls to move to another page; pagination-controls updates the page URL parameter.
6. page-init-controller calls searchActivities with the updated page number while preserving all other parameters.
7. activity-results-list/activity-calendar-view updates to show the new page of results.

Workflow: View activity details and return to the same browse state
1. User clicks an activity card in activity-results-list or a specific activity entry in activity-calendar-view.
2. The click handler constructs a URL for activity_detail.html that includes id set to the activity_id and returnUrl set to the current full browse URL (including all query parameters).
3. The browser navigates to activity_detail.html.
4. If the user clicks a 'Back to results' action in the detail page (handled there), that page navigates back to the returnUrl provided, restoring the user to the same filters, view, sort, and page of results.

Workflow: Open current results in map view
1. User clicks a map toggle or link in sort-and-view-bar.
2. sort-and-view-bar maps the current browse URL parameters into the expected map.html parameters:
   - dateStart/dateEnd from current date/dateStart/dateEnd state,
   - activityCategories from current categories,
   - contentGroups from current categoryId/content_group,
   - timeOfDay from timeOfDay,
   - daysOfWeek from dayOfWeek,
   - tags from tags,
   - accessibility from accessibility,
   - priceType derived from freeOnly (e.g., 'free' when freeOnly is true, or omitted/neutral otherwise),
   - priceMax from priceMax.
3. The component navigates to map.html with these query parameters.
4. The map page (defined elsewhere) initializes its own filters based on the incoming URL, staying consistent with the browse view settings.

Workflow: Save activities to favorites
1. User sees an activity card in activity-results-list or activity-calendar-view and clicks a 'save to favorites' control.
2. favorites-action-handler is invoked with the activity_id and calls addActivityToFavorites.
3. Upon success, favorites-action-handler updates UI state (e.g., marking this activity as favorited) using only dynamic data (such as returned favorite_item_id and updated favorites_count).
4. favorites-action-handler also refreshes user-tools-summary-bar counts by calling getUserToolSummaries or by using the favorites_count returned from addActivityToFavorites.
5. If addActivityToFavorites returns an error, the handler shows an appropriate error state within the activity card (e.g., a message or retry option).

Workflow: Save activities to watchlist
1. When browsing primarily classes/programs or other ongoing activities, user clicks a 'save to watchlist' control on an activity card.
2. watchlist-action-handler is invoked with the activity_id and calls addActivityToWatchlist.
3. On success, watchlist-action-handler updates card-level watchlist state and user-tools-summary-bar using the returned watchlist_item_id and watchlist_count.
4. If the watchlist action is attempted in a context where watchlists are not relevant, the UI may hide or disable the watchlist control based on categoryId or content_group logic.
5. Any errors from addActivityToWatchlist are surfaced on the card for user feedback.

Workflow: Monitor user tools summary
1. On page load, page-init-controller calls getUserToolSummaries.
2. user-tools-summary-bar displays dynamic counts for favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, and cart items.
3. When favorites-action-handler or watchlist-action-handler succeed, they either use returned counts or instruct page-init-controller to refresh getUserToolSummaries.
4. The updated counts appear in user-tools-summary-bar, and other components can also read this shared state if needed (e.g., to show badges).
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Page is loaded with or without URL parameters.
- Behavior: page-init-controller parses all incoming URL parameters (categoryId, keyword, date/dateStart/dateEnd, ageRange, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, freeOnly, timeOfDay, dayOfWeek, distanceMax, categories, scheduleRecurrence, ratingMin, tags, accessibility, sortBy, page, view).
- Data calls: getActivityFilterOptions (with context/content_group derived from categoryId), getDateShortcuts, getUserDefaultLocation, getUserToolSummaries, then searchActivities using derived parameters.
- Outcome: Filters, sort controls, date controls, view mode, and results components all initialize in sync with the URL.

Interaction: Change date or date range
- Trigger: User selects a new single date or date range from date-range-picker, possibly using quick shortcuts from getDateShortcuts.
- Behavior: date-range-picker updates either date (single date mode) or dateStart/dateEnd (range mode) in the URL, clears the other date-type parameter when necessary (to avoid ambiguity), and resets page=1.
- Data calls: page-init-controller calls searchActivities with updated date_start/date_end while preserving other filters.
- Outcome: Results refresh to match the selected date/time window; date controls display the active selection derived from URL parameters.

Interaction: Adjust age range
- Trigger: User selects an age range option in filter-sidebar.
- Behavior: filter-sidebar uses the selected age_range option (from getActivityFilterOptions.age_ranges) to set ageRange in the URL and also updates minAge and maxAge URL parameters using the option’s min_age/max_age values. If the user clears the age filter, these parameters are removed.
- Data calls: page-init-controller calls searchActivities with updated min_age and max_age.
- Outcome: Results are constrained to activities whose age ranges overlap the selected range.

Interaction: Adjust price and free-only filters
- Trigger: User selects a price range or toggles free-only within filter-sidebar.
- Behavior: For a free-only toggle, filter-sidebar sets freeOnly in the URL; for other price ranges, it sets priceMax based on a selected option from getActivityFilterOptions.price_ranges. Clearing price filters removes the related URL parameters.
- Data calls: page-init-controller converts freeOnly into an appropriate price_type parameter for searchActivities (e.g., 'free' when true, or unspecified when false) and sets price_max from priceMax, then calls searchActivities.
- Outcome: Results update to show only activities within the chosen price constraints.

Interaction: Adjust time of day and day of week
- Trigger: User selects one or more time-of-day buckets or days of the week in filter-sidebar.
- Behavior: filter-sidebar sets timeOfDay to a selected value (or list where supported) and dayOfWeek to a comma-separated list of selected day values (from getActivityFilterOptions.day_of_week_options) in the URL.
- Data calls: page-init-controller maps timeOfDay to time_of_day_buckets and dayOfWeek to days_of_week arrays for searchActivities.
- Outcome: Results are limited to sessions matching the chosen time windows and days.

Interaction: Adjust distance, categories, tags, accessibility, schedule, and rating
- Trigger: User changes any of these filters in filter-sidebar.
- Behavior: filter-sidebar writes the following URL parameters: distanceMax (number), categories (CSV of activity_category values), tags (CSV), accessibility (CSV), scheduleRecurrence (single value), ratingMin (number). Clearing a filter removes its parameter.
- Data calls: page-init-controller converts these parameters into searchActivities arguments: distance_max_miles, activity_categories, tags, accessibility_features, schedule_recurrence, rating_min.
- Outcome: Results reflect the combination of all active filters.

Interaction: Change sort order
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from sort-and-view-bar.
- Behavior: sort-and-view-bar sets sortBy in the URL and resets page=1.
- Data calls: page-init-controller passes sortBy directly to searchActivities.sort_by and calls searchActivities.
- Outcome: Results reorder according to the selected sort criterion.

Interaction: Toggle between list and calendar view
- Trigger: User clicks a toggle in sort-and-view-bar to change the view.
- Behavior: sort-and-view-bar updates the view URL parameter to either 'list' or 'calendar'.
- Data calls: No additional API call is required solely for view change; existing searchActivities results are reused, though page-init-controller may re-trigger searchActivities if any other parameters changed concurrently.
- Outcome: Either activity-results-list or activity-calendar-view becomes visible based on view, while data remains consistent.

Interaction: Navigate between pages of results
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous or a specific page number in pagination-controls.
- Behavior: pagination-controls updates page in the URL.
- Data calls: page-init-controller calls searchActivities with the updated page value.
- Outcome: The visible set of results updates to the selected page; pagination-controls reflects the current page from the searchActivities response.

Interaction: Open activity details
- Trigger: User clicks on an activity card in list view or a cell/entry in calendar view.
- Behavior: The card’s click handler builds a navigation URL to activity_detail.html with id set to the activity_id and returnUrl set to the current browse.html URL (including all query parameters).
- Data calls: None for the browse page at click time; subsequent logic occurs on activity_detail.html.
- Outcome: User navigates to the activity detail page and can later return to the same browse state via returnUrl.

Interaction: Add activity to favorites
- Trigger: User clicks a favorites control on an activity card.
- Behavior: favorites-action-handler calls addActivityToFavorites with the activityId.
- Data calls: addActivityToFavorites, optionally followed by getUserToolSummaries if broader counts need refreshing.
- Outcome: On success, the specific card reflects a favorited state and user-tools-summary-bar shows updated favorites_count; on failure, the card shows an error state.

Interaction: Add activity to watchlist
- Trigger: User clicks a watchlist control on an activity card (typically in classes/programs contexts).
- Behavior: watchlist-action-handler calls addActivityToWatchlist with the activityId.
- Data calls: addActivityToWatchlist, optionally followed by getUserToolSummaries.
- Outcome: On success, the card reflects a watchlisted state and user-tools-summary-bar shows an updated watchlist_count; on failure, the card shows an error state.

Interaction: View user tools summary
- Trigger: Page load and after successful favorites/watchlist actions.
- Behavior: page-init-controller calls getUserToolSummaries and stores the returned counts; user-tools-summary-bar reads and displays them.
- Outcome: Users see up-to-date counts for favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, and cart items at all times.
page_functionality.state_logic:
All page state is controlled via URL parameters and derived from them. On initialization, the page reads all incoming URL parameters and uses them to compute arguments for the assigned interfaces.

- categoryId: Determines the Activity.content_group context and labels. On load, page-init-controller maps categoryId to the content_groups value passed to searchActivities (typically as a single-element collection) and also passes content_group/context to getActivityFilterOptions. Changing categoryId (e.g., via header navigation) resets filters that are context-specific and triggers a new search.

- Date vs date range:
  - When date is present and dateStart/dateEnd are absent, the page operates in single-date mode. page-init-controller sets both searchActivities.date_start and searchActivities.date_end to this same date.
  - When dateStart and/or dateEnd are present, the page operates in range mode. dateStart maps to searchActivities.date_start, dateEnd to searchActivities.date_end. In this mode, the date parameter is ignored.
  - User actions in date-range-picker ensure mutual exclusivity: selecting a single date sets date and clears dateStart/dateEnd; selecting a range sets dateStart/dateEnd and clears date.
  - getDateShortcuts is used to pre-fill date, dateStart, and dateEnd based on user selection of a shortcut; the resulting values are written into the URL.

- Age filters:
  - ageRange holds the id of a chosen age range from getActivityFilterOptions.age_ranges.
  - When ageRange is set, filter-sidebar also writes minAge and maxAge to the URL using the selected option’s min_age/max_age.
  - page-init-controller passes minAge and maxAge directly to searchActivities.min_age and searchActivities.max_age.
  - Clearing the age filter removes ageRange, minAge, and maxAge from the URL, and searchActivities is called without age constraints.

- Price and free-only:
  - freeOnly is a boolean parameter. When true, page-init-controller sets searchActivities.price_type to the enum value for free; when false or absent, price_type is left unspecified so that non-free activities are also returned.
  - priceMax is a numeric parameter derived from a user-selected price range option. page-init-controller passes priceMax to searchActivities.price_max.
  - If freeOnly is true, UI logic can clear priceMax or leave it unreferenced in searchActivities, depending on implementation, but the authoritative state remains in URL parameters.

- Time and day-of-week:
  - timeOfDay stores a single value selected from getActivityFilterOptions.time_of_day_options; page-init-controller maps this to a single-element array for searchActivities.time_of_day_buckets.
  - dayOfWeek stores a comma-separated list of values from getActivityFilterOptions.day_of_week_options. page-init-controller splits this string and passes an array of values to searchActivities.days_of_week.

- Distance and location:
  - distanceMax is a numeric URL parameter representing the search radius in miles. It is passed to searchActivities.distance_max_miles.
  - getUserDefaultLocation is used to establish the reference point for distance calculations on the backend; the browse page does not store location in URL parameters, but distanceMax interacts with the user’s default location.

- Categories, tags, accessibility, schedule, rating:
  - categories is a CSV of Activity.activity_category values. page-init-controller splits this into an array and passes it as activity_categories to searchActivities.
  - tags is a CSV of tag values; page-init-controller passes these as tags to searchActivities.
  - accessibility is a CSV of accessibility feature values; page-init-controller passes these as accessibility_features.
  - scheduleRecurrence is mapped directly to searchActivities.schedule_recurrence.
  - ratingMin (number) is mapped directly to searchActivities.rating_min.

- Sorting and pagination:
  - sortBy is a string that must match one of getActivityFilterOptions.sort_options.value entries. It is passed to searchActivities.sort_by.
  - page is a number representing the current page. It is passed to searchActivities.page. page-init-controller determines a page_size (not stored in URL) and passes it to searchActivities.page_size.
  - Changing any filter, date, keyword, or sort resets page to 1. Pagination-controls manipulates only the page parameter.

- View mode:
  - view controls the presentation mode. Expected values are 'list' or 'calendar'.
  - page-init-controller reads view and decides whether to render activity-results-list (list mode) or activity-calendar-view (calendar mode). The searchActivities call is identical for both; only presentation logic differs.
  - Changing view does not modify other URL parameters and does not change searchActivities arguments besides possibly re-triggering a fetch for consistency.

- Keyword search:
  - keyword is mapped directly to searchActivities.keyword.
  - activity-search-bar updates keyword and resets page to 1 on submission.

- Outgoing navigation state mapping:
  - When navigating to activity_detail.html, the current URL (including all parameters) is URL-encoded and stored in the returnUrl parameter so the detail page can navigate back to this exact state.
  - When navigating to map.html, categoryId maps to contentGroups, categories maps to activityCategories, date/dateStart/dateEnd map to dateStart/dateEnd for the map, timeOfDay maps to timeOfDay, dayOfWeek maps to daysOfWeek, tags maps to tags, accessibility maps to accessibility, freeOnly maps to priceType (e.g., 'free' when true), and priceMax maps to priceMax. These mappings are constructed directly from current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes page state from URL parameters, orchestrates data fetching from all assigned interfaces, and keeps components synchronized when the URL changes.

Displays top-level context tabs based on categoryId (e.g., events, classes_programs, workshops_camps, seasonal_holiday) and reflects which context is active. Switching tabs updates categoryId and resets or adjusts context-specific filters, causing a new search.
Shows real-time counts of user-related tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, cart items). Updates when the page loads and after favorites/watchlist operations.
Allows users to enter a keyword to search within the current activity context and filters. Updates the keyword URL parameter and triggers a new search when submitted or cleared.

Provides controls to select a single date or a date range for browsing activities, including quick-select options based on getDateShortcuts. Ensures mutual exclusivity between date and dateStart/dateEnd in URL parameters.

Displays dynamic filter controls for age, price, time of day, day of week, distance, categories, schedule/recurrence, rating, tags, and accessibility based on getActivityFilterOptions. Writes the corresponding URL parameters when filters change and requests a new search via the controller.

Provides sort selection, view mode toggle (list or calendar), and a control to open the current results in the map view. Updates sortBy and view URL parameters and triggers navigation to map.html with mapped parameters.

Displays searchActivities results in a paginated list layout when view is set to list. Each item shows key activity information and provides controls for opening details and saving to favorites or watchlist.

Displays searchActivities results organized by date and time when view is set to calendar. Uses the next_start_datetime and related fields from searchActivities.items to place activities into calendar cells, with access to details and favorites/watchlist actions.
Renders pagination based on searchActivities.total_count and page_size, and updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates between pages.
Encapsulates the logic for adding an activity to the favorites list. Called by UI components and updates both the local UI state and the user tools summary based on addActivityToFavorites results.
Encapsulates the logic for adding an activity to the watchlist. Called by UI components and updates local UI state and user tools summary based on addActivityToWatchlist results.

Reads current URL parameters and displays a summary of active filters as removable chips or tokens. Removing a chip modifies the corresponding URL parameter and triggers a new search via the controller.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a centered, fixed-width layout on a light gray background. At the top of the content area, a single-line horizontal navigation (context-header tabs) mirrors the mockup's location/nav row. Below that sits a slim user tools summary bar. The main focal area is a large white content card that follows the mockup's two-column band: the left column contains all primary browsing controls and results (search, date, filters summary, sort/view, list or calendar, pagination), while the right column is a vertical filter sidebar panel that visually echoes the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' card from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a centered, fixed-width layout on a light gray background. At the top of the content area, a single-line horizontal navigation (context-header tabs) mirrors the mockup's location/nav row. Below that sits a slim user tools summary bar. The main focal area is a large white content card that follows the mockup's two-column band: the left column contains all primary browsing controls and results (search, date, filters summary, sort/view, list or calendar, pagination), while the right column is a vertical filter sidebar panel that visually echoes the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' card from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header region (tabs + tools summary) occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical breathing room. The main white content card fills the remaining viewport height and can extend vertically as results grow. Within this card, about 65–70% of the width is dedicated to the left column (search, controls, and results) and 30–35% to the right filter sidebar. The entire card sits inside a centered max-width container with 40–64px margin from the viewport edges and 24–32px padding inside the card, following the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious feeling. The search bar, date range picker, sort/view bar, and active filters summary have generous padding and clear separation, comparable to the airy hero area in the mockup. The results list and calendar are denser zones but each item has comfortable vertical spacing. The filter sidebar is compact but readable, akin to the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' stack: concise rows, tight but legible spacing, and clear sectioning. Overall, whitespace is used to chunk controls into understandable bands rather than packing everything tightly.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup's vertical-first, then lateral pattern. The eye starts at the centered navigation tabs, moves straight down to the search bar in the top-left of the main card, continues down through the date range picker, active filters, and sort/view bar, then into the primary content (list or calendar). The right-hand filter sidebar, with a slightly more colorful header, provides a secondary rightward pull, similar to the pink 'TODAY'S EVENTS' panel, encouraging users to glance across and refine results. The flow remains predominantly top-to-bottom, with key interaction pivots (tabs, search, filters, sort/view) forming a clear vertical spine.

layout.overall_description:
The page sits on a soft light gray canvas with a centered max-width content column. Near the top of this column, a single row of navigation tabs spans the center, acting like the editorial nav bar in the mockup. Beneath it, a slim, full-width user tools bar gently separates global navigation from page-specific activity tools, with generous breathing space above the main content. 

The main content is contained in a large, elevated white card that visually anchors the page, much like the hero-plus-sidebar band in the original mockup. Inside this card, the layout splits into two vertical columns: the left, wider column for searching and browsing activities, and the right, slightly narrower column for filters. At the top-left of the card, a wide, prominent search bar invites input; directly around and beneath it, the date range, active filter chips, and sort/view controls form a stacked sequence of horizontal control bands. Below that, a large content region alternates between a list of activity cards and a calendar grid, filling most of the left column’s height. Pagination quietly sits at the bottom of this column. 

On the right, the filter sidebar reads as a tall, secondary card-within-a-card: it aligns vertically with the top of the search bar, features a colored header strip similar in punch to the mockup’s 'TODAY'S EVENTS' label, and then a tightly stacked set of filter groups extending down the page. This two-column, editorial-style composition creates a clear primary path down the left side, with an inviting lateral pull to the right-hand filters, preserving the magazine-like feel and spacious rhythm of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, page-level controller component. It runs logically before any visible layout is rendered and does not occupy any physical space in the UI. Conceptually, it is attached to the page container, orchestrating data and state for the components that appear within the centered layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; no spatial footprint in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context-header navigation tabs are positioned near the top center of the page’s content column, just below any global site header. They form a single horizontal row of tabs that spans roughly 60–70% of the max-width container, centered, with equal left and right margins. Vertical padding around the tabs is generous (about 16–24px above and below), creating a clear, airy band similar to the navigation row in the mockup. The active tab for 'Browse Activities' is visually emphasized, but the overall strip remains light and text-focused. There is a 24–32px gap of vertical whitespace between the bottom of this tab row and the top of the user-tools-summary-bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, fixed in the normal document flow; scrolls away with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar appears directly beneath the context-header, spanning the full width of the centered content container, but still on the light gray background (outside the main white card). It is a low-profile horizontal strip, about 40–56px tall, with subtle background tint and 12–16px vertical padding. Content inside is left-aligned, following the 12-column grid, while the bar itself helps visually transition from the airy, text-only tabs above to the more substantial main card below. A 24–32px vertical gap separates the bottom edge of this bar from the top edge of the main white content card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, full-width within the centered container; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the activity-search-bar anchors the top-left of the card and sits within the left column of the two-column grid. The card has 24–32px padding; the search bar starts after this inner top padding and aligns to the left grid edge. It spans approximately 80–90% of the left column’s width, making it visually dominant, but leaves a small margin on the right for balance. Vertically, the search bar has generous padding and height so that it feels like the hero control, similar in importance to the hero carousel in the mockup, though more compact. There is about 16–24px of vertical space below the search bar before the date-range-picker/active-filters zone begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column of the main content card; its width responds with the card but remains confined to the left 65–70% of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-range-picker is positioned immediately adjacent to or just below the activity-search-bar within the left column. On desktop, it appears in the same horizontal band as the search bar: the search bar occupies the left ~65–70% of the left column, and the date-range-picker is right-aligned in the remaining ~30–35%, forming a unified control strip across the top of the left column. Vertical alignment places its input fields centered relative to the search bar’s height. On narrower screens, it can drop to its own row directly below the search bar, with a tight vertical gap of 8–12px. In all cases, it remains visually close to the search bar, reinforcing their relationship as primary filtering controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column; on wide screens it shares a row with the search bar, on narrow screens it stacks below while maintaining the same left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar occupies the entire right column of the main white content card, echoing the position and role of the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar from the mockup. It begins at the same vertical level as the top of the search bar/date picker band and extends downward along the right side of the card. This column takes up roughly 30–35% of the card’s internal width. Inside the sidebar, a colored header strip at the very top (e.g., a playful pink band with the word 'Filters' or similar) stretches full width of this column, about 40–48px tall, mirroring the bold label treatment of the events panel. Below the header, filter groups are stacked vertically with compact but legible spacing, using 16–24px padding around each group and 8–12px between controls. The sidebar maintains the card’s inner padding (about 24px) from the right edge, creating a neat inset panel feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the main card, the sidebar is a right-aligned column. On desktop it may be sticky relative to the viewport, sticking near the top of the visible area once scrolled past the card’s top padding, while on mobile or narrow widths it can drop below the left column content, becoming a full-width block stacked after results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-view-bar sits in the left column beneath the search and date/filters band, forming a clear horizontal toolbar separating high-level filters from the results. It spans the full width of the left column (within its grid), with about 12–16px vertical padding and a subtle top border or divider line to distinguish it. The sort controls (e.g., 'Sort by') are left-aligned, while view toggles (e.g., list vs calendar) are right-aligned, creating a balanced horizontal bar similar in visual weight to the navigation row in the mockup but nested inside the card. There is a modest 12–16px gap below this bar before the results list or calendar begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, stretching its full width; scrolls with the main content and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-results-list occupies the main content area of the left column, directly below the sort-and-view-bar. It spans the full available width of the left column and extends vertically as a stacked list of activity cards. Each card is a white or very light tile with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between items, preserving the comfortable editorial feel. This content block fills most of the vertical space beneath the control bands and becomes the visual core of the page when list view is active. When displayed, the list pushes pagination controls to the very bottom of the left column. When list view is inactive (calendar view shown), this component is hidden and takes up no vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, occupying the same footprint as the calendar view; only one of list or calendar is visible at a time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-calendar-view shares the same horizontal footprint as the results list in the left column, immediately below the sort-and-view-bar. When active, it occupies the full width of the left column with a calendar grid (month or week view) filling a substantial vertical area, approximately 60–70% of the remaining viewport height, ensuring it feels like a major content slab similar to the hero area in the mockup. There is consistent padding around the calendar grid so it reads as a distinct, structured block. When calendar view is active, the results list is hidden so that only one large content mode is presented at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, mutually exclusive with the results list; one is visible while the other is collapsed (no height).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls component is positioned at the bottom of the left column, directly below whichever primary view is active (results list or calendar). There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the list/calendar and the top of the pagination area, providing breathing room. The pagination controls themselves are centered horizontally within the left column, with a compact height so they do not visually compete with filters or search. They act as a soft visual endpoint to the vertical flow of the left column while still feeling connected to the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the bottom of the left column’s content stack; scrolls normally and may fall below the viewport on long result sets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-action-handler is a non-visual behavior layer associated with interactive elements within the activity-results-list and activity-calendar-view (e.g., favorite icons on activity cards). It does not render its own UI container or occupy standalone space; instead, it attaches to individual items within the left column’s content region and responds to user actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; no independent spatial footprint. Behaves as a logical layer bound to elements inside the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-action-handler similarly operates as a non-visual logic component connected to watchlist or 'remind me' controls embedded in activity items. It has no distinct block in the layout and does not affect spacing. Its presence is only manifested through icons or buttons that are visually part of the activity cards or calendar entries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; no independent spatial footprint. Associated with controls inside list or calendar items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-summary appears in the left column between the date-range-picker band and the sort-and-view-bar. It forms a horizontal row (or wrapping rows) of filter chips or tags that represent currently applied filters. This component spans most of the left column width, aligned to the left, with a light background or subtle border to distinguish it from the white card beneath. Vertical padding is modest (8–12px), keeping it visually lighter than the search bar and sort/view bar. There is a small gap (8–12px) above and below it, so it reads as its own thin, editorial-style strip that users can quickly scan before moving down to the sort controls and results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, expanding vertically as filters accrue and chips wrap; remains between top filters and the sort-and-view toolbar.

name: Map View
title: Activities Map
description: Interactive map for finding nearby family activities, filtering by date, distance, and categories, and adding activities to a day plan, favorites, or watchlist.

core_features: Initialize a map centered using URL parameters (centerLat, centerLng, locationId) or the user's default location from getUserDefaultLocation, then load activity markers via getMapActivities.
Provide a location search field that uses searchLocations for autocomplete and recenters the map when a location is selected, updating URL parameters accordingly.
Provide a distance/radius control driven by getActivityFilterOptions.distance_options_miles that limits which activities appear on the map via getMapActivities.

Provide filter controls for activityCategories, contentGroups, timeOfDay, daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility, priceType, and priceMax, with values stored in URL parameters and applied via getMapActivities.

Display clickable map markers for activities loaded by getMapActivities, with popups showing key details and actions including 'View Details', 'Add to Day Plan', 'Add to Favorites', and 'Add to Watchlist'.
Support adding an activity from a marker popup to a day plan using addActivityToDayPlan, then offering navigation to day_plan.html with the selected date.
Support adding activities to the default favorites list via addActivityToFavorites and to the watchlist via addActivityToWatchlist, updating user tool summary counts.
Show a compact summary of user tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart) using getUserToolSummaries.
Allow toggling from the map view to the list-based browse.html while preserving filter state by mapping current URL parameters to the appropriate browse.html parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Initial map load and activity markers:
1) User opens map.html, possibly with URL parameters (centerLat, centerLng, locationId, radiusMiles, dateStart, dateEnd, activityCategories, contentGroups, timeOfDay, daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility, priceType, priceMax).
2) The page-state-manager component reads URL parameters.
3) If centerLat and centerLng are present, they are used as the map center; otherwise, getUserDefaultLocation is called and the returned latitude/longitude are set as the center and written into URL parameters.
4) getActivityFilterOptions is called with context='map' and content_group derived from the contentGroups URL parameter (if any) to populate filter controls (distance options, category options, time-of-day options, day-of-week options, tags, accessibility, sort options if needed).
5) getMapActivities is called with center_latitude, center_longitude, radius_miles (from radiusMiles), date_start (dateStart), date_end (dateEnd), activity_categories (activityCategories), content_groups (contentGroups), time_of_day_buckets (parsed from timeOfDay), days_of_week (parsed from daysOfWeek), tags, accessibility_features (from accessibility), price_type (priceType), and price_max (priceMax).
6) The map-view component renders markers from the getMapActivities.markers array and keeps track of total_count for display or notifications.

Workflow 2 – Searching for a neighborhood or place:
1) User types a query into the location-search-form input.
2) On each input change beyond a minimum threshold, the component calls searchLocations with query and a limit determined by UI configuration.
3) The component displays a suggestion list based on the returned Location items.
4) When the user selects a location, the map center is updated to that location’s latitude and longitude, and the locationId URL parameter is updated to the selected Location.id; centerLat and centerLng URL parameters are also updated.
5) The page-state-manager reacts to the URL change and calls getMapActivities again with the new center_latitude and center_longitude while preserving other filters (radius, dates, categories, etc.).
6) The map-view component re-renders markers based on the new getMapActivities response.

Workflow 3 – Adjusting distance and filters:
1) User changes the distance via the radius-filter-control (e.g., selecting a different option from distance_options_miles).
2) The control writes the selected numeric distance value into the radiusMiles URL parameter.
3) The page-state-manager detects the URL change and calls getMapActivities with the updated radius_miles and existing center and filters.
4) User optionally adjusts filters in the category-filter-bar, time-and-day-filter-panel, or advanced-filter-panel (e.g., toggling categories, selecting timeOfDay buckets, choosing daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility, priceType, or priceMax).
5) Each change immediately updates the corresponding URL parameter (activityCategories, contentGroups, timeOfDay, daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility, priceType, priceMax).
6) On each filter change, getMapActivities is called with the updated parameters and the map-view markers are refreshed.

Workflow 4 – Interacting with a marker and viewing details:
1) User clicks a marker on the map.
