2) The map-marker-layer component identifies the marker’s activity_id and opens the marker-popup component with the marker’s data (title, primary_category_label, start_datetime, time_of_day_bucket, price_summary, min_age, max_age, rating, has_free_parking, distance_from_center_miles).
3) User clicks 'View Details' within the popup.
4) Navigation occurs to activity_detail.html with URL parameters id set to the marker’s activity_id and returnUrl set to the current map page URL (including all stateful parameters) so that the user can return with the same map state.

Workflow 5 – Adding an activity to a day plan from the map:
1) User opens a marker-popup and clicks 'Add to Day Plan'.
2) The day-plan-add-form opens, prefilled with:
   - activityId from the marker,
   - a default date derived from either the dateStart URL parameter (if present) or the date portion of the marker’s start_datetime,
   - a time_of_day_bucket derived from the marker’s time_of_day_bucket (modifiable by the user if needed).
3) User confirms or edits the date and time-of-day bucket and submits the form.
4) The form calls addActivityToDayPlan with date, activityId, an optional sessionId (if not known, omitted), and time_of_day_bucket.
5) On success, the component receives the updated day_plan object, including summary.total_items and summary.total_estimated_cost, and may update a local display message.
6) The user is given a 'View Day Plan' action; if clicked, navigation occurs to day_plan.html with the date parameter set to the same date sent to addActivityToDayPlan.

Workflow 6 – Adding activities to favorites and watchlist:
1) User opens a marker-popup and clicks an action to add to favorites.
2) The favorites-action-button component calls addActivityToFavorites with the activityId.
3) On success, the component reads favorites_count from the response and notifies the user; the page-state-manager may update the favorites count in the user-tools-summary-bar without requiring an additional getUserToolSummaries call.
4) Similarly, user can click an action to add to watchlist; watchlist-action-button calls addActivityToWatchlist with activityId.
5) On success, watchlist_count from the response is used to update the user-tools-summary-bar.
6) The buttons may reflect success state (e.g., disabled or with updated label) without needing extra interfaces.

Workflow 7 – Viewing tool summaries and toggling to list view:
1) On initial load and periodically as needed, the user-tools-summary-bar calls getUserToolSummaries to obtain favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
2) These counts are displayed in the summary bar for quick reference.
3) User chooses to see activities in list view instead of the map by using the view-toggle-control.
4) The view-toggle-control reads the current map URL parameters and maps them to browse.html parameters: dateStart → dateStart, dateEnd → dateEnd, activityCategories → categories, contentGroups → contentGroups, timeOfDay → timeOfDay, daysOfWeek → dayOfWeek, tags → tags, accessibility → accessibility, priceMax → priceMax.
5) Navigation occurs to browse.html with those parameters encoded in the query string, preserving the user’s filter state across views.
interactions: On page initialization, parse URL parameters, optionally call getUserDefaultLocation, then call getActivityFilterOptions, getMapActivities, and getUserToolSummaries.

On location search input change, debounce and call searchLocations to display suggestions; on suggestion selection, update locationId, centerLat, and centerLng URL parameters to recenter the map and trigger getMapActivities.
On map drag end or zoom change, update centerLat and centerLng in URL, then call getMapActivities with the new center while keeping other filters unchanged.
On distance/radius selection change, update radiusMiles URL parameter and reload markers via getMapActivities.
On category, content group, time-of-day, days-of-week, tag, accessibility, priceType, or priceMax filter changes, write changes to URL parameters and call getMapActivities with updated values.
On marker click, open a marker popup bound to the marker’s data; on popup close, no state change is required beyond UI.
On 'View Details' click in marker popup, navigate to activity_detail.html with id set to the marker’s activity_id and returnUrl set to the encoded current map URL.
On 'Add to Day Plan' click, open day-plan-add-form, then on submit call addActivityToDayPlan and on success present navigation to day_plan.html for the selected date.
On 'Add to Favorites' click, call addActivityToFavorites, then reflect success and update favorites_count in user-tools-summary-bar using the returned value.
On 'Add to Watchlist' click, call addActivityToWatchlist, then reflect success and update watchlist_count in user-tools-summary-bar using the returned value.
On 'View Day Plan' in any contextual UI (e.g., after adding to day plan), navigate to day_plan.html with date equal to the date used in addActivityToDayPlan.
On 'List View' toggle click, navigate to browse.html with mapped URL parameters so that the list view mirrors the current map filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is encoded entirely in URL query parameters. On load, the page reads centerLat, centerLng, locationId, radiusMiles, dateStart, dateEnd, activityCategories, contentGroups, timeOfDay, daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility, priceType, and priceMax. If centerLat and centerLng are present, they define the map center. If they are absent, getUserDefaultLocation is called and its latitude and longitude are used as the center and written back into centerLat and centerLng URL parameters. locationId acts as an identifier for the conceptual center; when the user selects a location via searchLocations, locationId, centerLat, and centerLng are all updated. radiusMiles, if present, is passed to getMapActivities as radius_miles; if absent, the UI may initialize it using one of the distance_options_miles from getActivityFilterOptions and write it into the URL before the first getMapActivities call. dateStart and dateEnd, when present, are passed directly to getMapActivities; if only a single date is needed, the application may ensure dateStart and dateEnd are equal. activityCategories and contentGroups are treated as comma-separated lists; they are passed unchanged to getMapActivities.activity_categories and getMapActivities.content_groups. timeOfDay, daysOfWeek, tags, accessibility parameters are also stored as comma-separated strings; before calling getMapActivities, these values are split into lists and mapped to time_of_day_buckets, days_of_week, tags, and accessibility_features respectively. priceType and priceMax map directly to getMapActivities.price_type and getMapActivities.price_max. Any change to filters, center, or radius updates the corresponding URL parameters immediately (e.g., via history.replaceState or navigation), and these URL changes act as the single source of truth for re-triggering data fetches. When navigating to browse.html, the map state is translated into the list view’s expected parameters: activityCategories becomes categories, daysOfWeek becomes dayOfWeek, and other parameters keep their names. When navigating to activity_detail.html, the current full map URL (including all query parameters) is URL-encoded and passed as returnUrl so that the detail page can link back to map.html while restoring the previous state. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all restorable state is reflected in the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing and updates, and triggers calls to getUserDefaultLocation, getActivityFilterOptions, getMapActivities, and getUserToolSummaries. Acts as the central controller for map state.
Displays counts for favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, and cart using getUserToolSummaries and updates counts when relevant actions succeed elsewhere on the page.

Provides an input to search for neighborhoods or places using searchLocations, lets the user select a location, then updates locationId, centerLat, and centerLng URL parameters and triggers a reload of map activities.

Allows the user to choose a distance around the current map center using options from getActivityFilterOptions.distance_options_miles and writes the selected value into the radiusMiles URL parameter, which influences getMapActivities.radius_miles.
Allows the user to select or adjust the dateStart and dateEnd values for activity filtering and persists these values in URL parameters to be passed to getMapActivities.

Displays category options derived from getActivityFilterOptions.category_options and allows users to toggle activityCategories; also manages the contentGroups filter. Writes selected categories and content groups to URL parameters that are passed to getMapActivities.

Provides controls for time-of-day and day-of-week filters using getActivityFilterOptions.time_of_day_options and day_of_week_options. Selected values are stored in timeOfDay and daysOfWeek URL parameters and mapped to getMapActivities.time_of_day_buckets and days_of_week.

Manages filters for tags, accessibility features, priceType, and priceMax. Tag and accessibility options are derived from getActivityFilterOptions.tag_options and accessibility_options. Selected values are stored in tags, accessibility, priceType, and priceMax URL parameters and passed to getMapActivities.

Renders the interactive map centered at centerLat and centerLng, displays activity markers from getMapActivities.markers, and notifies the state manager when the map center or zoom changes. Handles marker click events to open the marker-popup.
Transforms marker data from getMapActivities into map markers, tracks which marker is selected, and provides marker details to marker-popup when a marker is clicked.

Displays details for the selected map marker (activity title, category label, start time, time-of-day bucket, price summary, age range, rating, parking, distance) and exposes actions for viewing details, adding to a day plan, adding to favorites, and adding to watchlist.

Collects the necessary information (date and time_of_day_bucket) to add the selected activity from a marker to a day plan. Calls addActivityToDayPlan and, on success, emits events to update tool summaries and offer navigation to day_plan.html.
Adds the associated activity to the user’s default favorites list when activated, using addActivityToFavorites, and updates the favorites count in user-tools-summary-bar based on the response.
Adds the associated activity to the user’s watchlist using addActivityToWatchlist and updates the watchlist count in user-tools-summary-bar using the returned watchlist_count.

Switches between map.html and browse.html. When toggled to list view, gathers the current URL parameters and navigates to browse.html, mapping activityCategories to categories and daysOfWeek to dayOfWeek while passing through dateStart, dateEnd, contentGroups, timeOfDay, tags, accessibility, and priceMax.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header/logo/nav, the Map View page introduces a single large, centered white content card on a light gray background, matching the mockup’s editorial, framed look. Inside this card, content flows top-to-bottom: first a horizontal tools/filters bar (acting like the mockup’s single-line nav), then the main split band: a large map hero on the left and a right-hand vertical filters sidebar. Interactive overlays like marker popups sit on top of the map area, similar in prominence to the original hero carousel overlay.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header/logo/nav, the Map View page introduces a single large, centered white content card on a light gray background, matching the mockup’s editorial, framed look. Inside this card, content flows top-to-bottom: first a horizontal tools/filters bar (acting like the mockup’s single-line nav), then the main split band: a large map hero on the left and a right-hand vertical filters sidebar. Interactive overlays like marker popups sit on top of the map area, similar in prominence to the original hero carousel overlay.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The centered content card follows a max-width, 12-column grid. The top tools/filters row spans nearly the full card width but is not very tall. Below it, the main band allocates roughly 65–70% of the width to the map hero on the left (about 8 columns) and 30–35% to a right sidebar for time/day and advanced filters (about 4 columns), echoing the hero + ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ split. The card is inset from the page edges with generous outer margins (40–64px) and internal padding around 24px, preserving the sense of a primary content frame on a soft gray background.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout feels moderately spacious. The tools bar groups several controls but uses clear spacing and alignment to avoid clutter. The map area is visually dense with markers but buffered by wide margins and rounded card edges. The right filters sidebar stacks controls with compact but breathable spacing between groups, similar to the event list density from the mockup: readable, not crowded. Internal paddings follow the 8px scale (mostly 16–24px) to maintain clarity and hierarchy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the site header, the eye lands on the top of the white card and immediately scans the horizontal tools bar from left (location search, filters) to right (view toggle). From there, the eye drops into the large left-hand map hero, which dominates attention like the original image carousel. The accent-colored headers of the right filter panels then pull the gaze rightward, inviting refinement of results. When a marker is selected, the popup card briefly becomes the focal point, sitting above the map and aligning visually with the editorial card styling.

layout.overall_description: The Map View page sits on a soft, light gray background with a single large white card centered in the viewport, echoing a modern magazine spread. The global site header with the colorful logo and location sits above, and the card begins a comfortable distance below it, framing the primary interaction. Inside the card, a slim, full-width tools bar runs across the top, containing the location search box, radius and date controls, category chips, and a view toggle aligned to the right—visually reminiscent of the mockup’s single-line navigation. Beneath this band, the card splits into two columns on a 12‑column grid: a wide map hero on the left occupying about two-thirds of the width, and a narrower filters sidebar on the right occupying the remaining third. The map fills its panel edge-to-edge with generous rounded corners and plenty of white space around it; markers and popups float above the map surface. The right sidebar is a vertical stack of filter panels with a bold, slightly playful accent-colored header bar at the top of each, visually akin to the ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ block in the mockup. The whole layout feels clean and editorial: the map is the visual hero, the right-hand column is the focused refinement area, and all elements are padded and spaced using an 8px-based system so nothing feels cramped. When a marker is clicked, a tidy card-like popup appears over the map, showing event details and clear actions to add to a day plan, favorites, or watchlist, preserving the card-based, family-friendly aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and effectively spans the entire page as an invisible layer behind the UI. It conceptually covers the full width of the centered content card and extends to the viewport edges, orchestrating state for the map view, filters, and actions without taking up any visible space. It does not introduce any visual gaps or frames; it simply underpins all other components on this page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic layer that occupies no visual space; it is effectively fixed to the page and persists as the user scrolls or interacts with the map.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the user tools summary bar sits directly beneath the top edge of the card and just above the main tools/filters row, spanning nearly the full internal width (about 90–95% of the card, centered). It is a low-profile horizontal band, approximately 56–64px tall, with 24px horizontal padding and 16px vertical padding. It reads like a compact editorial subheader: on the left, concise text summarizing the current location, date range, and number of matching activities; on the right, small text links or badges indicating counts for the day plan, favorites, and watchlist. There is comfortable white space above and below it (16–24px) to separate it from the card boundary and from the control row below, keeping the overall header area visually airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it scrolls away with the page content. On longer pages, it remains at the top of the content card and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location search form sits within the horizontal tools row near the top of the white content card, immediately below the user tools summary bar. It is left-aligned and occupies roughly 35–40% of the card’s internal width, making it the dominant element of this row. The search input appears as a long, pill-shaped or softly rounded field with an icon on the left, 16px vertical padding, and 24px horizontal padding inside. There is 16px of horizontal space between this form and the next control (radius), following the nav-like spacing seen in the mockup. The form’s top edge aligns with the top edges of the radius and date controls, creating a clean single-line visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the tools row; it resizes proportionally with the overall card width on responsive breakpoints but maintains its position at the left of this row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The radius filter control is placed in the same top tools row as the location search, immediately to the right of it. It occupies a compact column of roughly 10–12% of the card’s internal width, appearing as a dropdown or segmented control with a label and value. The control has 12–16px of internal padding and is separated from neighboring controls by 16px of horizontal whitespace. Its vertical height matches the location search field so the entire row reads as a single cohesive strip of controls. The label text sits above or to the left in a small, muted style, preserving the minimal, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element in the tools row; it maintains its relative position between the location search and date-range filter as the layout scales.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date range filter appears in the same horizontal tools row, immediately to the right of the radius control. It spans about 16–20% of the card’s internal width, styled as a single combined date picker or a paired from–to field with a unified outline. It aligns vertically with the other controls in this row and is separated from them by consistent 16px gaps. Internal padding follows the 8px scale (about 12–16px vertically, 16–24px horizontally) for easy tapping while maintaining a tidy, lightweight look. Together with the radius control and search field, it forms a left-aligned cluster before the category bar and view toggle.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element; it remains in the top filter bar, adjusting width proportionally with the card while preserving its order and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter bar occupies the remaining central section of the horizontal tools row, between the date range filter and the view toggle control. It stretches across roughly 20–25% of the card’s internal width. Categories are presented as a single-line row of pill-shaped chips or concise text links, echoing the nav style from the mockup: each chip has 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding, with 8px gaps between chips. The bar is visually lighter than the main controls, using subtle borders or background tints to avoid overwhelming the row. On wide screens, the chips remain on a single horizontal line; there is about 16px of space between the rightmost chip and the view toggle.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the tools row; if the content overflows horizontally (e.g., many categories), chips can wrap to a second line while the overall bar remains at the top of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the main split layout beneath the tools row, the time-and-day filter panel anchors the top of the right-hand sidebar column. This sidebar starts about 24px below the tools row and occupies roughly 30–35% of the card width, aligning to the right edge. The panel itself is a white card nested within the sidebar, inset with 24px of internal padding. At its top, a full-width horizontal header bar in the brand’s pink accent (or similar) echoes the mockup’s ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ header, with the panel title centered or left-aligned in white text. Below the header, time-of-day sliders, day-of-week checkboxes, or quick presets are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between groups. The panel’s bottom edge leaves 16–24px of white space before the next sidebar panel begins, maintaining the clear editorial separation between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar; it scrolls along with the page content. If the panel content grows tall, it may internally scroll, but it remains visually anchored at the top of the sidebar column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advanced filter panel is placed directly below the time-and-day filter panel in the right-hand sidebar column, sharing the same width (about 30–35% of the card). There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the two panels to preserve breathing room. It mirrors the same card structure: a rounded white card with 24px internal padding and a colored header bar at the top—slightly less saturated or smaller than the time-and-day header if a hierarchy is needed. Inside, advanced options (such as age range, price, indoor/outdoor, accessibility) are arranged in stacked groups, each separated by 12–16px. The overall height extends to roughly align with the visible height of the map on the left, so the sidebar feels visually balanced without looking heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar and scrolls with the page. Internal content may scroll if very long, but the panel itself remains in the stacked vertical flow beneath the time-and-day filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map view is the primary visual hero of the page, occupying the full height of the left section of the main card, directly beneath the tools row and aligned with the top of the right sidebar panels. It takes roughly 65–70% of the card’s width, from the left internal edge up to the beginning of the sidebar, with a 24px internal margin around it. The map is framed as a large, rounded rectangle card with no excessive chrome, mirroring the presence and scale of the hero carousel in the mockup. It stretches downward to fill most of the remaining vertical space in the viewport, so that the user perceives it as the dominant, interactive landscape, while still leaving a comfortable margin at the bottom of the card (24–32px) before the card ends.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content card but fills its allotted left column entirely. The map canvas itself can pan and zoom within this frame, and internal overlays (markers, popups) sit above it. On vertical scroll, the whole map panel moves with the page rather than sticking to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map marker layer sits directly on top of the map view, occupying the same footprint as the map’s visible area. Markers are distributed across the map, with each marker positioned relative to geographical coordinates. Visually, they float above the map tiles but remain within the boundaries of the map card—never overlapping the white card edges or sidebar. The density of markers may be high in some areas, but their small size and consistent spacing maintain the overall clean, editorial feel of the map hero. Marker clustering, if used, centers clusters in high-density areas without changing the map’s basic rectangular footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the map view, panning and zooming along with the map. The layer does not scroll independently of the map; it is locked inside the map card’s frame.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a marker is selected, the marker popup appears as a compact, white, rounded card overlay anchored to that marker on the map. It typically sits slightly above and to the side of the marker, so the card’s top edge remains comfortably inside the map’s frame, with at least 16–24px of margin from the map’s borders. The popup width occupies roughly 25–30% of the map width on desktop and is oriented vertically: image or title at top, key details beneath, followed by action buttons. It echoes the main card styling with a thin subtle shadow, 16–24px internal padding, and compact vertical spacing (8–12px) between text elements. The card does not overlap the right sidebar; it stays fully within the map panel’s visible area, re-positioning if needed to avoid clipping at the edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating overlay anchored to the selected marker within the map view. It moves relative to the marker when the map is panned or zoomed and disappears or repositions when another marker is selected. It does not affect page layout or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan add form lives inside the marker popup as a compact, inline form section near the lower half of the popup. Below the basic event information, a small header or label such as “Add to Day Plan” appears, followed by a minimal form: date/time selectors or a simple dropdown for choosing the target day. This form uses minimal vertical real estate (roughly 72–96px tall), with tightly grouped fields and 8–12px spacing between them, to keep the popup from feeling crowded. Internal paddings line up with the popup’s padding (16–24px from edges), and the form sits just above the favorites/watchlist action buttons, visually reading as part of the same decision area for planning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded inline within the marker popup; it does not change the popup’s anchor or behavior. If the form grows taller (more fields), the popup can extend downward, but remains constrained to the map area, potentially introducing an internal scroll if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the marker popup, the favorites action button is placed near the bottom-right area of the card as part of a small action cluster. It typically appears as a pill or icon+label button, about 40–48px tall, with 12–16px horizontal padding, aligned horizontally next to the watchlist button with 8–12px spacing between them. The buttons are right-aligned relative to the popup’s content column, sitting on a final line below the day-plan form so they feel like clear, decisive actions. The visual style is clean and minimal—perhaps a subtle outline or a small brand-colored icon—so it stands out without overpowering the popup content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the marker popup’s action row. It remains anchored inside the popup; when the popup moves (due to map panning), the button moves with it. It does not alter overall page layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist action button sits directly adjacent to the favorites button in the marker popup’s bottom-right action cluster, aligned on the same horizontal baseline. It mirrors the size and shape of the favorites button (40–48px tall, 12–16px horizontal padding) to keep the action row balanced. There is an 8–12px gap between the two buttons, with both aligned to the popup’s right padding edge. Together, these two buttons form a compact duo of secondary actions directly beneath or beside the day-plan add controls, matching the editorial card’s restrained but purposeful interaction pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the marker popup’s action row; it inherits the popup’s floating, anchored behavior and does not affect the surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view toggle control occupies the far-right end of the horizontal tools row at the top of the content card, aligned to the right internal edge. It takes up about 10–12% of the card’s internal width and is visually separated from the category filter bar by a 16px gap. The control appears as a compact segmented switch (e.g., “Map | List”) or icon buttons, using minimal text and clear selection state. Its vertical height matches that of the other controls, creating a clean, single-line band. The control’s placement at the far right mirrors the way secondary navigation often sits in editorial headers, making it easy to spot without stealing attention from the central map.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the right end of the tools row. It does not stick to the viewport; as the user scrolls, it moves away with the rest of the card header content.

name: Activity Details
title: Activity Details
description: Shows detailed information for a single family activity, including schedule, location, pricing, tags, and tools to save, plan, or register.

core_features: Load and display full details for a single activity (title, description, age range, schedule, pricing, tags, accessibility, rating) using getActivityDetails(activityId) based on the id URL parameter.
Display venue/location information and distance for the activity using the Location object returned by getActivityDetails.
List all available sessions/time slots for the activity (ActivitySession list from getActivityDetails), with the ability to select a session and, if applicable, a time-of-day bucket.
Preselect a relevant date/session for the user when a date URL parameter is provided, and use that date when adding the activity to a day plan.
Provide primary user actions: Add to Favorites, Add to Day Plan, Add to Watchlist (for ongoing programs), and a Register/Book Now button when registration is available.
Use addActivityToFavorites, addActivityToDayPlan, and addActivityToWatchlist to persist user actions and reflect updated counts via getUserToolSummaries.
Offer navigation back to the originating list or map view using the returnUrl parameter when present or a default browse.html fallback when not.
Provide quick visibility into user tools (favorites count, today’s day plan items, watchlist count, cart items count) using getUserToolSummaries.
user_workflows: View activity details and return to results: 1) User opens activity_detail.html with an id URL parameter from any supported page. 2) On page load, the page calls getActivityDetails(activityId=id) and getUserToolSummaries(). 3) The page displays activity details, sessions, location, tags, accessibility, and user tool counts. 4) If returnUrl is present, a Back to results control is shown. 5) When the user clicks Back to results, the page navigates to the decoded returnUrl; if returnUrl is absent, it navigates to browse.html?from=activity_detail&restore=1 or uses browser history as a fallback.

Add an activity to Favorites: 1) After details load, the Add to Favorites action is available. 2) User clicks Add to Favorites. 3) The page calls addActivityToFavorites(activityId). 4) On success, the page updates the favorites_count using the API response and optionally via getUserToolSummaries, and updates the control state (e.g., showing that the activity is now favorited).

Add an activity to a Day Plan: 1) User reviews sessions and optionally selects a session and/or time-of-day bucket in the session selector. 2) When the user clicks Add to Day Plan, the component determines the date to use: first from the date URL parameter if provided, otherwise from the selected session’s start date, and if neither is available, it prompts the user to choose a date. 3) The page calls addActivityToDayPlan(date, activityId, sessionId (if selected), time_of_day_bucket (if selected)). 4) On success, the page shows confirmation and a View Day Plan link that navigates to day_plan.html?date=<returned day_plan.date>. 5) The day plan summary (e.g., total items) can be used to inform the user, using the day_plan object returned by the interface.

Add an activity to a Watchlist: 1) For activities that qualify as ongoing programs (based on activity.content_group and/or schedule_recurrence from getActivityDetails), the Add to Watchlist action is shown. 2) User clicks Add to Watchlist. 3) The page calls addActivityToWatchlist(activityId). 4) On success, the watchlist_count is updated and the control reflects that the activity is now on the watchlist.

Register or book an activity session: 1) User reviews sessions in the session selector. 2) If multiple sessions exist, user selects a specific session; if only one session exists, it can be auto-selected. 3) The Register/Book Now button is enabled only when registration_available is true on the activity and a session (where applicable) is selected. 4) When the user clicks Register/Book Now, the page navigates to registration.html with URL parameters activityId=<id> and sessionId=<selectedSessionId>.

Check user tool status from the activity page: 1) On initial load, the page calls getUserToolSummaries(). 2) The counts for favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count are displayed in the user tools summary. 3) After any action that changes these counts (such as addActivityToFavorites or addActivityToWatchlist), the summary is updated using values returned from those interfaces and optionally by re-calling getUserToolSummaries for consistency.
interactions: On page load, read id, date, and returnUrl URL parameters. If id is missing, display an error state; otherwise call getActivityDetails(activityId=id) and getUserToolSummaries().

When getActivityDetails resolves, bind activity, location, sessions, content_group_label, category_label, price_summary, rating_summary, distance_miles, tags_display, and accessibility_display to the relevant components.

If a date URL parameter exists, attempt to auto-select a session whose start_datetime matches that date; if none match, store the date as the target planning date for Add to Day Plan while allowing free session selection.

Session selection interaction: clicking a session in the list marks it as selected (sets selectedSessionId and derived selectedOrSessionDate); changing selection updates the registration button and day plan defaults.
Time-of-day bucket selection interaction: when available, selecting a time-of-day bucket (morning/afternoon/evening/daytime/after_4pm) sets a selectedTimeOfDay for use in addActivityToDayPlan.

Clicking Add to Favorites triggers addActivityToFavorites(activityId). On success, favorites_count in the user tools summary is updated using the returned favorites_count, and the button state changes to indicate success. On failure, show an inline error message and keep the button enabled for retry.

Clicking Add to Day Plan triggers validation: ensure a date is known (URL date, selected session date, or user-provided date). If the date is missing, show a date selection input. Once the date (and optional session/time bucket) is determined, call addActivityToDayPlan(date, activityId, sessionId, time_of_day_bucket). On success, display an inline confirmation with a View Day Plan link that opens day_plan.html?date=<day_plan.date>.

Clicking Add to Watchlist triggers addActivityToWatchlist(activityId) when the activity is eligible for watchlisting. On success, update watchlist_count and adjust the control state (e.g., disable or switch to a Remove/Added label, depending on design decisions handled elsewhere). On failure, show an inline error.

Clicking Register/Book Now performs a client-side check that activity.booking_required and activity.registration_available are both true and that a session is selected when required. If checks pass, the browser navigates to registration.html?activityId=<activity.id>&sessionId=<selectedSessionId>. If checks fail, display a contextual message (e.g., asking the user to select a session).

Clicking Back to results: if returnUrl is present, decode and navigate to that URL; if not, navigate to browse.html?from=activity_detail&restore=1, optionally attempting history.back() first if supported by the environment.

User tools summary interactions: clicking the favorites count navigates to favorites.html; clicking the day plan count for today navigates to day_plan.html?date=<today>; clicking watchlist_count navigates to watchlist.html; clicking cart_items_count navigates to the relevant cart or memberships view if implemented elsewhere (links only, no extra APIs from this page).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and API responses, with transient UI state maintained in memory (not in localStorage). Required URL parameter: id. If id is missing or invalid, the page does not call getActivityDetails and should show an error state. Optional URL parameter: date (YYYY-MM-DD). When provided, this value is stored as initialTargetDate. After getActivityDetails returns, the page scans sessions for any ActivitySession whose start_datetime falls on initialTargetDate. If a matching session exists, it becomes the initial selectedSessionId and selectedOrSessionDate equals the session's date. If no matching session is found, selectedSessionId remains unset, but selectedOrSessionDate defaults to initialTargetDate for use with addActivityToDayPlan. If date is not provided and sessions exist, the page may leave selectedSessionId unset until the user explicitly chooses, or set default selection according to a deterministic rule (e.g., earliest upcoming session), always derived from API data rather than hardcoded assumptions. Optional URL parameter: returnUrl. If present, it is used by the Back to results component to navigate back; it is not altered. The page maintains transient state variables such as selectedSessionId, selectedTimeOfDayBucket, selectedOrSessionDate, lastAddToDayPlanResult, and flags for action in-progress states (e.g., isAddingToFavorites, isAddingToDayPlan). These are not persisted beyond the page lifecycle. Action buttons (Add to Day Plan, Register/Book Now, Add to Watchlist) enable or disable based on the combination of activity fields (like booking_required, registration_available, schedule_recurrence or content_group) and the current transient state (whether a session and date are selected). Navigation to registration.html and day_plan.html is always constructed using the current in-memory state and/or API response values, never hardcoded constants other than the target filenames and parameter names.

components.functionality: Initializes the Activity Details page by reading URL parameters, invoking getActivityDetails and getUserToolSummaries, and propagating the resulting data and derived state (initial date, session selection) to child components.
Displays the core summary of the activity: title, subtitle (if available), content group label, category label, price summary, rating summary, tags, and accessibility highlights.
Shows the full description and key attributes of the activity, including age range, schedule recurrence, and any additional descriptive fields provided by the Activity entity.
Displays venue information and distance, combining Location fields and activity/location flags for things like parking and indoor/outdoor context.

Lists available sessions/time slots for the activity, allows the user to select one session, and optionally choose a time-of-day bucket for planning. It also applies any initial date preselection logic and informs other components about the current selected session and derived date.

Provides main actions for the activity: Add to Favorites, Add to Day Plan, Add to Watchlist (when applicable), and Register/Book Now. It enables or disables actions based on activity fields and current session/date selection and triggers the appropriate interfaces or navigation.
Encapsulates the logic to add the current activity to the default favorites list by calling addActivityToFavorites and updating both local UI state and user tool summaries.

Manages the Add to Day Plan workflow by resolving the target date (from URL date, selected session date, or user input), then calling addActivityToDayPlan. It exposes the resulting day_plan object and triggers navigation to day_plan.html when the user chooses to view the plan.

Handles adding the current activity to the watchlist for ongoing programs by invoking addActivityToWatchlist when the activity is eligible. It updates local state and user tool summaries based on the response.
Displays summary counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, and cart items) using getUserToolSummaries. Provides navigation shortcuts to the corresponding pages.
Provides a Back to results link that uses returnUrl when available or falls back to browse.html with restore parameters or browser history when not.
Shows transient success or error messages for actions (favorites, day plan, watchlist) using the message fields returned by the respective interfaces.
Handles and displays errors such as missing id parameter or failures from getActivityDetails, providing options to retry loading or navigate back to browsing.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits within the same centered, fixed-width layout as the mockup: global header/logo/nav on top, followed by a light gray background and a single prominent white content card in the center. Within this card, content is arranged in a two-column band similar to the hero + 'TODAY'S EVENTS' pattern: a wider left column for the main activity details (title, key info, actions, description) and a narrower right column for structured, scannable details (session selector, location summary, user status/tools). Above the two-column band, inline notifications (when present) span the full width of the card. A small back-navigation link sits just above the card, aligned to the left.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits within the same centered, fixed-width layout as the mockup: global header/logo/nav on top, followed by a light gray background and a single prominent white content card in the center. Within this card, content is arranged in a two-column band similar to the hero + 'TODAY'S EVENTS' pattern: a wider left column for the main activity details (title, key info, actions, description) and a narrower right column for structured, scannable details (session selector, location summary, user status/tools). Above the two-column band, inline notifications (when present) span the full width of the card. A small back-navigation link sits just above the card, aligned to the left.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main white content card occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with generous light gray margins on the sides and ~40–64px margin above and below. Inside the card, the left column uses about 65–70% of the card width and the right column 30–35%, echoing the hero + sidebar split. Vertical spacing between stacked elements within each column follows the 24–32px rhythm; smaller controls and toolbars use 8–16px internal padding. The header + action area uses about the upper 30–40% of the card’s initial viewport, with the remainder given to scrollable description and supporting information.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate: main text areas (header summary, description) have comfortable line length and spacing with clear separation between subsections. The right column is more compact, presenting a vertical stack of sessions and location info reminiscent of the tight but readable event list. Interactive toolbars and control clusters are visually grouped but not crammed; each button or icon has enough padding to be easily tappable while keeping the overall layout tidy and editorial rather than app-like.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the activity header in the upper-left of the card (replacing the hero image as the primary focal zone), then moves down to the primary actions bar directly below it. From there, the user’s gaze continues down through the description. In parallel, the narrower right column provides a secondary visual pull, with the session selector at the top acting like the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' anchor: its header styling draws attention, and users then scan its list top-to-bottom, continuing down to location info and tools summary. Inline feedback messages briefly take precedence when shown, as a full-width bar above the columns. The back-to-results link offers an earlier, lighter entry point above the card for users who want to step back quickly.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar, centered magazine-style layout, the Activity Details page appears as a large white card floating on a soft gray background, directly beneath the site’s logo, location label, and navigation. A subtle 'Back to results' text link sits left-aligned just above the card. Inside the card, an optional, slim notification band can appear at the very top, spanning its full width. Below that, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a spacious activity header with title and key meta information establishes the page’s focal point, followed by a horizontal row of primary action buttons and then the full activity description flowing downward. On the right, a vertically stacked sidebar begins at the same top height as the header; it starts with a visually emphasized session selector list, followed by a compact location summary card and a low-key user tools summary near the bottom. The overall effect mirrors the mockup’s hero + events sidebar: a strong left-aligned main story with a supporting right-hand rail, ample white space, and a clear vertical reading path. Loaders and error states temporarily take over the central card area, keeping the page feeling structured and calm even in transitional states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader appears as a semi-opaque white overlay that covers the entire viewport, including the global header and the main content card. It slightly dims the underlying interface while keeping the centered, card-based structure faintly visible beneath. In the exact center of the viewport, a simple, modern loading indicator (spinner or progress ring) is displayed with generous whitespace around it, aligned both vertically and horizontally. When active, it visually dominates the page and prevents interaction with the activity detail layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, full-viewport overlay that sits above all other components (highest z-layer) and disappears once initial loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the activity header is anchored at the top-left of the two-column layout, starting just below any inline notification bar. It spans the full width of the left column, which takes up roughly 65–70% of the card’s width, and aligns its right edge with the left edge of the right-hand sidebar. The header occupies a generous vertical block, about one-quarter to one-third of the card’s initial height, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. The activity title appears prominently near the top-left of this block, with key meta details (date range, age range, tags, pricing summary) arranged below or to the right, but all kept within this left column. A comfortable 24–32px vertical gap separates the bottom of the header from the primary actions bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the left column; scrolls normally with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section sits directly below the primary actions bar in the left column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the activity header. It spans the full width of the left column and stretches downward, becoming the main scrolling text area of the page. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the actions bar above. Within this section, paragraphs and subsections follow the spacious editorial style: comfortable line length, 16–24px line spacing, and clear 16–24px gaps between headings, bullet lists, and sub-blocks. The section visually anchors the lower half of the left column, extending further down than the right sidebar on longer descriptions and creating a tall reading area that reinforces the main-story feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable as part of the main left column; it naturally becomes the main vertical scrolling area when content is long.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location summary is positioned in the right-hand sidebar, directly beneath the session selector component with a 24px vertical gap between them. It spans the full width of the sidebar, which occupies about 30–35% of the card’s width on desktop. This section is presented as a compact mini-card within the sidebar: white background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px padding. The location name is aligned to the top-left, followed by address and basic map/parking hints stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. Its top edge aligns roughly with the mid-height of the left column’s header + actions area, making it feel like a second-tier but easily discoverable detail section. At the bottom, a 24–32px gap separates it from the user-tools summary bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column; it scrolls out of view in sync with the sidebar contents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session selector anchors the top of the right-hand sidebar, aligning its top edge with the top of the activity header in the left column. It spans the full width of the sidebar (about 30–35% of the card) and is styled analogous to the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' panel in the mockup: a subtle, colored horizontal bar at the top (e.g., a pink header strip) with the section title, followed by a vertically stacked list of individual sessions. Each session item is a slim row with time, date, and a brief label, using 8–12px vertical padding and clear 8–16px spacing between items, forming a compact yet readable list. The component’s outer edges respect the card’s inner padding, leaving about 24px between its right edge and the card border. A 24–32px gap separates the bottom of this list from the location summary below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; on shorter pages it appears fully without scrolling, and on longer pages it scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary actions bar is placed in the left column immediately below the activity header, separated by 24px of vertical space. It runs horizontally across the entire width of the left column, forming a low-profile toolbar band that feels integrated with the header and aligned visually with the top of the right sidebar. The bar has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, hosting its child action controls (including favorites, day-plan, and watchlist) arranged in a row from left to right. Primary call-to-action buttons (such as 'Register' if present) appear toward the left, with secondary icon-style or text-only actions toward the right end. A subtle divider (thin top border or soft background tint) differentiates the bar from both the header above and the description below. The bar creates a clear decision zone before the user dives into long-form description.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline; scrolls with the left column content and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites action is rendered as a compact button or icon-label control within the primary actions bar, typically positioned in the right half of the bar. It sits inline with other user tools with 8–16px horizontal spacing between neighboring controls. The control’s touch target aligns with the bar’s vertical center, ensuring consistent height across all actions. Visually, it is secondary to any main 'Register' or 'Sign Up' button, using a lighter weight or outline style so that it reads as a supportive save action rather than the primary next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the primary-actions-bar; its position shifts responsively (e.g., wrapping to a second row on small screens) but remains grouped with the other actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan action is another inline control within the primary actions bar, placed adjacent to the favorites action with similar 8–16px horizontal spacing. It aligns vertically with the bar’s center and matches the visual treatment (size, padding, label style) of the favorites and watchlist actions, forming a cohesive cluster of planning tools. Its label or icon clearly indicates 'Add to Day Plan,' and its visual emphasis is on par with the favorites and watchlist actions, slightly less prominent than any main registration button to maintain the editorial hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the primary-actions-bar; it participates in any wrapping or stacking behavior of the action toolbar on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist action appears in the same cluster of tools inside the primary actions bar, typically positioned near the favorites and day-plan controls, using consistent spacing and size. It is aligned horizontally with these neighboring actions and vertically centered within the bar’s padding. The control’s visual weight matches the other secondary tools, possibly represented as an icon with a short label. Together, these three handlers form a tight, right-leaning subgroup within the actions bar, visually distinct from any primary CTA on the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the primary-actions-bar; it remains grouped with the other save/plan actions and follows their responsive behavior (wrapping or stacking) on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user tools summary bar is located near the lower part of the right-hand sidebar, beneath the location summary with a 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the sidebar, respecting the same inner margin to the card edge as the other sidebar sections. Visually, it appears as a slim, lightly tinted horizontal band or subtle mini-card summarizing the user’s current state (e.g., 'Saved to Favorites', 'In your Day Plan'), using small icons and brief text. The content is left-aligned with 12–16px horizontal padding, and the bar’s height is modest so it feels like a quick status glance rather than a major section. When the right sidebar is short, it may sit closer to the bottom of the visible card, reinforcing its role as a summary of actions taken.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar; scrolls along with sidebar content and does not stick to the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results navigation appears just above the main white content card, inside the same centered page container. It is left-aligned, occupying only a small portion of the width (roughly the leftmost 20–25%), and presented as a simple text link with a left-pointing arrow icon. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between this link and the top edge of the card, and a similar gap between the global nav and the link above. Its understated styling and placement create a gentle, easy-to-scan escape route before the user fully engages with the card content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains at the top of the page layout and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, action feedback messages appear as a full-width horizontal band at the very top inside the main white content card, directly beneath the card’s top padding and above the two-column content band. The message area spans almost the entire width of the card, with 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, and uses a soft colored background (green, yellow, or red depending on status) to stand out without feeling heavy. The text is left-aligned and concise, with an optional close icon aligned to the right. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this band from the activity header below, temporarily shifting the content down while the message is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it pushes the rest of the content down when displayed and scrolls away with the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In an error state, the main white content card retains its centered position and overall size, but the usual two-column layout is replaced by a single, centered error panel. This panel is horizontally centered within the card and limited to about 60–70% of the card’s width, with ample 32–48px whitespace above and below it. The error icon and headline are centered near the top of the panel, with a brief explanatory message and any retry/return actions stacked beneath, all vertically centered within the visible portion of the card. The back-to-results navigation link above the card remains visible and usable, reinforcing a clear escape path. No right sidebar or primary actions bar are shown; the error content becomes the sole focus within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the standard content area within the card when active; static and centered, scrolling only if the error message becomes taller than the viewport.

name: Memberships & Passes
title: Memberships & Passes
description: Page for browsing, filtering, comparing, and navigating to details of memberships and passes, using URL parameters to control filters, sorting, pagination, and comparison selections.

core_features: Load and display a paginated list of active memberships and passes using getMemberships, driven by URL parameters for membership type, maximum price, sort order, and page number.
Load filter and sort configuration (membership types, price ranges, billing periods, benefit tags, sort options) using getMembershipFilterOptions and bind them to interactive filter controls.
Allow users to refine the membership list by membership type, maximum price (via price ranges), billing period, and benefits, with membership type, price, and sort order persisted in URL parameters.
Allow users to select multiple memberships for comparison via per-item 'Compare' checkboxes and a 'Compare selected' control that triggers a comparison table on the same page.
Render a comparison table using getMembershipComparison, highlighting key rows such as membership type, price, and included free events per year so users can evaluate plans side-by-side.
Provide navigation from each membership listing and comparison table entry to a dedicated membership detail page (membership_detail.html) using the selected membershipId URL parameter.
Show counts for user tools (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) via getUserToolSummaries to support navigation to cart.html and provide context about saved items.
user_workflows: Browse and filter memberships: User opens memberships.html (with or without URL parameters); the page reads membershipType, priceMax, sortBy, and page from the URL; getMembershipFilterOptions, getMemberships, and getUserToolSummaries are called; the user adjusts filters (membership type, price range, billing period, benefits) and sort order; the page updates URL parameters (membershipType, priceMax, sortBy, page) and re-calls getMemberships to update the list.

Compare selected memberships: User selects one or more 'Compare' checkboxes on membership items; the page updates the compareIds URL parameter to reflect the selected membership ids; when the user activates the 'Compare selected' control, the page calls getMembershipComparison with the membership_ids from compareIds and renders a comparison table on the same page; user can clear specific selections or all comparison selections, which updates compareIds and hides or updates the table.

Navigate to membership details and cart: From either the membership list or comparison table, user clicks on a membership name or a 'View details' link; the page navigates to membership_detail.html?membershipId={id}; user can also navigate to cart.html via a header or page-level 'View Cart' control, informed by cart_items_count from getUserToolSummaries.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters (membershipType, priceMax, sortBy, compareIds, page), then call getMembershipFilterOptions, getMemberships, and getUserToolSummaries; if compareIds is present and non-empty, also call getMembershipComparison.

When the user changes the membership type filter control, update the membershipType URL parameter, reset page to the first page, re-call getMemberships with the new membership_type, and update the list.

When the user selects a price range, derive the maximum price value from the selected range configuration, update the priceMax URL parameter, reset page, re-call getMemberships with the new price_max, and update the list.
When the user changes the sort option, update the sortBy URL parameter, keep other filters unchanged, re-call getMemberships with the new sort_by, and update the list.

When the user changes billing period or benefit tag filters, apply client-side filtering to the memberships returned by getMemberships (using billing_period_options and benefits fields) without changing URL parameters, and update the displayed list.

When the user toggles a 'Compare' checkbox on a membership item, update the internal selection state and the compareIds URL parameter to include or remove that membership id; if compareIds becomes empty, hide the comparison table.

When the user activates the 'Compare selected' control, read the current compareIds URL parameter and call getMembershipComparison with membership_ids set to the selected ids; render or refresh the comparison table with the returned memberships and field_labels.
When the user changes the page via pagination controls, update the page URL parameter, re-call getMemberships with the new page, and scroll or focus to the top of the membership list.
When the user clicks a membership name or 'View details' link (from list or comparison table), navigate to membership_detail.html with membershipId set to the clicked membership's id in the URL.
When the user clicks the 'View Cart' control, navigate to cart.html; cart_items_count from getUserToolSummaries is used only for display, not passed as a parameter.

When the browser back or forward button is used, detect URL changes, re-read membershipType, priceMax, sortBy, compareIds, and page, and re-trigger getMemberships and getMembershipComparison so the UI matches the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Memberships & Passes page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for core state. On initialization and on any URL change, the page parses membershipType, priceMax, sortBy, compareIds, and page. These parameters are mapped directly into API calls and UI state: membershipType maps to the membership_type parameter of getMemberships; priceMax maps to price_max; sortBy maps to sort_by; page maps to page; compareIds is split into an ordered list of membership_ids passed to getMembershipComparison when comparison is requested. The is_active_only parameter for getMemberships is always passed as true so only active memberships are shown. The page_size parameter for getMemberships is set from a global application configuration and reused consistently. When a user adjusts membership type, price range, or sort order via UI controls, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters and resets page to the first page; these changes trigger a fresh getMemberships call. Comparison selections are encoded in compareIds; toggling a compare checkbox modifies compareIds and therefore the back/forward browser history restores comparison state correctly. Some filters (such as billing period and benefit tags) are applied client-side against data returned by getMemberships and are not persisted in URL parameters; they are reset on full page reloads or when the user navigates away. No state is stored in localStorage or similar persistent client storage; navigation and reload behavior are entirely derived from URL parameters and live API responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the Memberships & Passes page by reading URL parameters, fetching filter options, memberships, comparison data (if needed), and user tool summaries, and wiring these responses into the page-level state that other components consume.
Displays summary counts for favorites, today's day plan items, watchlist, and cart; provides a 'View Cart' navigation control that leads to cart.html.

Renders filter and sort controls for memberships, including membership type selector, price range selector (mapped to priceMax), billing period selector, benefits selector, and sort dropdown; updates URL parameters and triggers list refresh when relevant filters change.

Displays the current page of memberships returned by getMemberships, after applying any client-side filters (billing period and benefits). Delegates rendering and item-specific interactions to membership-list-item components.

Represents a single membership entry showing key details (name, type, price, billing info, included free events per year, headline benefits) and includes interactive controls: a 'View details' link and a 'Compare' checkbox that participates in comparison selection.

Shows current count of memberships selected for comparison based on compareIds URL parameter, provides a 'Compare selected' control to initiate or refresh comparison, and may provide a 'Clear comparison' control to remove all selections and hide the comparison table.

Displays a side-by-side comparison of selected memberships using data from getMembershipComparison, including labeled rows such as membership type, price, included free events per year (using field_labels), and other key attributes (e.g., benefits) derived from Membership fields.

Renders pagination controls using total_count, page, and page_size from getMemberships; allows users to move between pages and updates the page URL parameter, triggering a refreshed getMemberships call.

Manages and displays stateful messages for loading, empty results, or error conditions for the membership list and comparison table, based solely on the status of active API calls and returned counts.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a vertically stacked, centered layout that mirrors the mockup’s editorial flow: global site header (outside this spec) → page-level card for Memberships & Passes. Inside the card, content flows top-to-bottom: a slim user tools summary bar at the very top, followed by a full-width filter bar, then a dominant two-column band where the left column holds the memberships list and the right column acts as a compact, vertical tools/comparison sidebar. Below this primary card, an optional secondary full-width comparison table card appears when the user has selected memberships to compare.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a vertically stacked, centered layout that mirrors the mockup’s editorial flow: global site header (outside this spec) → page-level card for Memberships & Passes. Inside the card, content flows top-to-bottom: a slim user tools summary bar at the very top, followed by a full-width filter bar, then a dominant two-column band where the left column holds the memberships list and the right column acts as a compact, vertical tools/comparison sidebar. Below this primary card, an optional secondary full-width comparison table card appears when the user has selected memberships to compare.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main white content card is centered within a light gray page background, spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width with a max-width around a 12-column grid (approx. 1120–1200px). Inside the card, 24px padding is used on all sides. Within the primary content band, about 65–70% of the horizontal width is allocated to the left membership list column and 30–35% to the right tools/comparison sidebar column, replicating the hero + ‘Today’s Events’ split. The header bars (summary and filters) occupy relatively little vertical height but stretch the full width of the card. The optional comparison table below uses the full width of the content container as its own white card with similar padding and spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and airy, following the mockup: key bands (summary bar, filters, comparison header) are concise but have enough padding to feel comfortable, not cramped. The membership list has clearly separated list items with generous vertical spacing, and each membership card uses whitespace to group information rather than tightly packing details. The comparison sidebar is visually compact but readable, with a stacked list of selected items and controls. Overall, whitespace around bands and between sections (24–32px) prevents any feeling of clutter despite multiple tools and data-heavy content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow remains strongly vertical with a gentle left bias, adapted from the hero layout. After the global header, the eye lands on the page’s main centered card, first encountering the user tools summary bar, then drops down to the filter bar. From there, the main attention is pulled into the left membership list column, which acts as the ‘hero’ content. The right tools/comparison column, styled with a subtle accent header similar to the ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ panel, provides a secondary rightward pull, encouraging users to glance at comparison status and actions while browsing. If a comparison is active, the flow continues downward to the full-width comparison table below, encouraging a top-down scan from filters to list to detailed comparison.

layout.overall_description: The Memberships & Passes page sits on a soft light gray background, with a single prominent white card centered in the viewport, framed by generous side margins. Inside this card, a slim, text-based user tools summary bar stretches across the top, followed by a tidy horizontal filter bar that spans the full width of the card. Below, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a tall, primary column shows a vertical stack of clean membership cards, each with ample padding and whitespace, forming the main browsing area. On the right, a narrower sidebar column echoes the mockup’s ‘today’s events’ panel, with a colorful accent header and a stacked list of selected memberships and comparison controls. The right column feels like a utility rail that stays in view as the user scrolls through memberships on the left. At the bottom of the main card, centered pagination controls mark the end of the list. When users select memberships to compare, a second white card appears just below the main card, still centered, containing a full-width comparison table laid out like an editorial chart. The overall effect is a calm, magazine-like page: a strong central content card, clear hierarchy, and a subtle right-hand tool column that supports but never overwhelms the main browsing experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component establishes the overall page canvas and main content card. The entire page background is a light gray tone, and within it, a single centered column defines the max-width container (roughly 1120–1200px wide or 80–90% of the viewport, whichever is smaller). The memberships page sits inside a large white card that begins approximately 64px below the global site header and is inset by 40–64px from the left and right viewport edges on desktop. This card has a subtle radius and shadow consistent with the design DNA, with 24px of internal padding on all sides. All subsequent components (summary bar, filters, list, sidebar, pagination, and any comparison table) are arranged within this container in a top-to-bottom flow, with vertical gaps of 16–32px between them according to their hierarchy. When the comparison table is shown, it appears as a separate white card directly below the main card, aligned on the same vertical center line and spaced down by 32–40px to preserve the framed editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a centered, fixed-width content region on a full-width background. The main card itself scrolls with the page; no fixed positioning, but it anchors all child components to a shared 12-column internal grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This bar is the first element inside the main white card, positioned at the top and spanning the full internal width of the container (100% of the card width minus 24px side padding). It sits approximately 8–16px below the top edge of the card’s padding zone, visually attached to the card top. The bar is a slim horizontal band, about 48–64px tall, with light background treatment (either the card’s base white or a very pale tint) and subtle bottom divider line to differentiate it from the filter bar below. Text (e.g., total memberships, active filters, quick links like ‘Clear all’) is left-aligned; supplementary controls (like view toggles, save search) can be right-aligned, forming a two-ends layout. There is 16–24px horizontal padding within the bar itself, aligning with the card’s grid. A vertical gap of around 16–24px separates this bar from the membership-filter-bar below, maintaining a clean stacked hierarchy similar to logo → location → nav in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; scrolls with the page as part of the main content. It visually anchors the tools area but does not stick to the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the user-tools-summary-bar, the membership-filter-bar spans the full width of the main card’s content area, functioning like the single-line horizontal navigation from the mockup. It is a horizontal strip about 64–80px tall, with controls such as dropdowns, chips, and search input arranged from left to right. Filters align to the left, while any high-level sort control sits on the right, mirroring the simplicity of a text-based nav. Horizontal padding (16–24px) matches the summary bar above, and vertical padding within the bar (12–16px) keeps it feeling open. A small vertical gap of 24px separates this filter bar from the main two-column content band below, mirroring the transition from nav to hero in the mockup and signaling a move from configuration to content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band inside the main card. It remains in document flow and scrolls off screen as the user moves down, allowing the list and comparison sidebar to take over visual focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership-list occupies the left portion of the primary two-column band, directly beneath the filter bar. The total available width beneath the filters is split into approximately 8 columns for the list (about 65–70% of the internal card width) and 4 columns for the right-hand tools/comparison sidebar (30–35%). The list column starts flush against the card’s left inner padding and stretches downwards as a tall vertical stack of membership-list-item cards. Each item is separated by 16–24px vertical spacing to maintain readability and rhythm. The list area has no heavy bounding box of its own; instead, it relies on the card’s background and the individual list item cards’ outlines to provide structure, similar to how multiple photos and content are arranged in the hero area of the mockup. The right edge of the list column aligns to the vertical grid line that separates it from the sidebar, leaving a clean gutter (16–24px) between the two columns. At the bottom of the list, just above pagination, there is a 24–32px gap to visually mark the end of the scrollable content before navigation controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This column grows vertically with content and scrolls normally. It forms the main browsing surface, so it is visually dominant in the left 2/3 of the card. On smaller screens, this column will stack above the sidebar, becoming full-width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each membership-list-item is a horizontally oriented card that fills the width of the membership-list column (minus any internal column padding). The card has 16–24px padding on all sides, a subtle border or shadow, and rounded corners consistent with the site’s card style. Vertically, items are moderately tall, allowing space for a title line at the top left, a short description or tagline, key metadata (age range, location, price), and primary actions (e.g., ‘View Details’, ‘Add to Compare’). Visual structure within each card follows a left-to-right arrangement: on the left, main information; on the right (aligned to the card’s right edge), a compact vertical stack of actions or pricing summary. There is 8–12px vertical spacing between text lines and 16px gaps between information blocks, keeping the content readable and echoing the editorial feel of the hero headlines. The cards do not touch; they are separated vertically by whitespace, making the list feel like a series of featured snippets rather than a dense table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully contained inside the membership-list column. Scroll behavior is standard. When a membership is selected for comparison, the card may reflect its state (e.g., highlighted chip or icon) without altering its size or breaking the vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-selector-bar lives in the right-hand sidebar column, positioned to mirror the mockup’s ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ panel. It starts at the same vertical level as the top of the membership-list column (directly under the filter bar) and occupies the full width of the right column (roughly 30–35% of the card). At its top, a horizontal header strip 40–56px tall uses the brand’s pink accent color and contains a bold, all-caps or strong label such as ‘COMPARE MEMBERSHIPS’, centered or left-aligned, creating a strong visual anchor similar to the vertical pink heading in the mockup. Below the header, the panel body has 16–24px internal padding and a white or very light background, with a subtle border separating it from the card background.
