layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feeling. The left column will feel content-dense due to the form, but each section (summary, session selector, form) is clearly separated with 24–32px vertical gaps and generous padding so it does not feel cramped. The right column is more compact but clean: stacked blocks with tight but readable spacing, similar in density to the ‘Today’s Events’ list. Use the 8px spacing scale: 24px padding inside panels, 16–24px gaps between major blocks, and 8–12px between related inline elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey is vertical-first, then lateral: the eye hits the local page title at the top of the card, moves down to the user tools bar, then into the left column where the activity summary and session selector introduce context before the form. In parallel, a strong, colored header on the right-column confirmation panel acts like the pink ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ masthead, creating a secondary rightward pull. As users scroll, their attention flows down through the form while the right sidebar (confirmation/summary) remains visually anchored, encouraging periodic rightward glances to review status and final details.

layout.overall_description: The Registration page sits within the same clean, editorial shell as the rest of the site: a light gray background frames a single, centered white registration card that follows a 12-column grid. At the top of this card, a clear page title and short descriptor establish context, followed immediately by a slim horizontal tools bar that runs edge to edge. Below, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a generous main area stacks the activity summary, session selector, and the registration form in a calm vertical flow; on the right, a narrower sidebar echoes the mockup’s ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ panel, using a colored header bar and a tidy vertical stack to present registration state and confirmation details. Status messages appear close to the top of the card so they’re hard to miss, while the confirmation panel in the right column remains visually strong and, on desktop, stays in view as users scroll the form. The result feels like a modern magazine registration feature: structured, airy, and easy to scan, with the heavy interaction work on the left and a reassuring, ever-present summary on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration page controller defines the centered, fixed-width main content card that sits on the light gray background. It is positioned below the global site header, centered horizontally, with approximately 40–64px margin from the top of the viewport and similar breathing room below. The card spans roughly 80–90% of the max-width container (e.g., about 960–1120px within a 1200px container), with 24px padding on all sides. Inside the card, the page controller reserves the top band (about 20–25% of the card’s above-the-fold height) for the local page title, subtext, breadcrumbs, and the user-tools-summary-bar. Beneath this band, it establishes the 2-column 12‑column grid: an 8-column left content area and a 4-column right sidebar, separated by a 24px gutter. All other components are placed within this card and constrained to this grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal document flow; defines the main white card and its internal 12-column grid within a centered, fixed-width layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar appears immediately below the local page title area inside the registration card, running horizontally across the full inner width of the card. It sits about 16–24px under the title/description text supplied by the page controller and about 24px above the start of the left/right content columns. The bar has a low-height, toolbar-like appearance (roughly 40–56px tall), with 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding. Tools such as ‘Back to activity’, ‘Print’, or ‘Help’ are aligned left, while any secondary actions (e.g., ‘Save progress’) align right, maintaining a clean single-row layout on desktop. Visually, this bar acts like the mockup’s navigation line: light weight, simple text links or icons, and enough surrounding whitespace that it doesn’t compete with the main content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width element inside the card; it anchors the top of the interaction area and pushes the column layout down but does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-summary-panel occupies the top of the left 8-column area created by the page controller. It starts approximately 24px below the user-tools-summary-bar, spanning the full width of the left column with 24px internal padding. Vertically, it is relatively shallow — roughly 1/5 to 1/4 of the left column’s above-the-fold height — but visually prominent, acting as the ‘hero text’ equivalent. It presents the activity title as a strong heading at the top left of this panel, with key details (date, location, age range, price) arranged in a compact, multi-line layout beneath, using 8–12px vertical spacing between lines. If a thumbnail image is used, it should sit on the left or top with text wrapping to the right/below, but the overall feel remains text-first and clean. A soft divider or subtle bottom border separates this summary from the session-selector that follows, with a 16–24px gap to keep sections distinct while maintaining a coherent vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the left column; it scrolls away with the rest of the main content as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session-selector is placed directly beneath the activity-summary-panel within the left 8-column area, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It also spans the full width of the left column with 24px internal padding, visually appearing as a subsection of the main content rather than a completely separate card. Sessions are presented in a structured list or grid within this block: for example, 1–2 columns of session rows with radio buttons or selection controls, each row having 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal spacing between time, date, and capacity indicators. The component’s total height is flexible: on desktop it might occupy about 1/4 of the left column’s initial visible height but can expand if many sessions are available, pushing the registration form further down. A clear section heading at the top left of the block (‘Choose a session’) aligns vertically with the left edge of the activity summary heading, reinforcing hierarchy and visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left column; grows vertically as needed, pushing the registration form downward while keeping alignment to the 8-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-form sits immediately below the session-selector, still confined to the left 8-column area. It begins about 24–32px under the last element of the session selector, marking it clearly as the main interaction section. This form is the tallest component in the left column, often extending well below the fold. It uses the same 24px internal padding as other panels, with form fields arranged in a single or two-column layout depending on viewport width, but always respecting the left column’s boundaries. Related fields (e.g., Participant Info, Guardian Info, Payment Details) are grouped into logical subsections, each with its own mini-heading and 16–24px vertical separation, maintaining the mockup’s spacious editorial feel. At the bottom of the form, primary actions (e.g., ‘Complete Registration’) align left, with any secondary text (e.g., ‘You won’t be charged yet’) just below, separated by 8–12px of space. The right confirmation panel is visible alongside this form on desktop, creating a constant left-right relationship where input happens on the left and outcomes are visible on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content within the left column; as users scroll down through the form, the right sidebar remains relatively fixed in view (on desktop), preserving context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-state-messages component appears near the top of the registration card to keep feedback highly visible. By default, it is placed directly below the user-tools-summary-bar and above both the left and right columns, spanning the full inner width of the card with 16–24px padding and a background color that contrasts gently with the white card (e.g., pale yellow for warnings, pale green for success, pale red for errors). When present, it compresses the vertical space between the toolbar and the start of the activity-summary-panel/confirmation sidebar to about 16px above and below, forming a clear, horizontal band of feedback. The text is left-aligned, with any icons (success/error) at the far left and content wrapping within the same width as the card. If multiple messages are stacked, they remain within this band, separated by 8px vertical spacing, keeping the layout clean and easy to scan before the user continues downward into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and full-width; appears and disappears as needed, pushing the main 2-column content band downward but not overlaying it or sticking on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation-panel occupies the entire right 4-column sidebar area, starting at the same vertical position as the top of the activity-summary-panel in the left column. It sits about 24px below the user-tools-summary-bar (and below the registration-state-messages band if that is visible), with 24px internal padding. The top of this panel features a bold, colored header strip running full width — for example, a pink or brand-accent bar labeled ‘Registration Summary’ or ‘Your Registration’, directly echoing the vertical emphasis of the ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ sidebar masthead in the mockup. Below this header, key summary information is stacked in a vertical list: selected session, participant count, pricing breakdown, and status (e.g., ‘In progress’, ‘Completed’), each item separated by 8–12px. On success, this panel transitions to display confirmation details (confirmation number, next steps, share/print options) in the same stacked format. The content density is moderately compact but not cramped, leaning on the editorial feeling of short, scannable lines rather than dense tables. On desktop, this panel is visually strong and remains in the right margin alongside the form, acting as an ever-present reference and confirmation area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a right sidebar; on larger screens it can use sticky positioning within the card, staying pinned near the top of the viewport while the left column scrolls, but constrained so it does not overlap the card’s header or footer. On smaller screens it stacks below the form, losing its sticky behavior to preserve vertical reading flow.

name: Favorites
title: Favorites
description: View and manage saved favorite activities, filter and sort them, remove items, and add favorites directly into a day plan.

core_features: Retrieve and display the user’s favorited activities using getFavoritesList, honoring filters and sorting defined by URL parameters.
Provide filter controls for date range, content groups, activity categories, and tags; update URL parameters and refresh the favorites list when filters change.
Allow sorting of favorites (e.g., by upcoming date, when added, or price) via a sort control bound to the sortBy URL parameter.
Enable quick navigation from each favorite to its activity detail page (activity_detail.html) with a returnUrl back to the current favorites state.
Allow users to remove items from favorites by calling removeFavorite and updating the list and favorite counts.
Allow users to add activities from the favorites list directly to a day plan by calling addActivityToDayPlan with a selected date and optional time-of-day bucket.
Provide an action to open the day plan (day_plan.html) for the date used when adding favorites to the plan, passing that date as a URL parameter.
Display summary counts for favorites and other user tools (day plan today, watchlist, cart) using getUserToolSummaries, keeping these counts updated after relevant actions.
user_workflows:
View favorites list:
1) User navigates to favorites.html with or without query parameters.
2) On page load, the page reads URL parameters: dateStart, dateEnd, contentGroups, activityCategories, tags, sortBy.
3) The page calls getFavoritesList with mapped parameters (date_start, date_end, content_groups, activity_categories, tags, sort_by).
4) The favorites list is rendered using the returned items and total_count.
5) The results header shows the total number of favorites based on total_count.

Filter favorites by date, category, and tags:
1) User adjusts filter controls (date range, content groups, activity categories, tags) in the filters bar.
2) On filter form submit or control change, the component constructs a new query string with updated dateStart, dateEnd, contentGroups, activityCategories, and tags values (removing any that are cleared).
3) The page navigates to favorites.html with the new query string (full reload or history API), preserving sortBy if it is not changed.
4) On load, getFavoritesList is called with the new parameter values.
5) The favorites list updates to show activities that match the filters.

Sort favorites:
1) User changes the sort option in the sort control (e.g., to date_soonest, added_recently, price_low_to_high).
2) The component updates the sortBy parameter in the URL query, leaving existing filter parameters intact.
3) The page reloads or re-renders, then calls getFavoritesList with sort_by set to the new sortBy value.
4) The list is re-rendered in the new sort order.

Open activity details and return to favorites:
1) User clicks on a favorite item’s title or card.
2) The click handler reads the activity_summary.activity_id and the current full favorites page URL (including all query parameters).
3) The browser navigates to activity_detail.html with URL parameters id set to the activity_id and returnUrl set to the encoded currentFavoritesUrl.
4) On the activity detail page, when the user chooses to return, that page navigates back to the returnUrl, restoring the prior filters and sort state for favorites.

Remove an activity from favorites:
1) User clicks the remove action (e.g., a button) on a favorite item.
2) The click handler calls removeFavorite with favoriteItemId equal to the item’s favorite_item_id.
3) On success, the response favorites_count is used to update any favorites count display; optionally, the component may also remove the item from the currently displayed list without a full refresh.
4) Alternatively (or additionally), the page may re-call getFavoritesList with the existing URL parameters to ensure the list and total_count are fully consistent.
5) If the last item is removed, the list updates to show no results while keeping filters intact.

Add a favorite to a day plan and open the plan:
1) User clicks 'Add to Day Plan' (or equivalent) on a favorite item.
2) A form (inline or modal) opens with fields for selecting a date and a time_of_day_bucket; if a specific sessionId is already known from context, it may be pre-filled, otherwise it can be left empty.
3) User selects the desired date (the plan date) and optional time_of_day_bucket, then submits the form.
4) The form handler calls addActivityToDayPlan with parameters: date set to the selected date, activityId set to activity_summary.activity_id, sessionId set as available (or omitted/empty if not), and time_of_day_bucket set to the selected value (or omitted if not chosen).
5) On success, the UI can display feedback using the returned message and day_plan summary (e.g., total_items, total_estimated_cost) and update any day-plan-related summary counts if needed via getUserToolSummaries.
6) The user can click an 'Open Day Plan' action, which navigates to day_plan.html with date set to the date used in the addActivityToDayPlan call.

View tool summaries (favorites, day plan today, watchlist, cart):
1) On page load, the page calls getUserToolSummaries with no parameters.
2) The favorites-tools summary component displays favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
3) After actions that affect these numbers (e.g., removing a favorite or adding to a day plan for today), the page can either:
   - Update only the directly affected count using values from removeFavorite or addActivityToDayPlan responses (e.g., favorites_count from removeFavorite), or
   - Re-call getUserToolSummaries to refresh all counts.
4) If the user clicks on any of the summary badges (e.g., favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart), the page can navigate to the corresponding tool page using standard navigation (with any required URL parameters defined on those pages).
interactions:
Filter change interaction:
- User edits a filter control (date range, content groups, activity categories, tags).
- Event handler: onFilterChange or onFilterSubmit.
- Behavior: updates the appropriate URL parameters (dateStart, dateEnd, contentGroups, activityCategories, tags) and reloads/re-renders the page.
- Side effect: triggers getFavoritesList with corresponding parameters (date_start, date_end, content_groups, activity_categories, tags).

Sort control interaction:
- User selects a different sort option.
- Event handler: onSortChange.
- Behavior: updates sortBy in the URL query while preserving other parameters, then reloads data.
- Side effect: triggers getFavoritesList with sort_by mapped from sortBy.

Favorite item navigation interaction:
- User clicks on a favorite activity’s title or card.
- Event handler: onFavoriteItemClick.
- Behavior: constructs a URL for activity_detail.html with id set to activity_summary.activity_id and returnUrl set to the current full favorites URL; navigates the browser to that URL.

Remove favorite interaction:
- User clicks the remove control on a favorite item.
- Event handler: onRemoveFavoriteClick.
- Behavior: calls removeFavorite with favoriteItemId from favorite_item_id; on success, removes the item from the local list or re-fetches via getFavoritesList using current URL parameters.
- Side effect: updates displayed favorites_count using the removeFavorite response, and optionally refreshes getUserToolSummaries.

Open add-to-day-plan form interaction:
- User clicks the 'Add to Day Plan' action on a favorite item.
- Event handler: onAddToDayPlanClick.
- Behavior: opens a form (e.g., modal) with fields for date and time_of_day_bucket (and optionally sessionId if available).
- No API calls yet until submit.

Submit add-to-day-plan interaction:
- User fills out the plan date and optional time_of_day_bucket (and sessionId if applicable) and submits the form.
- Event handler: onAddToDayPlanSubmit.
- Behavior: calls addActivityToDayPlan with parameters mapped from form input and the activity’s ID; on success, shows success feedback using returned message and day_plan summary.
- Side effect: may trigger getUserToolSummaries to refresh day_plan_today_items_count, especially if the selected date is today.

Open day plan interaction:
- After successfully adding an item to a day plan (or from a dedicated button), user clicks 'Open Day Plan'.
- Event handler: onOpenDayPlanClick.
- Behavior: navigates to day_plan.html with date set to the last used selectedDateForPlan (from the add-to-day-plan form).

User tool summary refresh interaction:
- On initial page load or after relevant changes, an event triggers fetching of tool summaries.
- Event handler: onInit (page load) and optionally onToolSummaryRefresh.
- Behavior: calls getUserToolSummaries and binds the returned counts to displays in the tools summary component.
- Side effect: keeps global tool counts in sync (favorites, day plan today, watchlist, cart).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Favorites page relies entirely on URL parameters and API responses for state. On every load of favorites.html, the page reads current query parameters and calls getFavoritesList accordingly.

URL to API parameter mapping:
- dateStart (URL) -> date_start (getFavoritesList)
- dateEnd (URL) -> date_end (getFavoritesList)
- contentGroups (URL) -> content_groups (getFavoritesList)
- activityCategories (URL) -> activity_categories (getFavoritesList)
- tags (URL) -> tags (getFavoritesList)
- sortBy (URL) -> sort_by (getFavoritesList)

When a parameter is absent or empty, that filter/sort is excluded from the getFavoritesList call.

State transitions:
- Changing filters or sort updates only the corresponding URL parameters; existing parameters are preserved unless explicitly cleared (e.g., clearing a date range removes dateStart/dateEnd from the URL).
- After any URL change, the page is reloaded or re-rendered, and getFavoritesList is called again using the new parameter values.
- The current full URL (including all parameters) is used as returnUrl when navigating to activity_detail.html, ensuring that returning restores the same filtered/sorted view.
- The selected date used when adding an activity to a day plan is kept in component state for the duration of the interaction and passed as the date parameter when navigating to day_plan.html; it does not introduce new URL parameters on favorites.html itself.
- Tool counts (favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, cart_items_count) are not stored in URL parameters; they are retrieved via getUserToolSummaries on load and after relevant actions, and are only reflected in in-memory UI state until the user reloads the page.

components.functionality: Provides controls for filtering favorites by date range, content group, activity category, and tags; synchronizes these filters with the URL query parameters and triggers re-fetching of the favorites list.
event_handlers: onInit: Read existing URL parameters and pre-populate filter controls to match current state.
onFilterChange: When any individual control changes, update corresponding in-component state (optional) without calling APIs yet.

onFilterSubmit: Construct a new query string with updated dateStart, dateEnd, contentGroups, activityCategories, and tags; navigate to favorites.html with this query (preserving sortBy if present), causing getFavoritesList to be called with mapped parameters.
onFilterReset: Clear all filter controls, remove related parameters from the URL, and reload the page to get an unfiltered favorites list.

components.functionality: Displays the count of favorites returned by getFavoritesList and provides a sort control bound to the sortBy URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Read sortBy from the URL and set the sort control’s selected value.

onSortChange: When user selects a different sort option, update sortBy in the URL (retaining other filter parameters) and trigger reload, which in turn calls getFavoritesList with sort_by mapped from sortBy.

components.functionality: Shows summary counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, and cart) using getUserToolSummaries; updates counts after relevant actions from this page.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getUserToolSummaries and bind returned counts to the summary display.
onFavoriteRemoved: After calling removeFavorite elsewhere, update favorites_count directly from removeFavorite response or re-call getUserToolSummaries for full refresh.
onDayPlanUpdated: After a successful addActivityToDayPlan call that affects today, optionally re-call getUserToolSummaries to refresh day_plan_today_items_count.
onFavoritesSummaryClick: Navigate to favorites.html (may be a no-op if already on this page).
onDayPlanSummaryClick: Navigate to day_plan.html with a suitable default date (e.g., today) if defined globally for that page.
onWatchlistSummaryClick: Navigate to the watchlist page (URL defined elsewhere).
onCartSummaryClick: Navigate to the cart page (URL defined elsewhere).

components.functionality: Renders the list of favorite items returned from getFavoritesList and instantiates a favorite-item-row component for each item.
event_handlers: onInit: After getFavoritesList returns, iterate through items to render child favorite-item-row components, passing each item’s favorite_item_id and activity_summary.
onListRefreshRequested: Re-call getFavoritesList with current URL parameters when triggered (e.g., after removal) and re-render the list.

components.functionality: Represents a single favorited activity with its summary data, and provides actions to open the activity detail, remove from favorites, and start the add-to-day-plan flow.
event_handlers: onFavoriteItemClick: When the user clicks the item’s title/card, build a URL to activity_detail.html with id equal to activity_summary.activity_id and returnUrl equal to the encoded current page URL (including current filters/sort), then navigate.

onRemoveFavoriteClick: Call removeFavorite with favoriteItemId set to favorite_item_id; on success, either remove this item from the displayed list and update favorites-tools-summary, or trigger favorites-list.onListRefreshRequested.
onAddToDayPlanClick: Open the add-to-day-plan-form component, passing the activity_summary.activity_id (and any available session context if present) as input.

components.functionality: Provides a form for selecting a date and optional time_of_day_bucket (and optionally sessionId if available) to add the selected favorite activity to a day plan via addActivityToDayPlan.
event_handlers: onOpen: Initialize the form when invoked from favorite-item-row, binding the selected activityId and clearing any previous date/session/time_of_day_bucket selections.
onDateChange: Update the prospective day plan date in the form state.
onTimeOfDayChange: Update the time_of_day_bucket selection in the form state.
onSessionSelectionChange: If session context is available, update sessionId in the form state.

onSubmit: Call addActivityToDayPlan with date set to the selected date, activityId set to the bound activity ID, sessionId set from state if present, and time_of_day_bucket set from state if selected. On success, display confirmation using returned message and summary and emit an event (onDayPlanUpdated) for favorites-tools-summary.
onClose: Close the form without calling any API, discarding any unsaved selections.
onOpenDayPlanClick: After a successful submission, navigate to day_plan.html with date equal to the selected date from the form (this date is used as selectedDateForPlan for navigation).

components.functionality: Coordinates overall page behavior: reads URL parameters, triggers API calls (getFavoritesList and getUserToolSummaries), and wires events between child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read all URL parameters; call getFavoritesList with mapped parameters and call getUserToolSummaries; pass the results down to favorites-results-header, favorites-tools-summary, and favorites-list.
onFiltersUpdated: When favorites-filters-bar modifies the URL, re-run onInit to fetch updated data.
onSortUpdated: When favorites-results-header modifies sortBy in the URL, re-run onInit to fetch sorted data.

onFavoriteRemoved: After favorite-item-row completes removeFavorite, either update the in-memory list or call getFavoritesList again using current URL parameters; notify favorites-tools-summary of the new favorites_count.
onDayPlanUpdated: After add-to-day-plan-form successfully calls addActivityToDayPlan, optionally re-call getUserToolSummaries to refresh day_plan_today_items_count.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the global site frame from the mockup (logo block at top center, location label beneath, then a single-line navigation bar). Beneath that, place a single prominent white content card for the Favorites page, centered in the viewport. Inside the card, use the same two-column structure as the mockup: the main content column on the left (favorites list, header, filters) and a narrower tools/summary sidebar on the right (favorites-tools-summary). The add-to-day-plan-form appears as a centered modal overlay when invoked, sitting above the entire page. The pagination (favorites-page-controller) anchors the bottom of the card as a full-width row.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the global site frame from the mockup (logo block at top center, location label beneath, then a single-line navigation bar). Beneath that, place a single prominent white content card for the Favorites page, centered in the viewport. Inside the card, use the same two-column structure as the mockup: the main content column on the left (favorites list, header, filters) and a narrower tools/summary sidebar on the right (favorites-tools-summary). The add-to-day-plan-form appears as a centered modal overlay when invoked, sitting above the entire page. The pagination (favorites-page-controller) anchors the bottom of the card as a full-width row.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered, fixed-width max container (roughly 1100–1200px) using a 12-column grid. Inside the main white card, allocate about 65–70% of the width (approximately 8 columns) to the left main column that holds the favorites-results-header, favorites-filters-bar, and favorites-list. Allocate the remaining 30–35% (approximately 4 columns) to the right sidebar, anchored to the top and used for favorites-tools-summary. Vertical spacing between stacked bands inside the card (header, filters, list, pagination) follows the 24–32px rhythm, with 24px padding inside the card on all sides and 40–64px margin between the card and the surrounding gray page background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use moderate density: the header and filter toolbar occupy a relatively shallow vertical band but with comfortable padding, similar to the nav spacing in the mockup. The favorites list is denser (stacked rows) but each favorite-item-row is still treated as a mini-card with generous internal padding and 8–16px gaps between rows so nothing feels cramped. The right tools sidebar is compact but not crowded, with clear separation between summary text and primary actions. The add-to-day-plan-form modal uses a slightly more spacious layout to reduce cognitive load (larger padding and clearer field spacing).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the vertical-first, then lateral flow from the mockup. The user’s eye moves from the global logo and location down to the Favorites card. Inside the card, the eye hits the favorites-results-header at the top left, then moves down to the filters bar just below it. From there, the main scan path goes down through the favorites list on the left. In parallel, a secondary visual pull to the right is created by the sidebar tools card (favorites-tools-summary), echoing the mockup’s 'Today’s Events' panel: its colored header/primary action draws attention, encouraging users to glance right after understanding the main content on the left. When a user opens the add-to-day-plan-form, the flow is temporarily recentered on the modal in the middle of the screen.

layout.overall_description: The Favorites page sits inside the same editorial-style frame as the mockup: a light gray background fills the browser window, with the Macaroni KID logo, location, and global nav centered near the top and spaced generously. Below this, a large white content card floats in the middle of the page, inset from the viewport edges by broad gray margins. Inside this card, content is arranged on a 12-column grid. The left two-thirds form the primary Favorites area: at the very top, a slim but clear header row announces the page (favorites-results-header), followed immediately by a full-width filter toolbar (favorites-filters-bar) spanning the card from left edge to the start of the right sidebar. Below that, the left column becomes a vertical stack of favorite-item-row cards (favorites-list) running down the page with consistent spacing. Alongside this, on the right one-third of the card, a tall, top-aligned sidebar (favorites-tools-summary) mirrors the visual role of the mockup’s 'Today’s Events' column, providing a compact summary of favorites and key tools such as adding items to a day plan. At the very bottom of the white card, beneath the list and aligned with both columns, a horizontal pagination/controls band (favorites-page-controller) gives users a clear stopping point and navigation to more favorites. When users choose to add a favorite to a day plan, a centered white modal (add-to-day-plan-form) overlays the entire page on a dimmed backdrop, remaining faithful to the site’s clean card language while momentarily becoming the sole focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-filters-bar is positioned inside the main white Favorites card as a horizontal band directly beneath the favorites-results-header. It spans the full usable width of the card’s inner grid, from the left card padding edge to the gutter just before the right sidebar column (roughly 8 of 12 columns). Vertically, it occupies a modest height, about 56–72px, with 16–24px of internal vertical padding so controls aren’t cramped. Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, chips, toggles) are left-aligned, flowing from left to right, with 16px gaps between them, and there is a clear 16–24px gap below the filters bar before the first favorite-item-row in the favorites-list. The bar is visually subtle—a light border or faint background tint can distinguish it from the white card while maintaining the overall minimal editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page. On taller pages, it remains at the top of the content card without sticky behavior, mirroring the non-sticky nav in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-results-header sits at the very top inside the main white Favorites card, directly under the global navigation and card title area. It occupies a single horizontal row across the full inner width of the card, including both the main content and the right sidebar area, but its primary focal content is left-aligned over the main 8-column region. The header height is around 64–80px, with 24px of top padding from the card edge and 16–24px of bottom padding down to the filters bar. On the left side, a bold page title (e.g., Favorites) and a subtle results count align flush with the left card padding. On the right side of the same row, a sort control or view toggle can be right-aligned near the inner edge of the right sidebar column, creating a balanced but clearly left-weighted composition. The spacing and typography mirror the spacious, editorial feel of the logo and nav area in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the top anchor of the Favorites card content. It does not stick while scrolling; as the user scrolls down, it exits the viewport in the same way the hero image would move up on the mockup’s homepage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-tools-summary occupies the right-hand sidebar inside the main card, mirroring the 'Today’s Events' column from the mockup. It starts flush with the top of the card’s content area, aligned horizontally with the favorites-results-header and favorites-filters-bar, and spans roughly 4 of 12 columns (about 30–35% of the card width). The sidebar is a vertical card within the card: a white panel with 24px internal padding and a distinctive top band or header area that may use the brand’s playful accent color to echo the bold pink header in the original 'Today’s Events' panel. Within this sidebar, concise text summarizing the number of selected favorites, upcoming planned items, or instructions appears at the top, followed by vertically stacked buttons or links (e.g., bulk add to day plan, clear selection). These elements are arranged in a compact, top-aligned block, leaving comfortable whitespace toward the bottom. The left edge of this sidebar aligns to the inner grid gutter, creating a clear visual separation (about 24px) from the left-column favorites-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Top-aligned within the main card; on tall pages, it may optionally use a sticky behavior within the viewport (stopping at the card’s bottom) so that the tools summary remains visible as the user scrolls through the list, similar to a persistent 'Today’s Events' reference column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-list fills the main left column of the card beneath the favorites-filters-bar. It begins roughly 16–24px below the filters bar and extends downward to near the bottom of the card, occupying about 8 of 12 columns (65–70% of the card width). Each favorite-item-row is stacked vertically in a single column, aligned with the left card padding, with a consistent 8–16px vertical gap between rows. The right edge of the list aligns to the inner gutter that separates it from the favorites-tools-summary sidebar, leaving a clear vertical division between the two zones. The list uses a simple linear flow from top to bottom, with enough vertical padding inside each row so that the list feels card-based and airy rather than tabular or cramped. When the content exceeds the viewport height, the entire page scrolls, carrying both the list and sidebar together as in the mockup’s hero-plus-sidebar arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stacked content inside the card. It expands vertically based on the number of favorites, pushing the card’s bottom edge down and causing the page to scroll; it does not have independent scrollbars separate from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite-item-row is designed as a horizontal mini-card within the favorites-list, aligned to the left column’s width. The row spans the full width of the left content column, with 16–24px horizontal padding on both sides and 16–20px vertical padding inside its boundaries. Visually, it has a subtle border or soft shadow to distinguish it from the card background, staying consistent with the site’s card language. Content inside the row is organized into three main zones: on the left, a square or slightly rectangular thumbnail image (roughly 64–96px wide) aligned vertically center; in the middle, a text block containing the activity title, date/time, age range, and location stacked with 4–8px spacing; on the right, action controls including a filled heart icon (indicating it is saved), a primary 'Add to Day Plan' button, and a secondary remove or more options link. These right-side controls are right-aligned and vertically centered, mirroring the clean, editorial alignment of controls in the mockup. There is a consistent 8–16px gap between adjacent favorite-item-row cards to maintain readability and visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list; rows do not expand beyond their container except when showing inline feedback (such as a small confirmation message tucked just beneath the action controls), which should still maintain the row’s overall rectangular silhouette.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-day-plan-form appears as a centered modal overlay when triggered from a favorite-item-row or from the favorites-tools-summary. It sits on top of a semi-transparent darkened backdrop that covers the entire viewport, subtly blurring or dimming the main content card beneath. The modal itself is a white card with slightly rounded corners, maintaining the same design language as the main content card but on a smaller scale. On desktop, it occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (about 480–640px) and is vertically centered, with at least 64px of margin from the viewport edges on all sides. Inside the modal, there is a clear hierarchical structure: a bold title at the top, then vertically stacked form fields (date, time, notes, etc.) separated by 16–24px of vertical space, followed by a horizontal row at the bottom with primary and secondary buttons aligned to the right. The internal padding is generous (24–32px on all sides) to reduce cognitive load and match the site’s editorial spaciousness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport center, independent of page scroll. When open, the page behind is effectively locked from scrolling and interaction. If the form content exceeds the viewport height on smaller screens, the modal body can scroll internally while keeping the header and primary actions visible or easily accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-page-controller is placed at the bottom of the main Favorites card, beneath the favorites-list (and visually aligned with the bottom of the favorites-tools-summary sidebar). It occupies a low-height horizontal strip spanning the full inner width of the card. There is a generous 24–32px vertical gap between the last favorite-item-row and the pagination controls to clearly signal the end of the current page of results. Pagination elements (previous/next arrows, page numbers, or a 'Load more' control) are horizontally centered within this band or lightly right-aligned depending on the site’s pagination style, using simple text and icon treatments that echo the understated navigation in the original mockup. The band has slightly more whitespace above and below the controls than a typical list item, giving it the feel of a footer to the card’s content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the bottom of the content card. As the user scrolls, it enters the viewport only when the user reaches the end of the current set of favorites; it does not remain fixed or sticky to the viewport.

name: Day Plan
title: Day Plan
description: Planner interface to review and organize activities scheduled for a specific date, grouped by time of day with options to reorder, move, remove, and navigate to activity details.

core_features: Load and display a day plan for a selected date using the `date` URL parameter and the `getDayPlan` interface.
Group day plan activities by time-of-day bucket (morning, afternoon, evening, etc.) based on `getDayPlan` results.
Allow users to change the time-of-day bucket of an activity (e.g., move an item from afternoon to evening) via `updateDayPlanItemTime`.
Allow users to reorder activities within the same time-of-day bucket using `updateDayPlanItemSortOrder`.
Allow users to remove activities from the day plan via `removeDayPlanItem`.
Provide quick navigation to activity detail pages from each day plan item, passing `id`, `date`, and `returnUrl` URL parameters.
Allow users to change the planned date (previous/next day, direct date selection, and shortcut dates) and reload the day plan with `getDayPlan`.
Display total item count and total estimated cost for the day plan using `getDayPlan.summary`.
Display global user tool counts (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart) using `getUserToolSummaries`.
Provide date shortcut navigation (e.g., upcoming weekend) using `getDateShortcuts`.
user_workflows:
View the plan for a specific day:
1. User navigates to `day_plan.html` (optionally with a `date` URL parameter from another page).
2. Page controller parses the `date` parameter. If absent or invalid, it derives a valid current-date value at runtime and updates the URL.
3. The page calls `getDayPlan(date)` with the resolved date.
4. The page calls `getDateShortcuts()` to populate date shortcut buttons.
5. The page calls `getUserToolSummaries()` to show tool counts.
6. The day plan header and time-bucket lists render using the `getDayPlan` response. If there are no items, an empty-state component is shown.

Change the planned date (via date picker or previous/next controls):
1. User interacts with the date selector (e.g., chooses a date or clicks previous/next).
2. The date selector computes the new date value (without hardcoding) and updates the page URL parameter `date` using navigation (e.g., `day_plan.html?date=<newDate>`).
3. The page controller detects the changed `date` (via URL change) and calls `getDayPlan(newDate)`.
4. The day plan UI re-renders with items and summary for the new date.

Jump to a shortcut date (e.g., upcoming Saturday or this weekend start):
1. On page load, `getDateShortcuts()` returns shortcut dates.
2. Shortcut buttons bind to these dates (e.g., upcoming Saturday).
3. When a user clicks a shortcut, the date selector updates the `date` parameter in the URL to the selected shortcut date.
4. The page controller calls `getDayPlan` for that shortcut date and updates the UI.

Reorder activities within the same time-of-day bucket:
1. User drags a day plan item within its current bucket’s list and drops it into a new position.
2. The bucket list component computes a new `sort_order` for the dragged item (based on its new position, without hardcoded values).
3. The component calls `updateDayPlanItemSortOrder(dayPlanItemId, sort_order)`.
4. If `success` is true, the page controller calls `getDayPlan(currentDate)` again to retrieve the updated ordering and refresh the UI.
5. If `success` is false, an error message is displayed and the UI reverts to the previous order.

Move an activity to a different time-of-day bucket:
1. User drags an item from one time-of-day section (e.g., morning) to another (e.g., afternoon), or uses a bucket-change control on the item.
2. The component identifies the target bucket value (e.g., `afternoon`).
3. The component calls `updateDayPlanItemTime(dayPlanItemId, time_of_day_bucket)`.
4. If `success` is true, the page controller calls `getDayPlan(currentDate)` to refresh the items grouped by bucket.
5. If `success` is false, an error message is shown and the item remains in its original bucket.

Remove an activity from the day plan:
1. User clicks a remove/delete control on a day plan item.
2. A confirmation interaction (e.g., lightweight confirmation prompt) is presented by the component.
3. On confirmation, the component calls `removeDayPlanItem(dayPlanItemId)`.
4. If `success` is true, the page controller calls `getDayPlan(currentDate)` to get the updated items and summary and re-renders the lists.
5. If `success` is false, an error message is shown and the item remains visible.

Open activity details from the day plan:
1. User clicks on an activity’s title or dedicated details link within a day plan item.
2. The component constructs the navigation URL to `activity_detail.html` with parameters:
   - `id` = `activity_summary.activity_id` from `getDayPlan`.
   - `date` = current `date` URL parameter (the day being planned).
   - `returnUrl` = the current `day_plan.html` URL including its `date` parameter.
3. Browser navigates to `activity_detail.html` with these parameters.
4. When the user returns (via back navigation or link using `returnUrl`), the day plan page reloads using its `date` parameter and calls `getDayPlan(date)` again.

Review day summary (total activities and estimated cost):
1. After `getDayPlan(date)` completes, the summary object is available.
2. The summary panel displays `summary.total_items`, `summary.total_estimated_cost`, and `summary.currency`.
3. When the user moves, reorders, or removes items, the page controller re-fetches `getDayPlan` so the summary dynamically updates based on the backend’s calculations.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Trigger: Page is opened.
- Behavior: Parse `date` from URL, normalize or set to a valid runtime-derived current date if missing, then call `getDayPlan(date)`, `getDateShortcuts()`, and `getUserToolSummaries()`.
- UI Update: Render header, user tool summary bar, day plan buckets, and summary panel based on responses.

Date picker change:
- Trigger: User changes the date using an input (e.g., calendar picker field).
- Behavior: Component validates the input, updates the `date` URL parameter via navigation, and triggers re-fetch of `getDayPlan` for that date.
- UI Update: All components bound to the day plan re-render for the new date.

Previous/next date navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks previous-day or next-day control.
- Behavior: Component calculates adjacent date relative to current `date`, updates `date` in the URL, and page controller calls `getDayPlan` for the new date.
- UI Update: Day plan lists and summary update for the new date.

Shortcut date selection:
- Trigger: User clicks a shortcut button linked to data from `getDateShortcuts()`.
- Behavior: Component reads the corresponding shortcut date, updates `date` in the URL, and triggers `getDayPlan` for that date.
- UI Update: Day plan is updated for the selected shortcut date.

Reorder within bucket (drag-and-drop or equivalent):
- Trigger: User reorders an item within its current time-of-day list.
- Behavior: Component determines the new `sort_order` for the dragged item and calls `updateDayPlanItemSortOrder(dayPlanItemId, sort_order)`. On success, it triggers a refresh via `getDayPlan(currentDate)`.
- UI Update: Items appear in the new order and summary (if dependent on order) is refreshed.

Move between time-of-day buckets:
- Trigger: User drags an item to a different bucket or selects a different time-of-day bucket from a control on the item.
- Behavior: Component sends `updateDayPlanItemTime(dayPlanItemId, time_of_day_bucket)` with the new bucket value. On success, it triggers `getDayPlan(currentDate)`.
- UI Update: Item appears in the new bucket section; all buckets re-render to reflect backend truth.

Remove item from day plan:
- Trigger: User clicks a remove/delete icon on an item and confirms.
- Behavior: Component calls `removeDayPlanItem(dayPlanItemId)`. On success, page controller calls `getDayPlan(currentDate)`.
- UI Update: Removed item disappears; item counts and summary update accordingly.

Open activity details:
- Trigger: User clicks the activity title or a details button.
- Behavior: Component constructs URL `activity_detail.html?id=<activityId>&date=<currentDate>&returnUrl=<encodedCurrentDayPlanUrl>` and navigates.
- UI Update: Browser navigates away; return via `returnUrl` reloads this page with existing `date` parameter.

View user tool summaries:
- Trigger: Page load or explicit refresh control (if provided).
- Behavior: Component calls `getUserToolSummaries()` and displays counts for favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, and cart.
- UI Update: Counts and indicators update; tool links (e.g., to favorites list) use these counts for display only.

Handle empty day plan:
- Trigger: `getDayPlan(date)` returns `summary.total_items` equal to zero or `items` array empty.
- Behavior: Empty-state component displays context about no items for this date and offers navigation links (e.g., to `index.html` or `map.html`) including the `date` parameter.
- UI Update: No bucket lists are shown; only the empty-state guidance and still the summary panel (showing zero totals).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is controlled via URL parameters and API responses.

1) `date` URL parameter:
- Input: The page reads `date` from `window.location` when `day_plan.html` loads.
- Normalization: If `date` is missing or invalid, the client derives a valid current calendar date at runtime (without hardcoding a specific value), updates the URL to `day_plan.html?date=<derivedDate>`, and uses that date.
- Source pages: `index.html`, `map.html`, `favorites.html`, and `activity_detail.html` may link to this page with a `date` parameter. The page does not assume where the parameter came from; it simply uses its current URL value.
- Usage: The resolved `date` is passed directly to `getDayPlan(date)`. When navigation controls or shortcuts change the selected day, they update the `date` parameter in the URL and then reload or re-fetch data accordingly.

2) Day plan data state:
- `getDayPlan(date)` returns a structure containing `day_plan_id`, `date`, optional `title` and `notes`, an `items` array, and a `summary` object.
- The UI state for the list of activities is derived entirely from `getDayPlan` response. Time-of-day groupings in the UI are created based on each item's `time_of_day_bucket` (using `session_summary.time_of_day_bucket` or `time_of_day_bucket` field from the item where applicable) and its `sort_order`.
- After every mutating action (`updateDayPlanItemTime`, `updateDayPlanItemSortOrder`, `removeDayPlanItem`), the page calls `getDayPlan(currentDate)` again to ensure the UI state reflects backend truth. Local transient state is used only for interaction feedback (e.g., drag hover) and is not persisted.

3) Navigation state to other pages:
- When linking to `activity_detail.html`, the page constructs URL parameters from its current state:
  - `id` = the `activity_summary.activity_id` from `getDayPlan`.
  - `date` = current `date` URL parameter.
  - `returnUrl` = the fully-qualified or relative URL of the current `day_plan.html` page including its `date` parameter.
- `returnUrl` is treated as opaque state that the activity detail page can use for navigation back; this page simply reconstructs itself based on its `date` when reloaded.

4) User tools state:
- `getUserToolSummaries()` provides counts for favorites, today's day plan, watchlist, and cart. This data is read-only on this page and is not stored in URL parameters.
- The 'day plan today' count refers to the plan for the current day (as defined by backend logic), which may or may not match the `date` parameter being viewed. The page displays both the selected date's plan (from `getDayPlan(date)`) and the overall today-count (from `getUserToolSummaries()`) without attempting to reconcile them locally.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. All persistent state is inferred from the URL parameters and the assigned interfaces' responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and refresh logic for the Day Plan page based on the `date` URL parameter. Orchestrates calls to `getDayPlan`, `getDateShortcuts`, and `getUserToolSummaries`, and triggers re-fetches after any mutations.
Displays the selected date (from URL), optional day plan title and notes (from `getDayPlan`), and anchors the date selector controls.

Enables users to change the planned date via direct date input, previous/next controls, and shortcut buttons populated from `getDateShortcuts`. Updates the `date` URL parameter and triggers the page controller to reload the day plan.
Displays counts for favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, and cart. Provides navigation links (e.g., to favorites or cart pages) while remaining read-only in terms of tool data.

Groups day plan items by time-of-day bucket (e.g., morning, afternoon, evening, daytime, after_4pm) and renders each group as a sortable list. Supports reordering items within a bucket and moving items between buckets, while delegating item-level actions to the activity item component.

Represents a single activity in the day plan. Shows activity title, short description, primary category label, price summary, location name, and session times. Provides controls to open activity details, change its time-of-day bucket, reorder (via drag handle), and remove the item.

Handles the API interactions for mutations triggered from a single day plan item: updating its time-of-day bucket, updating its sort order, and removing it. After each successful mutation, it triggers the page controller to refresh the current plan.

Encapsulates direct calls to `updateDayPlanItemTime`, `updateDayPlanItemSortOrder`, and `removeDayPlanItem` for item-level actions, returning structured results to the actions controller and page controller.

Displays the aggregated summary for the selected date’s day plan, including total activities and total estimated cost in the appropriate currency. Automatically updates whenever `getDayPlan(date)` is re-fetched.

Shows an informative message and navigation options when there are no items in the day plan for the selected date. Uses the current `date` to build links to discovery pages (e.g., index or map) so users can start adding activities for that date in other flows.
Constructs and triggers navigation to `activity_detail.html` for a given activity, including the current planning date and a `returnUrl` pointing back to the current Day Plan page.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup's linear, top-to-bottom structure. Within the centered page canvas, the Day Plan page starts with a compact header-summary near the top, followed by a large, single white content card. Inside this card, the first band is a horizontal date selector, immediately followed by a horizontal tools/summary bar. Below that, the card splits into two columns: on the left, a tall, scrollable list of time-of-day buckets with activities; on the right, a vertical day-summary panel that echoes the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup's linear, top-to-bottom structure. Within the centered page canvas, the Day Plan page starts with a compact header-summary near the top, followed by a large, single white content card. Inside this card, the first band is a horizontal date selector, immediately followed by a horizontal tools/summary bar. Below that, the card splits into two columns: on the left, a tall, scrollable list of time-of-day buckets with activities; on the right, a vertical day-summary panel that echoes the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a centered, fixed-width main container (roughly 1064–1200px) inside a light gray full-page background. The white content card should occupy the majority of the viewport height and about the full width of the main container, with generous outer margins (40–64px) from the browser edges. Inside the card, allocate approximately 65–70% of the width (around 8 of 12 columns) to the left-hand time buckets list and 30–35% (around 4 of 12 columns) to the right-hand summary panel. The header-summary above the card uses modest vertical space but is visually separated with 24–32px of spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious, editorial feel. The card has 24px inner padding on all sides. The header-summary, date selector, and tools bar each have clear breathing room with 16–24px vertical separation. Time buckets are stacked with noticeable gaps (16–24px) between them, and each bucket groups several activity items with 8–12px vertical spacing. The right summary panel is compact but readable, with tight but consistent spacing between summary rows. Overall, prioritize legibility and scannability over packing maximum items, mirroring the mockup’s balance of rich content inside a clean, uncluttered frame.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves vertically from top to bottom: first the 'Day Plan' header-summary at the top center/left, then directly into the white content card where the date selector is the first focal control. From there, the user’s attention flows down into the left column to scan time buckets and individual activities. The right-hand summary panel, with its colored header bar and vertical card shape, provides a secondary pull to the right, similar to the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar. Within each activity item, the eye starts at the left (title/detail link), then moves rightward to the item actions, supporting quick decision-making and reordering.

layout.overall_description: The Day Plan page sits on a soft light gray background, with a centered main column that feels like a magazine spread rather than a dense app. Near the top, a simple 'Day Plan' header and brief summary text introduce the page, leaving generous whitespace above and below. Just beneath, a large white card spans most of the page width, framed by ample gray margins. Inside this card, a clean horizontal date selector band anchors the top, allowing the user to switch days easily. Directly below, a slim tools and summary bar runs the full width, showing key counts and quick actions. The main body of the card is split into two clear columns: on the left, a tall, scrollable list of time-of-day buckets with their activities stacked from morning to evening; on the right, a narrower but visually bold day-summary panel, topped with a colored header bar that echoes the mockup’s right-hand 'TODAY'S EVENTS' panel. The left side feels like the working canvas where the user manipulates activities, while the right side feels like an at-a-glance agenda. Generous spacing, clear typographic hierarchy, and the consistent card-based structure create an organized, family-friendly planner that matches the editorial aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall canvas. It sets a full-width, full-height light gray background. Centered within the viewport is a fixed-width main content container (approximately 1064–1200px wide) aligned to the horizontal center of the screen. This main container starts 48–64px from the top of the viewport, leaving a comfortable buffer beneath any global site header. Within this container, the day-plan-header appears first at the top, followed by a vertical gap of about 24–32px, then the primary white content card that contains the date selector, tools bar, time buckets list, summary panel, and empty state when applicable. The page controller ensures the white card never touches the browser edges, maintaining the framed, magazine-like effect from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a centered, responsive layout with a max-width container; scrolls vertically with the page and maintains a consistent light gray background behind all content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-header sits at the top of the main content container, just below any global navigation/logo area. It is left-aligned within the centered container but visually balanced by generous whitespace on both sides. The header spans roughly 70–80% of the container width, leaving comfortable margins to the left and right, and includes the page title ('Day Plan') and a short descriptive line beneath. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical padding within the header block itself, and about 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the large white content card below. This placement mirrors the mockup’s logo/location block above the main hero card, acting as a contextual introduction without crowding the main planner content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element at the top of the page’s content column; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-date-selector is the first element inside the large white content card. It runs horizontally across nearly the full width of the card, aligned with the card’s internal padding (about 24px inset from left and right edges). Vertically, it appears 16–24px below the top edge of the card, with 16–20px of padding above and below the date controls. The date selector’s controls (current date, previous/next arrows, calendar picker, etc.) are arranged in a single row, centered or left-aligned, echoing the mockup’s simple horizontal navigation bar. There is a clear 16–24px gap between the bottom of the date selector and the user-tools-summary-bar that follows, giving each band its own breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band at the top of the card’s content. On longer pages, it scrolls with the content; optionally, it may become sticky at the top of the viewport within the card’s area to keep date context visible, but visually it behaves as part of the card, not as a global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar sits directly beneath the date selector, spanning the same inner width of the white card (aligned to the 24px padding on both sides). It appears as a low-profile horizontal bar with slightly tighter vertical padding (around 12–16px), containing quick actions (e.g., 'Clear Day', 'Reorder', filters) on the left and a compact summary (e.g., total activities, total time) on the right. It reads like a functional counterpart to the mockup’s single-line navigation, offering at-a-glance controls before the main planner area. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this bar and the split two-column layout where the time buckets and summary panel begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip that stays directly under the date selector; scrolls with the page. In longer plans, it can optionally be made sticky beneath the date selector to keep tools visible while the user scrolls through time buckets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-time-buckets-list occupies the left side of the main content area inside the white card, just below the user-tools-summary-bar. It is laid out as a tall vertical column that spans approximately 65–70% of the card’s internal width, corresponding to about 8 of 12 grid columns. The left edge aligns with the card’s left padding; the right edge stops at an internal vertical divider zone, leaving about 32px of horizontal gap between this list and the right-hand day-plan-summary-panel. Within this column, individual time buckets (e.g., Morning, Afternoon, Evening) are stacked from top to bottom with 16–24px of separation between them, each rendered as a sub-card or grouped section with a clear bucket label at the top and the day-plan-activity-item rows beneath. The list extends downward and is the main scrollable content of the page, mirroring the hero carousel’s dominance in the left side of the mockup but adapted for list-based planner content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the left portion of the card’s main body and scrolls vertically with the page. It adapts in height based on content; on taller pages, the user scrolls mainly through this column while the right summary panel may remain partially or fully visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each day-plan-activity-item is a horizontal row within a time bucket, spanning the full width of the bucket’s content area (minus bucket-level padding). The item row is approximately 40–56px tall by default, with 12–16px of vertical padding inside. Content is left-aligned: the activity title (which incorporates or wraps the activity-detail-navigation-link) appears at the far left, with secondary metadata (time, location, tags) either beneath the title or to its immediate right in a lighter style. On the far right of the row, the day-plan-activity-item-actions cluster is aligned, separated from the text by flexible space so actions line up vertically across rows. A subtle divider or 8–12px of vertical space separates each item from the next, maintaining the moderately spacious density and keeping the list readable, similar to the compact but clear event list in the mockup’s sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within each time bucket container; expands or contracts in height based on content (e.g., multi-line titles) while maintaining consistent left-to-right alignment of text and actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-activity-item-actions component is positioned at the far right side of each activity item row. Within the row’s horizontal space, it forms a tight cluster of icon buttons or small labels (e.g., move, remove, details, drag handle) aligned to the right edge of the time bucket’s content area. Vertically, the actions are centered within the item row so they align across multiple items, creating a clean vertical line of controls down the right side of the left column. Horizontal spacing between individual action icons/buttons is 8–12px, and there is a small 8–12px inset from the row’s right padding, ensuring the actions don’t touch the bucket border. The layout supports quick scanning from left (activity title) to right (actions) without feeling cluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the right side within each activity row; remains horizontally fixed relative to the item even when the content on the left wraps to multiple lines.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-activity-item-api-adapter is a non-visual infrastructure component. It does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind each day-plan-activity-item, handling data updates (move, remove, reorder) and syncing with the backend without altering the visual arrangement of items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual representation; does not affect spatial layout. It operates purely in the background, enabling interaction logic for the visible activity items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-summary-panel occupies the right-hand column of the main white content card, mirroring the 4-column 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar in the mockup. It starts at the same vertical level as the top of the day-plan-time-buckets-list, directly below the user-tools-summary-bar. Horizontally, it sits to the right of the time buckets list, separated by approximately 32px of whitespace, and spans roughly 30–35% of the card’s internal width (around 4 of 12 grid columns). The panel itself is a nested card with its own background (white) and a subtle border or shadow. At the very top of this panel is a horizontal colored header bar (e.g., brand pink) with the panel title (such as 'Day Summary') in uppercase or bold, visually echoing the mockup’s sidebar header. Below the colored bar, the panel content stacks vertically: key metrics, a mini agenda list, or highlights, each separated by 8–16px. The panel’s height roughly tracks the height of the left column content, but in practice the page will scroll while this panel remains visually distinct and slightly more compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned within the white card’s internal grid; can optionally use sticky positioning so that its top edge remains in view as the user scrolls through the taller left-hand time buckets, reinforcing its role as a persistent at-a-glance summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-plan-empty-state appears in place of the detailed time-buckets list when there are no activities for the selected date. It is positioned in the left column area where day-plan-time-buckets-list would normally render, starting just below the user-tools-summary-bar and aligning with the card’s left padding. The empty state content (icon/illustration, headline, and explanatory text) is centered within this left column both horizontally and, when possible, vertically within the remaining viewport height, using generous padding (32–48px) and whitespace around it to reinforce a calm, friendly message. The right-hand day-plan-summary-panel remains visible in its usual position, now summarizing that the day has zero planned activities. This arrangement preserves the two-column structure from the mockup while shifting emphasis to the single empty-state message on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same spatial region as the time-buckets list in the left column and scrolls normally with the page. It collapses or is hidden when one or more time buckets/activities are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-detail-navigation-link is embedded within each day-plan-activity-item, typically wrapping the activity title text on the left side of the row. It is aligned flush with the left edge of the item’s content area, matching the baseline of the title text. The link is styled as a subtle but clearly interactive element (e.g., brand-colored text with a gentle underline on hover) that does not break the clean, editorial feel of the row. Because it is integrated into the main title, it occupies no additional layout space beyond the text itself, but it becomes the primary interactive touchpoint for navigating to detailed activity information. Its position at the far left of each row aligns with the natural reading order and the primary vertical scanning path down the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the text content of the activity item; it does not alter the block-level layout of the row and flows naturally as the text wraps on smaller screens.

name: Watchlist
title: Watchlist
description: The Watchlist page shows the user's saved classes and ongoing programs, allows filtering by age, schedule, time of day, and distance, and provides actions to open full details, register, or remove items from the watchlist.

core_features: Load the user's watchlisted classes and programs via getWatchlist, using URL parameters as filter and sort inputs.
Display a list of watchlisted items, each with key activity details (title, description, recurrence, next start time, price summary, location, distance) from activity_summary.
Provide filter controls for age range, schedule recurrence, days of week, time of day, and maximum distance, with state persisted in URL parameters.
Provide a sort control (e.g., by distance or start time) mapped to the sortBy URL parameter and getWatchlist.sort_by.
Allow navigation from a watchlist item to activity_detail.html with id and returnUrl parameters populated from the selected activity.
Allow navigation from a watchlist item to registration.html with activityId and an optional sessionId (suggested or next available, when known).
Allow users to remove individual items from the watchlist using removeFromWatchlist and immediately update the visible list and watchlist count.
Display a summary of user tool counts (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, cart) using getUserToolSummaries.
user_workflows:
View and refine saved classes:
1) User opens watchlist.html (optionally with URL filters already set).
2) Page reads URL parameters (ageMin, ageMax, scheduleRecurrence, daysOfWeek, timeOfDay, distanceMax, sortBy).
3) Page calls getWatchlist with mapped parameters (age_min, age_max, schedule_recurrence, days_of_week, time_of_day_buckets, distance_max_miles, sort_by).
4) Page renders the watchlist items list from the returned items array and shows total_count.
5) User adjusts filters (age range, schedule, days, time of day, distance) and clicks an Apply/Update button.
6) Page updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter state and re-calls getWatchlist.
7) List updates to show only items matching the new filters.

Remove a saved class/program from the watchlist:
1) User locates an item in the watchlist list.
2) User clicks the Remove action for that item.
3) Page calls removeFromWatchlist with the item’s watchlist_item_id.
4) On success, the page removes that item from the rendered list and updates any displayed watchlist count using the returned watchlist_count.
5) If the list becomes empty for the current filters, the empty-state message component is shown.

Open full details for a saved class:
1) User locates a watchlisted item in the list.
2) User clicks either the item row or a dedicated View details action.
3) Page constructs a URL to activity_detail.html with id set to activity_summary.activity_id and returnUrl set to the current watchlist page URL (including any filters and sort parameters).
4) Browser navigates to activity_detail.html; from there, user can return to the watchlist using the returnUrl parameter.

Register for a class from the watchlist:
1) User locates a watchlisted item that is eligible for registration (based on information available in the item’s summary/context).
2) User clicks the Register action on that item.
3) Page determines the activityId from activity_summary.activity_id and, if available in the client context, a suggestedOrNextSessionId (e.g., the next upcoming session for that activity).
4) Page navigates to registration.html with activityId and, when available, sessionId=suggestedOrNextSessionId.
5) After registration, the user can navigate back to watchlist.html (e.g., using browser back or links on the registration flow).

Review overall saved tools status:
1) On page load, the page calls getUserToolSummaries with no parameters.
2) The returned favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count are displayed in a user tools summary component.
3) When a watchlist item is removed via removeFromWatchlist, the watchlist_count in this component is updated using the response from removeFromWatchlist.
interactions: Filter changes (age, schedule, days, time-of-day, distance): When the user changes filter inputs and submits the filter form, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (ageMin, ageMax, scheduleRecurrence, daysOfWeek, timeOfDay, distanceMax). It then maps these to the getWatchlist parameters and re-fetches the watchlist data.

Sort change: When the user selects a different sort option (e.g., distance_nearest_first or start_time_earliest), the sortBy URL parameter is updated. The page maps sortBy to getWatchlist.sort_by and re-fetches the watchlist data.

Remove watchlist item: When the user clicks Remove on an item, the page calls removeFromWatchlist(watchlistItemId). On success, the item is removed from the in-memory list, the list is re-rendered, and user tool summary watchlist_count is updated from the response.

View details navigation: When the user clicks a watchlist item or its View details action, the page builds a URL activity_detail.html?id={activity_id}&returnUrl={encoded_current_watchlist_url} and navigates there.

Register navigation: When the user clicks Register on a watchlist item, the page navigates to registration.html?activityId={activity_id}&sessionId={suggestedOrNextSessionId_if_available}. If no appropriate sessionId is available in the client context, the sessionId parameter can be omitted or left blank according to the registration page’s contract.

Empty-state handling: After each getWatchlist response, if total_count is 0, the list component hides and an empty-state component is displayed. If user clicks a Clear filters action in the empty state, filter-related URL parameters are removed and getWatchlist is called again without those filters.

User tools summary load: On initial page load and whenever the page is refreshed, getUserToolSummaries is called. The counts are bound to the user tools summary component without any additional local aggregation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The watchlist page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. On page load, the client reads ageMin, ageMax, scheduleRecurrence, daysOfWeek, timeOfDay, distanceMax, and sortBy from the query string. Missing parameters are treated as unset filters and are not passed to getWatchlist. The page maps these URL parameters to the getWatchlist interface parameters as follows: ageMin → age_min, ageMax → age_max, scheduleRecurrence → schedule_recurrence, daysOfWeek → days_of_week (parsed from a comma-separated list into an array of weekday identifiers), timeOfDay → time_of_day_buckets (wrapped as an array if a single bucket is selected), distanceMax → distance_max_miles, and sortBy → sort_by. Whenever the user submits updated filters or changes sort, the page updates the URL (adding, updating, or removing these parameters) and then re-invokes getWatchlist with the new mapped values.
