This ensures that browser back/forward navigation restores previous filter/sort states, and that copying/sharing the URL preserves the same watchlist view. State derived from server responses (such as the current list of items and total_count) is kept in memory only for rendering and is always considered refreshable based on the URL-driven getWatchlist call, without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading URL parameters, calling getWatchlist and getUserToolSummaries, and distributing their results to dependent components. It watches for changes to the relevant URL parameters (ageMin, ageMax, scheduleRecurrence, daysOfWeek, timeOfDay, distanceMax, sortBy) and, on any change, re-invokes getWatchlist with the mapped parameters.

Renders filter controls for age range (min and max), schedule recurrence, days of the week, time-of-day bucket, and maximum distance in miles. Initializes its fields from the current URL parameters, and when the user submits, updates the URL and triggers a watchlist refresh via the orchestrator.

Provides a sort selector for the watchlist (e.g., by distance or by next start time). Reads the initial sortBy value from the URL, updates sortBy in the URL when changed, and then requests a new getWatchlist call via the orchestrator.

Displays the list of watchlist items returned by getWatchlist.items. Each row shows details from activity_summary (such as title, short_description, schedule_recurrence, primary_category_label, next_start_datetime, price_summary, location_name, distance_miles) and provides actions to view details, register, and remove the item from the watchlist.

Handles removal of a single watchlist item. When triggered (for example, from the watchlist-list component), it calls removeFromWatchlist with the watchlist_item_id, and on success removes the item from the current in-memory list and updates the watchlist count in the user tools summary component with the response data.

Shows a message and available actions when getWatchlist.total_count is 0 for the current filters. It can offer a Clear filters action that removes filter-related URL parameters, prompting the orchestrator to reload getWatchlist without filters.

Displays counts for the user’s tools (favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, cart_items_count) using the result of getUserToolSummaries. The watchlist_count value is updated when removeFromWatchlist completes successfully.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header (logo, location, nav) at the very top, then a generous gap, then a single large, centered white content card that contains the entire Watchlist experience. Inside this card, the layout immediately splits into a two-column band reminiscent of the hero + 'TODAY'S EVENTS' pattern: on the left, the main watchlist content (title, tools, list or empty state); on the right, a vertical sidebar that houses filters in a compact tools panel. Within each column, content flows top-to-bottom in a clear hierarchy: header row → tools/summary → list (or empty state) on the left; header strip → stacked filters on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header (logo, location, nav) at the very top, then a generous gap, then a single large, centered white content card that contains the entire Watchlist experience. Inside this card, the layout immediately splits into a two-column band reminiscent of the hero + 'TODAY'S EVENTS' pattern: on the left, the main watchlist content (title, tools, list or empty state); on the right, a vertical sidebar that houses filters in a compact tools panel. Within each column, content flows top-to-bottom in a clear hierarchy: header row → tools/summary → list (or empty state) on the left; header strip → stacked filters on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport with ample breathing room. Below it, the main watchlist card sits centered in a max-width (approximately 12-column) container, with 40–64px of light gray background margin on all sides to create a framed effect. Inside the card, about 65–70% of the horizontal width (8 of 12 columns) is reserved for the main watchlist column on the left, while 30–35% (4 of 12 columns) forms the right-hand filter sidebar. The card itself uses about 24px inner padding on all sides, and vertical spacing between internal sections (header row, tools, list, sidebar panels) ranges from 16–32px in line with the spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderately spacious. The left column’s list of saved items uses card-like rows with comfortable padding (roughly 16–24px inside each item and 16–24px between items), keeping text readable and actions easy to target. The right filter sidebar is slightly more compact but still airy, with stacked controls and modest 8–16px gaps between them, similar to the compact 'TODAY'S EVENTS' list. Overall, the page avoids crowding: the main card, the spacing between columns, and the open background all combine to maintain a clean, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first hits the global logo and location, then drops down to the centered watchlist card. Within the card, attention is pulled first to the left top area: the 'Watchlist' heading and associated tools/summary, then immediately down into the vertical list of saved items. The right sidebar, with its framed panel and colored header strip for filters, becomes a strong secondary attractor, encouraging a left-to-right glance after the user grasps their saved items. The sort control at the top-right of the left column acts as a visual bridge between the main content and the sidebar, reinforcing a zig-zag scan pattern: heading → sort → filters → back to list.

layout.overall_description: The Watchlist page sits on a soft, light gray background with a centered, fixed-width white card that visually mirrors a modern magazine spread. Above, the familiar Macaroni KID logo, location label, and horizontal navigation occupy the top portion of the viewport with generous breathing room. Below this, the main card floats in the center with a subtle shadow and rounded corners, framed by 40–64px of gray background on all sides. Inside the card, the content immediately resolves into a two-column layout: on the left, a broad main column (about two-thirds of the width) holds the 'Watchlist' heading, a subtle tools/summary strip, and a vertical stack of saved-class cards or an empty state; on the right, a narrower vertical panel styled similarly to the original 'TODAY'S EVENTS' sidebar presents filters in a stacked, text-first manner with a colored header band. The result feels editorial and organized: the left side reads like a feature article listing your curated activities, while the right side functions like a control margin, letting you refine what you see without overwhelming the main content. Generous spacing between the header row, toolbars, list items, and sidebar sections keeps everything light and easy to scan, while the card’s centered placement and consistent 8px-based spacing system give the page a calm, cohesive structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-query-orchestrator functions as an invisible structural layer inside the main white watchlist card. It spans the full 12-column width of the card’s inner content area, sitting just inside the card’s 24px padding and enclosing all other watchlist-specific components (title, tools, list, filters). It does not render any visible UI; instead, it simply defines the logical block in which the left and right columns are laid out according to the 8/4 column split. Visually, the user perceives only the card and its internal layout, not the orchestrator itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a non-visual, full-width block-level container within the main card; it scrolls with the page and has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-filters component occupies the entire right sidebar column of the main card, roughly 30–35% of the card’s width (4 of 12 columns). It appears as a tall, white vertical panel aligned to the top-right of the card, starting level with the top of the left column’s heading row. The panel has about 24px of internal padding and a subtle border or shadow, with a horizontal, colored header strip (e.g., pink, echoing the 'TODAY'S EVENTS' bar) labeled something like 'Filter watchlist' spanning its full width at the very top. Beneath this header, filter controls (age, schedule, time of day, distance) are stacked vertically with 8–16px gaps, forming a compact yet readable column of selectors. The filters panel stays visually distinct but secondary to the list, separated from the left column by a consistent gutter (approximately 24–32px) that preserves the card’s airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard right-hand sidebar within the main card, top-aligned with the left column content; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-sort-control is placed at the top of the left column inside the main card, acting like a compact, text-based control bar that aligns visually with the right filters panel. It sits on the same horizontal row as the 'Watchlist' page heading (or just below it), anchored to the top-right corner of the left column, approximately 65–70% of the card’s width from the left edge. The sort control itself is a short, single-line element (such as a dropdown or segmented control) that spans only as wide as needed, leaving space to its left for the page title. There is roughly 8–16px of vertical spacing above and below it, giving it enough breathing room to be tappable/clickable without feeling bulky, and a 16–24px gap below before the user-tools-summary bar begins. Positioned here, it visually bridges the main list and the right filter panel, encouraging users to consider both ordering and filtering together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders as an inline-right control within the left column’s top row; it flows with the document and remains part of the scrollable main content with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-list component occupies the majority of the left column beneath the sort control and user-tools-summary, taking up about 65–70% of the card’s total width (an 8-column span). It begins roughly 16–24px below the tools/summary strip and extends downward as a vertical stack of card-like rows representing each saved class or program. Each watchlist item spans the full width of this left column, with 16–24px of internal padding and 16–24px of vertical spacing between items, echoing the mockup’s spacious yet structured content presentation. Item content (title, age range, schedule, distance, CTAs) is arranged text-first, with primary actions like 'View details' or 'Register' toward the right side of each item, and smaller secondary actions (including remove) tucked neatly near them. As the main scannable area, the list is visually dominant but tempered by ample whitespace so it feels like a curated editorial column rather than a dense app table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level list within the left column; its height is variable based on the number of items and it scrolls with the page, with no fixed or sticky elements inside by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-remove-action appears inside each individual watchlist-list item as a contextual control rather than as a separate page-level block. Within each item’s card, it is positioned toward the right side, aligned either on the same horizontal line as the primary action (e.g., 'Register') or just beneath it, using a smaller, text-link or icon-button style to signal its secondary nature. There is a small 8–12px horizontal gap between the remove action and the primary CTA to prevent crowding, while maintaining a compact action group. Vertical padding around the action aligns with the item’s overall padding, so the row still feels open and balanced, preserving the clean, magazine-like rhythm of items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded as an inline element within each list item card; it moves with its parent item as the user scrolls and does not alter the overarching layout or column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist-empty-state replaces the watchlist-list within the left column when there are no saved items, occupying the same 8-column main content area. It is centered horizontally in the left column and positioned with generous vertical breathing room, roughly centering it within the visible height of the card while still respecting the top spacing beneath the sort control and tools/summary row. The empty state appears as a compact but prominent white panel or illustration block with a clear headline (e.g., 'Your watchlist is empty'), supporting text, and one or two primary actions (such as 'Browse events'), all arranged in a vertically stacked, center-aligned layout. The panel’s width is narrower than the column (about 60–70% of the left column width), leaving soft margins on both sides so it feels like a focal callout. The right filter sidebar remains visible and unchanged, subtly encouraging users to explore and filter the broader catalog even when the watchlist is empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same block position as the list within the left column; it scrolls with the page, with no fixed behavior, and only appears when there are no items to render in the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary component sits in the left column directly below the heading/sort row and above the watchlist-list or empty state. It spans the full width of the left column (8 columns) and reads as a low-profile toolbar or summary strip, approximately 56–72px tall, with 16–24px of internal horizontal padding. Content inside might include the count of saved items, quick toggles (e.g., 'Show only ongoing programs'), or a compact explanation; these elements are aligned horizontally, left-to-right, but spaced so that the strip feels light and not crowded. A subtle background tint or top border differentiates this bar from the list items below while maintaining the white card aesthetic. There is a 16–24px gap between this toolbar and the start of the first list item (or empty state), preserving the clear sectional rhythm seen in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders as a standard horizontal toolbar within the left column; it remains just below the heading and sort control and scrolls away with the content as the user moves down the page.

name: About
title: About
description: Page that loads and displays dynamic background information about the local family events guide and shows a summary of the user’s tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart).

core_features: Request and display About content (title, HTML body, last updated metadata) using getAboutContent when the page loads.
Support direct linking to sections of the About content using a URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=...), updating scroll position after content loads.
Show a user tools summary bar using getUserToolSummaries, displaying counts for favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, and cart items.
Allow navigation from the user tools summary bar to related pages (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) via standard links with URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states for both getAboutContent and getUserToolSummaries without relying on hardcoded data.
user_workflows: 1) User opens About from footer → Browser loads about.html → about-page-controller reads URL parameters → about-page-controller calls getAboutContent → While waiting, about-content-section shows a loading state → On success, about-content-section renders the dynamic title and body_html, and last-updated-indicator shows last_updated → section-anchor-navigator checks the section URL parameter and scrolls to the matching anchor within the loaded content if available.

2) User opens About from an external link specifying a section (e.g., about.html?section=sourcing) → about-page-controller initializes and calls getAboutContent → After body_html is rendered, section-anchor-navigator locates the element whose anchor or data attribute matches the section value and scrolls to it → If the user clicks a different section tab, section-anchor-navigator updates the section parameter in the URL and scrolls to the corresponding section again.

3) User wants to understand tool availability while reading About → On page load, about-page-controller calls getUserToolSummaries in parallel with getAboutContent → user-tools-summary-bar shows a loading state until data is returned → On success, it displays counts for favorites, day plan items for today, watchlist items, and cart items → User clicks a tool item (e.g., favorites count) → user-tools-summary-bar navigates to the corresponding tool page (e.g., favorites.html) using a standard link.

4) User refreshes About content while staying on the page → User triggers a refresh action (e.g., clicks a reload control within about-content-section) → about-page-controller calls getAboutContent again without reloading the entire page → Loading state is shown only in the content area → On success, the content and last_updated display are updated → section-anchor-navigator re-applies the current section parameter to maintain the user’s position where possible.

5) Error handling for About content → getAboutContent fails (network or server error) → about-content-section displays a generic error state without any hardcoded content data, and optionally provides a retry action → If user clicks retry, about-page-controller calls getAboutContent again.

6) Error handling for user tools summary → getUserToolSummaries fails → user-tools-summary-bar shows an error or fallback state (e.g., hides counts and disables links or shows a neutral message), without assuming any default numeric values → If a retry action exists, triggering it calls getUserToolSummaries again.
interactions: User clicks the 'About' link in the site footer: browser navigates to about.html (optionally with a section parameter).
On page load, about-page-controller automatically calls getAboutContent and getUserToolSummaries; no user action is required.
User scrolls within the About content; the browser handles scroll, and section-anchor-navigator may update browser history/hash or internal state if scrolling crosses defined section boundaries.

User clicks a section navigation item (e.g., a tab or sidebar link representing a conceptual section of the About content): section-anchor-navigator updates the section URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=<value>) using pushState/replaceState-compatible logic and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor inside body_html once it is present.

User clicks a tool summary item (favorites, today’s plan, watchlist, cart) in user-tools-summary-bar: a standard link click navigates to the relevant page URL (e.g., favorites.html, day-plan.html?date=today), preserving any needed URL parameters for those target pages.
User clicks a refresh/retry control for About content in about-content-section: triggers a new call to getAboutContent via about-page-controller.
User clicks a refresh/retry control for tool summaries in user-tools-summary-bar: triggers a new call to getUserToolSummaries via about-page-controller.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses URL parameters instead of localStorage to control its state. The primary parameter is `section`, used for deep-linking and internal navigation. Example: `about.html?section=<sectionKey>`. On initial load, about-page-controller parses the `section` parameter value (if present) and stores it in in-memory state during the session. After `getAboutContent` resolves and body_html is rendered, section-anchor-navigator attempts to find a content anchor that corresponds to `section` (e.g., by matching against element IDs, name attributes, or data attributes within the rendered HTML) and scrolls to it. If no matching anchor is found, the page stays at the top of the content. When the user clicks a different section navigation item, section-anchor-navigator updates the `section` parameter in the URL (using navigation or history APIs) and triggers a scroll to the new section. If the page is reloaded with the new URL, the same section logic applies. The user tools summary does not introduce additional URL parameters on the About page itself; it simply reflects the server-side state from `getUserToolSummaries`. Navigation from user-tools-summary-bar to other pages can include their own URL parameters (e.g., `day-plan.html?date=today`), but these are handled by those pages. Neither About content nor tool counts are cached in URL parameters; the page always relies on real-time calls to getAboutContent and getUserToolSummaries to avoid any hardcoded or assumed data values.

components.functionality: Central controller for about.html. On page load, it parses URL parameters, triggers API calls to getAboutContent and getUserToolSummaries, and distributes the resulting data and states (loading, success, error) to child components. It also coordinates re-fetching when retry or refresh actions are triggered by child components.

Displays the dynamic About information. It receives the title, body_html, and last_updated data from about-page-controller. In loading state, it shows a non-data-specific loading indicator. In error state, it shows an error message and exposes a retry action. In success state, it renders the title as the page’s heading and injects body_html as the main content, while delegating last-updated display to last-updated-indicator.

Shows the last updated information for the About content. It receives last_updated from about-page-controller and formats it for display. If last_updated is unavailable or null, the component hides itself or shows a neutral placeholder without assuming any concrete date.

Manages deep-linking and navigation between conceptual sections of the About content using the `section` URL parameter. On initialization, it reads the current `section` parameter and, once about-content-section signals that content is rendered, scrolls to the matching anchor within body_html (if one exists). It also renders a set of section navigation controls (for example, tabs or sidebar links) that correspond to known keys. When a user clicks one of these controls, it updates the `section` URL parameter and scrolls to the corresponding anchor.

Displays summary counts for the user’s core tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart) based on getUserToolSummaries. In loading state, it shows non-data-specific placeholders. In error state, it can hide tool-specific details and show a retry control. In success state, it shows each tool with its corresponding count and provides navigable links to each tool’s dedicated page.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The About page follows the same top-to-bottom, centered card pattern as the mockup: global header (logo, location, main nav) sits above a large, centered white content card on a light gray page background. Inside this card, the layout splits into two main vertical zones: a dominant left column that replaces the hero carousel with the About content (title, meta info, section anchors, and rich text), and a narrower right column that reuses the 'Today’s Events' sidebar pattern for a 'User Tools Summary' panel. A slim page-level control/header band sits at the top of the card, spanning its full width and framing the content underneath.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page follows the same top-to-bottom, centered card pattern as the mockup: global header (logo, location, main nav) sits above a large, centered white content card on a light gray page background. Inside this card, the layout splits into two main vertical zones: a dominant left column that replaces the hero carousel with the About content (title, meta info, section anchors, and rich text), and a narrower right column that reuses the 'Today’s Events' sidebar pattern for a 'User Tools Summary' panel. A slim page-level control/header band sits at the top of the card, spanning its full width and framing the content underneath.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The centered content card maintains a fixed max-width (aligned to a 12-column grid) and is surrounded by generous light-gray margins that take roughly 15–20% of the viewport on large screens. Inside the card, approximately 65–70% of the card width (left 8 columns) is reserved for the About content and its section navigation, while 30–35% (right 4 columns) is reserved for the user-tools summary sidebar. Vertical spacing between major elements inside the card uses roughly 24–32px steps, with 24px internal padding around the card edges and similar padding inside the right-hand sidebar panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The About page keeps a moderately spacious, editorial feel. The top page-control band and section anchor navigator are clean and single-line, with ample breathing room above and below. The About content section uses comfortable line lengths and paragraph spacing, while still allowing multiple subsections on screen without feeling crowded. The user-tools summary bar is more compact, stacking its items with modest vertical spacing so the user can quickly scan their tools without overwhelming the main About narrative.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first lands on the global logo and location, then moves down to the top of the About card. Within the card, the page controller/header at the top establishes the page context, followed immediately by the About title and last-updated info. From there, attention drops slightly to the horizontal section-anchor navigator, which guides users deeper into the page. The main reading path continues down the left column through the About content, while the bold, right-aligned user-tools summary bar creates a secondary pull to the right, encouraging occasional side-glances to the user's tools and status as they scroll the main content.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits within a centered, magazine-style layout: a light gray background frames a single large white card that contains all About-specific content. Below the global logo, location, and primary navigation, this card begins with a full-width page-level control band that introduces the About page. Inside the card, the content splits into two columns. The left, wider column carries the page’s editorial weight: a clear About heading, a subtle last-updated line, a slim horizontal section-anchor navigator, and then the main About narrative with subsections and imagery. The right, narrower column echoes the vertical 'Today’s Events' sidebar from the mockup: a white panel with a colorful header bar, summarizing the user’s tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart) in a stacked list. As the user scrolls, the left column reads like a modern article, while the right column remains a stable reference for their personal tools, maintaining the clean, family-friendly, card-based aesthetic of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller frames the entire About content card just below the global site header. Visually, it appears as a full-width band at the top of the white card, spanning the entire centered max-width container (all 12 columns of the grid). It sits approximately 40–64px below the bottom of the global navigation, with the light gray page background visible around the card. Inside this band, there is generous 24px padding on all sides. The primary About page title (e.g., 'About Macaroni KID [Location]') is left-aligned within this band, starting roughly 24px from the card’s left edge. Any page-level controls (such as a subtle breadcrumb or view toggle, if present) align to the right edge within the same band, mirroring the simple, text-first styling of the main navigation. The band’s height naturally expands to fit its content but visually reads as a low-profile header strip that anchors the rest of the page content. All subsequent components (last-updated-indicator, section-anchor-navigator, about-content-section, user-tools-summary-bar) sit below this controller within the same white card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-section occupies the main left column of the card, directly below the section-anchor-navigator and last-updated-indicator. Horizontally, it spans approximately 65–70% of the card width, aligned to the left edge of the card (grid columns 1–8 out of 12). Its left edge is flush with the card’s interior padding (about 24px from the card border), and its right edge lines up with the vertical division between the main content and the user-tools summary bar. Vertically, there is roughly 16–24px of space between the bottom of the section-anchor-navigator and the top of the about-content-section, maintaining a clear but tight visual connection. Inside this section, text blocks, subheadings, and any illustrative images or callouts are arranged in a single-column, editorial style with comfortable margins: paragraphs separated by 12–16px, section headings given 24–32px above them, and any inline media constrained to the same content width to keep a consistent reading column. The result is a readable, article-like area that dominates the left side of the card while leaving enough negative space around it to avoid feeling dense, even when multiple subsections are visible on screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card; scrolls naturally as the user reads down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated-indicator appears as a small, subdued line of meta text positioned within the left column, between the about-page-controller band and the section-anchor-navigator. It is horizontally aligned with the About title: its left edge aligns to the same vertical line as the title text, roughly 24px in from the card’s left border. The indicator sits about 8–12px below the bottom of the main About title, giving a close but clear relationship that signals metadata. It may extend toward the right within the left column but should not cross into the right sidebar’s space; instead, it should visually cap the top of the left content area. Its font size and color are slightly muted compared to the title and section anchors, ensuring it reads as supportive information rather than a focal point. There is a comfortable 16–24px gap below this indicator before the section-anchor-navigator begins, so the meta line doesn’t crowd the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls with the content and remains attached to the top of the left column’s content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-anchor-navigator is a single-line horizontal navigation row that sits in the left column immediately below the last-updated-indicator and above the main about-content-section. It aligns with the same left margin as the title and body text (about 24px from the card’s left edge) and spans the full width of the left column (up to the vertical division before the right sidebar). Navigation items (e.g., 'Overview', 'Our Story', 'How It Works', 'Contact') are laid out left-to-right in a single row, each with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and 8–12px vertical padding, echoing the clean inline navigation from the mockup. There is ample whitespace above (16–24px from the meta line) and below (16–24px before the start of the body content) so the nav feels light and scannable rather than crowded. A subtle underline or pill-style highlight may indicate the active section, using brand accent color in a way that parallels the pink header bar of the right sidebar, subtly tying the two columns together visually.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the card, this row can become sticky relative to the viewport when the user scrolls past the initial title area, remaining pinned to the top of the visible left column until the end of the about-content-section. This mimics a section-tab behavior and supports quick intra-page navigation while maintaining the editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar occupies the right sidebar zone of the main card, mirroring the 'TODAY’S EVENTS' panel placement from the mockup. Horizontally, it spans approximately 30–35% of the card width (grid columns 9–12 out of 12) and is aligned flush to the card’s right interior padding (about 24px from the right border). It starts near the top of the card’s content area: vertically aligned so that its colored header bar is roughly level with the section-anchor-navigator and the upper portion of the about-content-section. The bar itself is a distinct white card-within-a-card, with a prominent colored header strip at the top (using the brand’s playful accent color, similar to the pink 'TODAY’S EVENTS' header). This header contains a concise title like 'Your Tools' and possibly small labels for counts (e.g., 'Favorites (3)'). Below the header, the interior of the bar lists the user’s tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart) in a vertical stack. Each tool row has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with minimal dividers or subtle hairlines between items to maintain clarity without clutter. The total height of the sidebar is enough to show all tools and a few key details at once but remains visually lighter than the main content column. Generous margin (24–32px) surrounds the sidebar within the main card, ensuring it doesn’t feel cramped against the about-content-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar within the bounds of the main card on larger screens: once scrolled into view, it remains fixed relative to the viewport’s right side while the left-column about-content-section scrolls, until the bottom of the card is reached. On narrower screens, it should stack below the about-content-section, becoming a full-width block that scrolls normally.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can see current support contact details and send questions, suggestions, or corrections via a simple contact form.

core_features: Display current support contact details (email, phone, address, support hours) using the getContactInfo interface.
Provide a basic contact form (name, email, subject, message) that submits via the submitContactForm interface and shows an inline confirmation including any returned ticket ID.
Pre-fill the contact form subject and/or message context from URL parameters so users coming from other pages (e.g., an activity details page) can quickly send a relevant inquiry.
Show a compact summary of the user’s tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, cart items) using getUserToolSummaries, with navigation links to those tools.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Send a general question
1. User clicks the 'Contact' link in the footer on any page; browser navigates to contact.html.
2. On page load, the page controller calls getContactInfo to retrieve support_email, support_phone, address_lines, and support_hours and passes them to the contact-info-panel component.
3. In parallel, the page controller calls getUserToolSummaries and passes the returned counts to the user-tools-summary-bar for display.
4. The contact-form component initializes empty input fields (name, email, subject, message) because no subject-related URL parameter is present.
5. User fills out all required fields and clicks the submit button.
6. The contact-form validates locally (e.g., required fields, basic email format). If valid, it calls submitContactForm with the current form values.
7. When submitContactForm returns, the contact-form updates the submission-status-message component with success, message, and ticket_id.
8. If success is true, the form shows a confirmation state (including ticket_id if provided) and may clear or lock the inputs. If success is false, an error state is shown using the returned message, and the user can edit inputs and resubmit.

Workflow: Report an issue with a specific activity
1. User is on an activity details page and clicks a 'Contact' link that navigates to contact.html with URL parameters (for example: contact.html?subject=activity_issue&activityId={activityId}).
2. On page load, the page controller parses URL parameters and passes them to the contact-form component.
3. The contact-form pre-fills the subject field based on the 'subject' parameter and can include the activityId value in the subject or the message body (for example, by prefixing or appending a label containing the ID), so that the context reaches submitContactForm within the subject and/or message strings.
4. getContactInfo and getUserToolSummaries are called as in the general workflow; contact details and user tool counts are displayed.
5. User optionally refines the pre-filled subject/message, then clicks submit.
6. submitContactForm is called with name, email, subject (including any encoded activity context), and message. The response is used to show confirmation or error via submission-status-message.
7. User remains on contact.html with the same URL parameters; no navigation is required after submission.

Workflow: Quickly check tool counts while on the Contact page
1. User opens contact.html via the footer link.
2. On page load, getUserToolSummaries is called; the favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count are passed to user-tools-summary-bar.
3. The bar displays each tool with its current count.
4. User clicks one of the tool summary links (e.g., favorites). The click triggers navigation to the appropriate tool page (for example: favorites.html, day-plan.html?date={today}, watchlist.html, or cart.html), without affecting the state of the contact form; if the user uses the browser Back button, the partially filled contact form state can be restored by client-side logic (not via URL parameters).
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Event: contact.html is loaded.
- Actions:
  - Parse URL parameters (e.g., 'subject', 'activityId', 'source').
  - Trigger getContactInfo; when resolved, bind support_email, support_phone, address_lines, and support_hours to contact-info-panel.
  - Trigger getUserToolSummaries; when resolved, bind favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count to user-tools-summary-bar.
  - Initialize contact-form field values; if URL parameters exist, pre-fill subject and/or message using those values.

Interaction: Edit contact form fields
- Event: User types into name, email, subject, or message fields.
- Actions:
  - Update local form state for each field.
  - Clear any previous validation errors for the field being edited.
  - If a prior submission error is being displayed, allow it to remain until a new submission is attempted.

Interaction: Submit contact form
- Event: User clicks the 'Send' or equivalent submit button.
- Actions:
  - Run client-side validation (required fields and basic email format).
  - If validation fails, block submission and show inline error messages per field.
  - If validation passes, disable the submit button and show a submitting state.
  - Call submitContactForm with the current values of name, email, subject, and message (subject and/or message may already contain URL-based context like an activity ID or source label).
  - On success response (success = true):
    - Pass response data to submission-status-message to display a success confirmation and the ticket_id if present.
    - Optionally reset or lock the form fields and re-enable the submit button in a "submitted" state.
  - On error response (success = false):
    - Display an error message using the returned message in submission-status-message.
    - Re-enable the submit button so the user can attempt again.

Interaction: Dismiss submission status
- Event: User clicks a close icon or 'OK' within submission-status-message.
- Actions:
  - Hide the status banner.
  - Maintain the current state of the form fields so the user can adjust and resubmit if needed.

Interaction: Click user tools summary link
- Event: User clicks a segment of user-tools-summary-bar (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart).
- Actions:
  - Use standard navigation to go to the related page using URL-based addressing, for example:
    - Favorites: favorites.html
    - Day plan (today): day-plan.html?date={today-date}
    - Watchlist: watchlist.html
    - Cart: cart.html
  - No API calls on click from this page (all data needed was already fetched on load).

Interaction: Refresh or deep link with URL parameters
- Event: User reloads contact.html with subject or context-related URL parameters.
- Actions:
  - Re-run page load parsing of URL parameters.
  - Re-initialize the contact-form fields using the new parameters (e.g., change the auto-filled subject).
  - Re-fetch getContactInfo and getUserToolSummaries to ensure details and counts are current.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and in-page component state. URL parameters do not trigger new server interfaces beyond the required getContactInfo, getUserToolSummaries, and submitContactForm calls.

Supported URL parameters:
- subject: A free-text value used to pre-fill the contact-form subject input. On page load, if 'subject' is present, the contact-form initializes its subject field with this value. This value is then passed directly into submitContactForm.subject when the form is submitted.
- activityId: A value representing the related activity. The Contact page does not call any activity-related interfaces; instead, the contact-form component can embed this value into the subject and/or message (e.g., by concatenating it into the strings) so that the context is included within submitContactForm.subject or submitContactForm.message.
- source: A string indicating where the user came from (for example, 'events_list' or 'membership_page'). As with 'activityId', this parameter is not sent as a separate API parameter but may be included by the client within the subject or message text before calling submitContactForm.

URL parameters are read only once on page load to initialize the contact-form state. Subsequent edits by the user do not modify the URL. Submitting the contact form does not change the URL (no redirect or query-string modification is required); success and error states are managed entirely in client-side component state using the response from submitContactForm.

The calls to getContactInfo and getUserToolSummaries are not affected by URL parameters; they always return the current canonical contact details and tool summary counts at the time of page load. If the user manually reloads the page, the interfaces are called again to refresh data, and URL parameters are re-applied to the form initialization.

components.functionality: Displays current support contact details retrieved from getContactInfo, including support_email, support_phone, address_lines, and support_hours. Handles loading and error states so users can still use the contact form even if contact details fail to load.

Shows a compact summary of the user’s tools using counts from getUserToolSummaries (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist entries, and cart items) and provides navigation links to those related pages.

Collects user inputs (name, email, subject, message), initializes subject and/or message from URL parameters when available, validates required fields, and submits the data via submitContactForm. After submission, it passes the response to submission-status-message and manages the form’s disabled/enabled state.
Displays the outcome of the latest submitContactForm call. Shows success confirmation (including ticket_id if provided) or an error message. Can be dismissed without affecting the form contents.

Coordinates page-level behaviors: parses URL parameters, triggers initial calls to getContactInfo and getUserToolSummaries, and passes their results to child components. Ensures that on any reload or direct visit, the page is correctly initialized without using localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: global header area (outside this spec) → page wrapper → a single, prominent white content card centered in the viewport. Inside that card, content is stacked vertically in a clear sequence: first a full‑width submission status banner (when present), then a slim horizontal user tools bar, followed by a two‑column main band where the left column is the primary contact form and the right column is a secondary contact info panel. All components sit on a shared editorial-style card with clearly defined edges against a light gray page background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: global header area (outside this spec) → page wrapper → a single, prominent white content card centered in the viewport. Inside that card, content is stacked vertically in a clear sequence: first a full‑width submission status banner (when present), then a slim horizontal user tools bar, followed by a two‑column main band where the left column is the primary contact form and the right column is a secondary contact info panel. All components sit on a shared editorial-style card with clearly defined edges against a light gray page background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The centered content card uses a max‑width container (roughly 1040–1200px) with generous outer margins so it feels framed by the light gray background. Inside the card, vertical padding is about 24px top and bottom, with 24px–32px between stacked components. Horizontally, the main band below the user tools bar follows the 12‑column grid: the contact form occupies roughly 8 columns (about 65–70% of the card width, left side) and the contact info panel occupies 4 columns (about 30–35%, right side). The submission status banner and user tools bar both span nearly 100% of the card width, maintaining a clear top band above the two‑column area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious. The status banner and user tools bar are visually light, using compact but readable text with comfortable padding so they don’t feel crowded. The contact form side is denser, similar to the hero carousel in the mockup: multiple fields stacked with consistent vertical rhythm and generous field spacing. The right contact info column is more compact like the ‘TODAY'S EVENTS’ sidebar: a vertical stack of concise contact details and small actions, separated by subtle dividers and 8–12px gaps. Overall whitespace ensures the page feels calm and readable, not overloaded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mimics the mockup’s vertical-first, then lateral scanning pattern. After the global logo/nav (outside scope), the eye moves to the top of the white card. If present, the status banner draws immediate attention as a colored strip across the top. The user tools bar beneath acts as a quick-scan row of secondary options. From there, the user’s focus drops into the main two‑column band: the left contact form column is dominant and naturally becomes the primary focal area, similar to the hero image. Once the user understands the form, the eye is pulled rightward to the narrower contact info sidebar with its slightly bolder header treatment, echoing the mockup’s ‘TODAY'S EVENTS’ panel as a structured, scannable list of support details.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page is presented as a centered, magazine‑style composition on a light gray background. A single large white content card sits in the middle of the viewport, with generous breathing room above, below, and on both sides so the card feels framed and important. Inside the card, a full‑width top band is reserved for system feedback: when a message is submitted, a colored status banner appears here, stretching edge‑to‑edge within the card. Just below it, a slim horizontal user tools bar spans the width, offering secondary shortcuts like FAQs or guidelines. Beneath this band, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, occupying about two‑thirds of the width, a spacious contact form with a clear title and stacked fields forms the main interaction area. On the right, a narrower column mirrors the mockup’s vertical events sidebar: a white panel with a subtle pink or brand‑accent header labeled something like “Contact Info” or “Support Details,” listing email, social links, and office details in a tidy vertical stack. The form and sidebar align to a shared baseline and share consistent padding, making the card feel like a single, cohesive module. The overall effect is editorial rather than app‑like: clean type, ample white space, and a clear left‑to‑right reading path that guides the user from the form to the detailed contact information.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller acts as the structural wrapper for the entire Contact page content below the global header. It centers a max‑width content region in the viewport, using a 12‑column grid and generous outer margins on desktop (approximately 40–64px from the browser edges on left and right, and 48–64px of space above and below the main card). Inside this centered region, it renders one large white content card on a light gray page background, with subtle corner radius and a soft drop shadow in line with the card-based mockup. All other components—the submission status message, user tools summary bar, contact form, and contact info panel—are placed inside this card. The controller maintains a linear top‑to‑bottom flow: status banner at the top of the card, then the horizontal tools bar, then the two-column layout below, keeping alignment and spacing consistent with the base 8px spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a non-scrolling, non-sticky layout wrapper that defines the central page grid and max-width container. It ensures that on wider viewports, the card remains centered and does not expand beyond the defined maximum width, while on narrower viewports it allows the card to flex to nearly full width with reduced side margins. It does not itself render visible UI elements beyond the background, card container, and overall spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-message appears at the very top inside the white content card, directly under the card’s top padding boundary and spanning nearly 100% of the card’s inner width. It presents as a horizontal banner with a modest height (approximately 48–64px), using a colored background (e.g., soft green for success, soft red for error) that stands out against the white card. There is 16–24px of padding inside the banner, with text centered vertically and aligned to the left, following the editorial style. When present, it creates a clear, immediate focal point as the first element in the card. Below the banner, a vertical gap of about 16–24px separates it from the user-tools-summary-bar so the top of the card doesn’t feel cramped. When no status is active, this banner fully collapses, allowing the user-tools-summary-bar to rise to the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is part of the natural document flow, pushing content below it down when visible and collapsing entirely when hidden. It is not sticky; as the user scrolls the page, the banner scrolls away with the card. It may appear with a brief fade or slide animation but remains anchored to the top of the content card rather than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar sits directly below the submission-status-message (or at the very top of the card if no status is shown), spanning the full inner width of the white content card. Visually, it reads as a slim, horizontal band with a light background tint or a subtle bottom border to distinguish it from the main body content. Within this band, a small title or label appears on the left (e.g., “Helpful Links” or “Tools”), followed by a row of concise text links or pills aligned horizontally and left-justified, with each item separated by 8–16px. The bar has approximately 16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding (matching the card’s internal padding), maintaining the spacious yet compact feel of the navigation in the mockup. Below the bar, a vertical gap of roughly 24px sets it apart from the two‑column main content so it reads as a distinct, lightweight utilities row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is a standard in-flow horizontal component. On desktop it remains a single row; on narrower viewports, its items wrap to a second line if needed but the bar itself remains at the top of the content stack, above the form and contact info. It is not sticky and does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the left side of the main two‑column band inside the white content card and serves as the dominant content block, analogous to the hero carousel in the mockup. Starting roughly 24px below the user-tools-summary-bar, the form column begins with a prominent heading (e.g., “Get in Touch” or “Contact Us”) left-aligned and set on the same baseline as the right column’s header for visual cohesion. The form column uses approximately 8 of the 12 available grid columns, equating to about 65–70% of the card’s width. Within this column, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px between each field group, and left/right padding aligns with the card’s 24px inner padding. The layout is clean and linear: label above input, full-width fields, and clear grouping for message, contact details, and consent or subscription options. The submit button is placed at the bottom left of this column, aligned with the left edge of the inputs, with 24–32px top margin separating it from the last field. The right edge of the form column aligns vertically with the left edge of the contact-info-panel column, maintaining a clear, vertically aligned gutter between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is a non-sticky, scrollable content block within the card. On desktop, it remains a left column; on tablet and mobile breakpoints, the contact-form shifts to occupy 100% width and appears above the contact-info-panel, preserving the linear reading order while maintaining generous vertical spacing between grouped fields. It expands vertically as fields and validation messages appear but remains constrained by the card’s max width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel is positioned to the right of the contact-form, forming the secondary column of the main band and visually echoing the ‘TODAY'S EVENTS’ sidebar from the mockup. It begins on the same vertical line as the top of the contact form’s heading, creating a unified baseline. The panel occupies roughly 4 of the 12 grid columns (about 30–35% of the card’s width) and is separated from the form by a clear vertical gutter of 24–32px. The panel itself appears as a distinct, nested card within the larger card: white background, light border or shadow, and internal padding of about 24px. At the top of this panel, a horizontal header bar or accent strip in the brand pink runs full width of the panel, with a label such as “Contact Info” or “Support” aligned left, mimicking the bold pink header of the ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ sidebar. Below this header, contact details (email, phone, social links, mailing address, business hours) are stacked in compact sections. Each item or section is separated by 8–12px vertical spacing and optionally subtle dividers, creating a dense but still readable list. The overall column height is visually balanced to sit roughly in line with the typical vertical extent of the contact form, though it may be slightly shorter, leaving some white space at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It behaves as a fixed-width column relative to the grid, maintaining its right-aligned position within the main card on desktop. It scrolls with the page and is not sticky. On narrower viewports (tablet and mobile), it reflows below the contact form as a full-width panel, maintaining its card-like appearance and internal padding but expanding to match the card’s width with consistent side margins.

name: Help & FAQ
title: Help & FAQ
description: Page where users can read dynamically loaded help articles, FAQs, and step-by-step guides about using search, filters, favorites, day plans, registrations, memberships, and other tools, and quickly see the status of their own tools.

core_features: Load and display structured Help & FAQ content using getFAQContent, grouped into sections and questions with expandable answers.
Provide client-side search and task-based filtering across all loaded FAQ questions and answers.
Support deep-linking to a specific section and question using URL parameters so users can share direct links to particular help topics.
Surface user tool summaries (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist, cart items) using getUserToolSummaries and use them to contextualize relevant help topics.
Allow users to filter help content by task identifiers (from related_task_ids) to see all FAQs related to a specific task or workflow.
Handle contextual links within help answers that navigate to browsing events (browse.html?categoryId=events) or to the map view (map.html).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Read general help and navigate by section
1) User opens help.html with no URL parameters.
2) The page controller calls getFAQContent and getUserToolSummaries.
3) After getFAQContent resolves, the first section from the returned sections array is set as the active section (unless a sectionId URL parameter is present).
4) The section sidebar lists all sections from getFAQContent; the active section is highlighted.
5) The question list shows all questions in the active section, with answers collapsed by default.
6) User clicks another section in the sidebar; the active section changes, the URL is updated with sectionId for the chosen section, and the question list refreshes for that section.

Workflow: Search within the Help & FAQ
1) User types text into the FAQ search bar.
2) On each change or on form submit (depending on implementation), the search query is written to the URL as query.
3) The question list component applies a client-side filter over all questions from getFAQContent, matching against question text and answer_html (after stripping HTML tags for matching).
4) The list shows only questions matching the query across all sections; the active section stays visible but the UI indicates that a search filter is applied.
5) User clears the search input; the query parameter is removed from the URL and the full, unfiltered list for the active section is restored.

Workflow: Open a specific question via a shared link
1) User receives a URL such as help.html?sectionId=<some-section-id>&questionIndex=<number>.
2) On page load, the controller calls getFAQContent and waits for the sections array.
3) When sections are available, the controller locates the section whose id matches sectionId. If no match is found, it falls back to the first section.
4) The controller interprets questionIndex as a zero-based index within the active section's questions array. If the index is in range, that question is set to expanded state; otherwise, no question is auto-expanded.
5) The page scrolls to the expanded question.
6) If the user clicks another question, the controller updates questionIndex in the URL to the new index, keeping the state shareable.

Workflow: View user tool status and see related help
1) On page load, the controller calls getUserToolSummaries and receives counts for favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
2) The user-tools summary strip displays these counts.
3) User clicks one of the tool summary items (for example, the entry representing favorites).
4) The controller writes a tool parameter to the URL (for example, tool=favorites) and optionally adjusts the active section to the first section whose title or questions best match that tool keyword, by scanning the loaded FAQ data.
5) The question list automatically scrolls to, or highlights, questions related to that tool (based on matches in question text, answer content, or related_task_ids), so the user immediately sees help relevant to the selected tool.
6) If the user clears the tool filter, the tool parameter is removed from the URL and the content returns to the standard view for the active section.

Workflow: Filter help by task (using related_task_ids)
1) The controller inspects related_task_ids across all questions from getFAQContent to build a unique set of task identifiers.
2) The task filter bar lists these task identifiers as selectable filters, displaying the raw identifier strings returned from the API.
3) User clicks a task identifier in the filter bar.
4) The controller writes taskId=<selected-task-id> to the URL.
5) The question list filters to show only questions that include the selected task ID in their related_task_ids array, across all sections.
6) User clicks a clear control on the task filter bar; taskId is removed from the URL and the full unfiltered set (or other active filters such as query) is restored.

Workflow: Use map-related or browse-related help and navigate to the corresponding tools
1) User opens a question about browsing events or using the map view by clicking on it in the question list.
2) The answer_html for that question is rendered. It may contain contextual links implemented by the backend (for example, an anchor designed to navigate to browsing events or map view).
3) When the user clicks an answer link that has been designated for event browsing (for example, via a data attribute or specific href pattern that the front-end recognizes), the click handler triggers navigation to browse.html with the URL parameter categoryId=events.
4) When the user clicks an answer link related to the map, the click handler triggers navigation to map.html (with any additional parameters encoded in the link if provided by the backend).
5) The user arrives on the destination page with the correct context (events category in browse.html or map view in map.html), returning later to help.html if needed via the browser’s back button.
interactions: Section selection: Clicking a section entry in the sidebar sets that section as active, updates the URL parameter sectionId to the section.id value, and refreshes the question list to show questions from that section. If a questionIndex parameter is present, it is reset or reinterpreted in the context of the new section.

Question expand/collapse: Clicking a question row toggles visibility of its answer. When a question is expanded, the URL parameter questionIndex is set to the index of that question within the active section’s questions array. If the same question is collapsed, questionIndex is removed from the URL (or set to a neutral value) to avoid implying a specific open question.

FAQ search: Typing into the search input updates a local search query state. On submit (or live update), the query is encoded in the URL as query. Clearing the input removes query from the URL. The question list filters questions based on the query while preserving other filters such as taskId.

Task filter selection: Clicking a task token in the task filter bar sets taskId in the URL to that token’s identifier. Only questions whose related_task_ids include that identifier remain visible. Clicking the same token again or a clear button removes taskId from the URL and restores the broader question set.

Tool summary click: Clicking a tool in the user-tools summary strip (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) writes tool=<tool-key> to the URL. The controller then adjusts the active section and/or internal highlighting to focus on questions most relevant to that tool. Clicking a close icon on the summary strip or a 'clear tool context' action removes the tool parameter.

In-answer navigation links: When the user clicks links rendered from answer_html, a delegated click handler examines the link. If it matches a recognized pattern or data attribute for a contextual navigation action to browse.html, the handler ensures navigation to browse.html?categoryId=events. If the link corresponds to map navigation, the handler navigates to map.html. Other links are followed as standard in-page links, subject to any global routing rules.

URL-driven state: When the URL changes (for example via back/forward navigation), the controller re-parses sectionId, questionIndex, query, taskId, and tool, and re-applies them to the page state without reloading interface data, so that navigation history works as expected.

Initial load and data readiness: On initial page load, the controller calls getFAQContent and getUserToolSummaries in parallel. Components that depend on these interfaces render loading states until data is ready. If getFAQContent returns an empty sections array, the UI shows an appropriate 'no content' state backed by logic rather than static FAQ items, and search/filters are disabled until data is available.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL query parameters to represent its state so that it is fully bookmarkable and shareable. The recognized parameters include:

- sectionId: A string matching one of the section.id values from getFAQContent.sections. If provided and valid, the corresponding section becomes active on load or URL change. If sectionId is missing or does not match any known section, the first section in the sections array is used.

- questionIndex: A non-negative integer interpreted as the index of a question within the active section’s questions array. If present and within bounds, the specified question is auto-expanded. If invalid (negative or out of range), it is ignored. Changing sectionId may cause questionIndex to be cleared or re-applied, depending on whether it still points to a valid question in the new section.

- query: A free-text string used by the search bar to filter questions. When present, the question list applies a text match over both question and answer_html (converted to plain text for search). Removing this parameter clears the search filter. The search filter can be combined with taskId and sectionId, and the intersection of all active filters determines which questions are displayed.

- taskId: A string that must match one of the values found in the related_task_ids arrays returned from getFAQContent. When present, only questions whose related_task_ids contain this value are shown, across all sections unless the UI is configured to constrain to the active section. Removing taskId from the URL restores the unfiltered question set (subject to any query filter).

- tool: A string indicating the user tool context (for example, favorites, day_plan, watchlist, cart). When present, the controller uses this to adjust how sections and questions are prioritized or highlighted. It may, for example, automatically set sectionId to the first section whose title or questions include the tool keyword. Removing tool returns the page to a neutral context.

These parameters are updated using the History API (pushState/replaceState) in response to user actions such as section selection, question expand/collapse, search submission, task filter selection, and tool summary clicks. On popstate events, the controller re-computes the active section, filters, and question expansion using the current URL without re-calling getFAQContent or getUserToolSummaries, ensuring consistent navigation behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading via getFAQContent and getUserToolSummaries, interprets URL parameters (sectionId, questionIndex, query, taskId, tool), maintains derived page state (active section, active question, active filters, tool context), and passes this state and data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when help.html is loaded. Triggers parallel calls to getFAQContent() and getUserToolSummaries(). After getFAQContent resolves, chooses the active section based on URL.query.sectionId or falls back to the first section. After both interfaces resolve, notifies child components that data is ready.
onUrlChange: Listens to browser popstate events. Re-reads URL parameters and recomputes active section, active question, and filters without re-fetching data, then updates child components.

onSectionChange: Receives events from the section sidebar when the user selects a section. Updates internal active section state, writes sectionId to the URL, and resets or updates questionIndex as appropriate using pushState or replaceState.

onQuestionToggle: Receives events when a question is expanded or collapsed. When expanded, sets questionIndex in the URL to the question’s index within the active section. When collapsed, removes questionIndex from the URL. Ensures that only one question is treated as 'primary' for deep-linking, even if multiple are open in the UI.
onSearchUpdate: Receives search query changes from the search bar. Writes query to the URL (or removes it if empty), and recomputes which questions are visible in the list.
onTaskFilterChange: Receives selected or cleared taskId from the task filter bar. Writes taskId to or removes it from the URL, and recomputes visible questions.

onToolContextChange: Receives selected tool context from the user-tools summary strip. Writes tool to or removes it from the URL, and optionally adjusts active section by scanning getFAQContent.sections for the best match for that tool key.

onInAnswerNavigation: Receives events from the question list when a link within answer_html triggers a contextual navigation action. Performs navigation to browse.html?categoryId=events or map.html according to the link’s encoded instruction.

components.functionality: Displays a list of FAQ sections returned from getFAQContent. Allows the user to switch the active section and see the corresponding questions.
event_handlers: onSectionClick: Triggered when the user clicks a section entry. Sends the clicked section.id to faq-page-controller.onSectionChange so the controller can update sectionId in the URL and propagate the new active section.

onSectionsDataChange: Triggered when getFAQContent.sections changes (initial load or refresh). Re-renders the section list and ensures the active section is highlighted according to faq-page-controller.activeSectionId.

components.functionality: Provides an input for users to search across all FAQ questions and answers. It synchronizes the search term with the query URL parameter and informs the controller when the search query changes.
event_handlers: onChange: Fired on every input change (or debounced). Updates a local search term and optionally calls faq-page-controller.onSearchUpdate so that the controller writes query to the URL and updates filtering.
onSubmit: Fired when the user submits the search form. Prevents the default full-page reload and calls faq-page-controller.onSearchUpdate with the current search term.
onClear: Fired when the user clears the search input. Calls faq-page-controller.onSearchUpdate with an empty string so that the controller removes query from the URL and clears the filter.

components.functionality: Extracts unique task identifiers from related_task_ids across all questions and displays them as selectable filters. Allows user to see all help content related to a specific task, such as planning a day or managing registrations.
event_handlers: onTaskFilterClick: Fired when the user clicks a task identifier. Sends the selected identifier to faq-page-controller.onTaskFilterChange, which writes taskId to the URL and updates filtering.
onClearTaskFilter: Fired when the user clears the task filter. Calls faq-page-controller.onTaskFilterChange with a null/empty value so the controller removes taskId from the URL.
onFaqDataChange: Fired when getFAQContent.sections changes. Rebuilds the list of unique task identifiers from related_task_ids so the filter bar always reflects current data.

components.functionality: Shows the list of questions and answers for the active section, filtered by search query and taskId. Questions are clickable rows that expand to reveal answer_html. The component also handles in-answer link clicks for contextual navigation.
event_handlers: onQuestionClick: Fired when the user clicks a question row. Determines the index of the clicked question within the active section’s questions array and toggles its expanded state. Calls faq-page-controller.onQuestionToggle with the question index and the new expanded/collapsed state.

onRenderAnswerHtml: On question expansion, renders the answer_html content for that question using a safe HTML rendering pipeline. Attaches delegated click handlers to any anchor elements inside the rendered content.

onAnswerLinkClick: When a link inside answer_html is clicked, inspects the link’s attributes (such as href or data attributes). If the link corresponds to a recognized contextual navigation action (for browsing events or viewing the map), prevents default behavior and calls faq-page-controller.onInAnswerNavigation with a payload indicating the target (browse or map). Otherwise, allows the link to behave as a normal navigation link.

onFilterStateChange: Listens to changes from faq-page-controller regarding query, taskId, and activeSectionId. Re-applies filtering to determine which questions are visible and whether questionIndex is valid for the current view. If questionIndex no longer points to a visible question (for example, filtered out), no question is auto-expanded.

components.functionality: Displays summary counts for favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, and cart items using getUserToolSummaries. Allows the user to click a tool to focus help content on that tool.
event_handlers: onToolClick: Fired when the user clicks one of the tool summary items. Sends a tool key (for example, 'favorites', 'day_plan', 'watchlist', or 'cart') to faq-page-controller.onToolContextChange, which writes tool to the URL and adjusts the help context.
onClearToolContext: Fired when the user clears the tool-based view. Calls faq-page-controller.onToolContextChange with a null/empty value so the controller removes tool from the URL.

onToolSummariesDataChange: Fired when getUserToolSummaries data is updated. Re-renders the summary counts and updates any visual indicators that reflect which tool is currently selected via URL.query.tool.

components.functionality: Shows the currently active help context derived from URL parameters and controller state (active section, search query, task filter, tool context), and offers quick controls to clear filters.
event_handlers: onClearSearchClick: When the user clicks to clear the search indicator, calls faq-page-controller.onSearchUpdate with an empty string so the controller removes query from the URL.
onClearTaskFilterClick: When the user clears the task filter via this bar, calls faq-page-controller.onTaskFilterChange with null so taskId is removed from the URL.
onClearToolContextClick: When the user clears the tool context via this bar, calls faq-page-controller.onToolContextChange with null so tool is removed from the URL.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Preserve the mockup’s top-down structure: global header (logo, location, main nav) sits above a single, large white content card. Inside this card, the Help & FAQ page follows a clear vertical stack first, then a two-column split: a top band with the FAQ search bar and filters spanning the full card width, followed by an 8-column main content area on the left (FAQ questions) and a 4-column right sidebar (help sections and user tool status). The controller component conceptually wraps the entire card and defines this structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Preserve the mockup’s top-down structure: global header (logo, location, main nav) sits above a single, large white content card. Inside this card, the Help & FAQ page follows a clear vertical stack first, then a two-column split: a top band with the FAQ search bar and filters spanning the full card width, followed by an 8-column main content area on the left (FAQ questions) and a 4-column right sidebar (help sections and user tool status). The controller component conceptually wraps the entire card and defines this structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered, fixed-width container on a light gray background. The white content card occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with generous outer margins. Inside the card, the top search-and-filter band takes about 20–25% of the card’s vertical height on first load, and the main two-column content occupies the remaining 75–80%. Horizontally, about 65–70% of the card width is allocated to the FAQ question list on the left (approx. 8 of 12 columns) and 30–35% to the sidebar on the right (approx. 4 of 12 columns), mirroring the original hero + events split.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use a moderately spacious, editorial density. The search bar is wide and visually dominant but surrounded by ample whitespace above and below. The filter bar is compact but clearly spaced, using the 8–16px scale. The question accordion list is dense in content but separated by clear dividers and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. The right sidebar packs a vertical list of help sections and a tools summary, but uses light dividers and 8–12px vertical spacing per item so it feels organized, not crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye top-down then left-right, similar to the mockup. After the global header, the eye lands on the large white card and first encounters the central FAQ search bar. From there, attention moves slightly down to the filter bar and the small status indicator on the right of that row. The next strong pull is to the left, into the tall FAQ question list where users will spend most of their time reading.
