The right sidebar, with a slightly colored or accented header and compact navigation list, forms a secondary vertical reading path: users glance right to jump between help sections or check their tool status, then return to the main content on the left.

layout.overall_description: The Help & FAQ page appears within the same centered, magazine-style frame as the rest of the site: a light gray background fills the viewport, and in the center sits a large white card with soft shadows and generous margins beneath the global logo/location/nav header. Inside this card, a prominent FAQ search bar spans most of the width near the top, with plenty of whitespace so it reads as the primary action. Directly beneath, a slim horizontal filter bar runs left-to-right, while a small status indicator pill anchors to the top-right of this band, giving a quick sense of system or account state. Below this header region, the card splits into two columns: on the left, a wide, scrollable list of FAQ questions in an accordion pattern fills about two-thirds of the card, creating the main reading area; on the right, a narrower vertical sidebar takes the remaining third, stacking a labeled list of help sections above a concise summary of the user’s tools and their status. The right sidebar subtly mirrors the ‘Today’s Events’ panel from the original mockup, using a slight color accent in its header and compact text rows, while the left content column maintains an airy but information-rich feel. Together, the elements create a clear flow from search, to filters and status, then into detailed answers, with the sidebar always available as a quick navigation and status reference as users scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller defines the main white content card that sits centered beneath the site’s global header. It occupies a fixed max-width (aligned to the 12-column grid) and is horizontally centered within the light gray page background, with approximately 40–64px of margin on the left and right and a similar vertical margin separating it from the header above and the bottom of the viewport. Inside this card, the controller lays out a vertical stack: first the FAQ search bar, then the filter + status band, and then the main two-column content region. The two-column region is configured as an 8/4 split on desktop: the left 8 columns host the FAQ question list, and the right 4 columns host the sidebar navigation list and user tools summary arranged vertically. The controller ensures consistent internal padding of about 24px on all sides of the card and between major content blocks, maintaining the mockup’s spacious, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural container in normal document flow, establishing the centered content card and the underlying 12-column grid; it does not itself appear as a separate visual element beyond defining the card’s padding, max-width, and two-column layout behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search bar is placed at the very top of the white content card, immediately below the card’s internal top padding, and is the first interactive element users see. It is centered horizontally within the card, spanning roughly 60–70% of the card’s width on desktop, leaving generous whitespace on both sides so it feels like a hero element. There is approximately 24–32px of whitespace above and below the search bar before other elements, reinforcing its importance. The input field is a single-line search box with rounded corners, aligned horizontally, and any supporting text (e.g., placeholder or hint) sits inside the field. On larger screens, the search bar does not extend all the way to the edges of the card; instead, it sits comfortably within the inner grid columns, visually anchoring the page content and echoing the central hero focus from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in normal flow at the top of the card; on longer pages it scrolls away with content, maintaining a clean, document-like experience rather than a fixed search header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ task filter bar sits directly beneath the search bar, separated by about 16–24px of vertical spacing. It stretches horizontally across nearly the full inner width of the card, aligning to the 12-column grid, with its primary filter controls left-aligned starting at the same left inset as the question list below. Visually, it reads as a slim horizontal band of pill-shaped filter buttons or segmented controls that describe help topics like ‘Search & Filters’, ‘Favorites’, ‘Day Plans’, ‘Registrations’, etc. Each filter pill has around 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, matching the design DNA. The filter bar’s height is relatively compact compared to the search bar, ensuring it supports rather than competes with it. On the right side of this same horizontal row, leaving a comfortable 16–24px gap, the FAQ state indicator aligns to the top-right, creating a single integrated control band that spans from left filters to right status.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal flow under the search bar. On scroll, it moves with the content; it is not sticky, preserving the editorial, page-like feel of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ state indicator appears in the same horizontal band as the filter bar, anchored to the right edge of the white card’s inner content area. It is a compact horizontal pill or bar—roughly one grid column wide—aligned vertically with the filter controls on the left. There is around 16px of horizontal space between the last filter control and the start of the state indicator, so it feels visually distinct but part of the same row. Its height is modest, comparable to a button, and it may use a subtle color accent (e.g., a soft green or yellow tint) to communicate status without overpowering the primary content. The indicator’s text (e.g., ‘All tools active’, ‘Some features limited’) is centered within the pill. This placement gives users a quick rightward glance to understand their current tool or system status immediately after refining what they’re looking for with filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the filter bar in the normal flow, aligned to the right within its row; it scrolls away with the filter bar and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ question list occupies the left main column of the card, starting directly below the filter/status band. It begins after a vertical gap of about 24px, reinforcing a clear separation between controls and content. This list spans approximately 8 out of 12 grid columns, taking up about 65–70% of the card’s width, and runs from the left inner margin of the card to the central column boundary. Each FAQ entry is an accordion item with a bold question title row and a collapsible answer body beneath. Question rows have around 16px vertical padding and are separated by subtle dividers or 8–12px vertical spacing, resulting in a column that feels information-rich but not jammed. As answers expand, they flow downward in this left column, pushing later questions further down while the right sidebar remains visually aligned alongside. The top of this column aligns horizontally with the top of the sidebar navigation, creating a balanced two-column grid reminiscent of the hero-and-events split in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the main scrolling content area, occupying the left portion of the two-column layout. It scrolls vertically as users browse answers, while the right sidebar may stay partially visible depending on screen height and sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ section sidebar sits in the right-hand column of the main content area, aligned to begin at the same vertical position as the top of the FAQ question list. It occupies about 4 of the 12 grid columns, roughly 30–35% of the card width, starting at the column boundary to the right of the question list. This sidebar is structured as a vertically stacked navigation list of help categories, such as ‘Getting Started’, ‘Using Search’, ‘Managing Favorites’, ‘Day Plans’, ‘Registrations & Payments’, and ‘Memberships’. At the top of this right column, a labeled header bar—optionally with a soft brand accent color similar to the mockup’s pink ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ strip—clearly titles the section (e.g., ‘HELP SECTIONS’). Below, individual section links are arranged in a single column, each with 8–12px vertical padding and minimal but consistent spacing. The entire sidebar is contained within a subtle card or panel with about 24px internal padding, giving it a distinct, framed appearance while visually echoing the vertical events list from the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured to behave as a sticky sidebar on taller pages: on desktop, once the top of this panel reaches the top of the viewport, it remains fixed relative to the viewport while the left FAQ content scrolls, as long as there is vertical space within the parent card. This keeps navigation options visible without breaking the card’s centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user tools summary strip is positioned directly beneath the FAQ section sidebar within the same right-hand column, separated from it by about 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of that column and appears as a shorter, horizontal card or band summarizing the user’s tools (e.g., counts of favorites, active day plans, saved searches, or membership status). Internally, it may be laid out as 2–3 compact horizontal segments or icon-label pairs, each with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, aligned left-to-right within the card. Compared to the sidebar list above, this strip is lower in height and denser, yet still respects the spacing system so it doesn’t feel cramped. Visually, it serves as a quick-glance dashboard aligned to the right side of the page, mirroring the idea of a ‘status panel’ similar in importance to the events sidebar in the mockup but with a more utilitarian, textual presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal flow below the section sidebar within the right column. When the sidebar is sticky, the summary strip scrolls along with the page until it reaches the bottom edge of the parent card, at which point it may scroll out of view while the main navigation portion remains fixed.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that retrieves and displays the current privacy policy and shows how user tools (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) relate to stored data, using dynamic content from backend interfaces.

core_features: Load and render the privacy policy content from the backend using getPrivacyPolicyContent, including the dynamically provided HTML body and last-updated timestamp.
Provide in-page navigation to different sections of the privacy policy by parsing the returned HTML headings and linking them to URL parameters for deep-linking and scrolling.
Synchronize the visible policy section with URL parameters (for example, a `section` parameter) so that specific sections can be linked to and revisited directly.
Display a dynamic summary of user tool counts (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, cart items) using getUserToolSummaries to help explain how data is used by those tools.
Allow the user to optionally hide or show the user tools summary bar based on a URL parameter, without using local storage.
Handle loading and error states for both interfaces, with a retry action that re-calls the respective interface without hardcoding any policy content or counts.
Support auxiliary actions relevant to a policy page (such as printing or copying a section-specific link) while keeping all content itself fully driven by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View the current privacy policy
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer and lands on privacy.html (no parameters, or with arbitrary parameters).
2. On page load, the page controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state (no hardcoded policy data).
4. Once data is returned, the privacy-content-viewer renders the body_html and displays the last_updated value.
5. The privacy-toc-sidebar parses headings from the rendered content and builds a clickable list of sections.
6. If a URL parameter `section` is present, the content viewer scrolls to the corresponding section and highlights it.
7. User can read the policy, click headings in the table of contents, or print the page.

Workflow: Open the privacy policy focused on a specific section
1. User clicks a link from elsewhere in the site (for example, from a cookie banner) that points to privacy.html with a `section` URL parameter.
2. On page load, the controller reads the `section` parameter and stores it as the target section key.
3. The page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and renders the body_html.
4. Once rendering completes, the content viewer searches for the target section anchor (for example, by id or data attribute derived from the heading text) and scrolls it into view.
5. The privacy-toc-sidebar marks that section as active, and any internal state (such as expanded/collapsed sections) is set so that the targeted section is visible.
6. If the user clicks another section in the TOC, the controller updates the `section` parameter via the History API and repeats the scroll-to-section behavior.

Workflow: Check how saved tools relate to data usage
1. User opens privacy.html and the page controller checks the URL parameter `hideTools`.
2. If `hideTools` is not set to a value that indicates hiding, the controller calls getUserToolSummaries in parallel with getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. After getUserToolSummaries returns, the user-tools-summary-bar displays the counts for favorites, day plan today items, watchlist items, and cart items.
4. The user can click on any of the tool labels (for example, 'Favorites') to navigate to the respective tool page (e.g., favorites.html) using standard navigation URLs and parameters defined elsewhere.
5. The user can also click within the policy content (if such headings exist in the dynamic HTML) to scroll to sections that conceptually describe how those tools use data.
6. If the URL parameter `hideTools` is set to a value indicating that the bar should be hidden, the page skips calling getUserToolSummaries and renders the policy without the summary bar.

Workflow: Share or bookmark a specific policy section
1. User is reading privacy.html and scrolls to a particular section.
2. The user clicks a 'copy link to this section' control associated with that section.
3. The policy-link-share-control determines the section identifier (used internally by the TOC and the `section` URL parameter).
4. The control constructs a URL to privacy.html that includes `section=<sectionKey>` and updates the browser address bar via the History API.
5. The control copies that URL to the clipboard using browser APIs, without modifying any server data.
6. When another user visits the copied URL, the 'Open the privacy policy focused on a specific section' workflow is followed.

Workflow: Recover from loading errors
1. User navigates to privacy.html.
2. getPrivacyPolicyContent or getUserToolSummaries fails (for example, due to network issues).
3. The relevant component (privacy-content-viewer or user-tools-summary-bar) shows a generic error state with a 'Retry' action.
4. When the user clicks 'Retry', the page controller calls only the failed interface again.
5. On success, the component is updated with the new dynamic data; on repeated failure, the error state remains visible without showing any hardcoded fallback privacy text or counts.
interactions: Click on a table-of-contents entry: updates the `section` URL parameter, scrolls to the corresponding heading in body_html, and sets that entry as active.

Click on a heading within the policy content (if headings are rendered as anchors): scrolls to that heading (no state change) or can optionally update the `section` parameter to match the clicked heading.
Click on 'Print' control in the content viewer: triggers the browser print dialog for the current page without altering any data.
Click on 'Copy link to this section': constructs a URL including the current `section` parameter for the active section and copies it to clipboard.

Toggle view mode (for example, compact vs full) using a control associated with the policy content: updates a `view` URL parameter and re-renders the policy with collapsed or expanded sections entirely on the client side.

Click on the 'Favorites', 'Day plan', 'Watchlist', or 'Cart' summary entries in the user-tools-summary-bar: navigates the browser to the respective tool pages (for example, favorites.html, day-plan.html) using standard links.
Click on 'Retry' in an error state for the policy content: re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and replaces the previous loading or error UI with the new result.
Click on 'Retry' in an error state for the user tools summary: re-invokes getUserToolSummaries and updates the summary bar once data is available.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page relies solely on URL parameters (not localStorage or cookies) to manage its front-end state. The key URL parameters are:

1. `section`: A string key identifying a particular section of the privacy policy. It is not passed to any interface. On initial load, the page controller reads `section`. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves and the HTML is rendered, the privacy-content-viewer attempts to locate the DOM node representing that section (for example, by matching an id or data attribute derived from headings in body_html). If found, it scrolls that node into view and visually highlights it. When a user clicks a TOC entry or a 'copy link to section' control, the page updates `section` in the URL via the History API without reloading. If `section` is missing, the page shows the top of the policy content by default.

2. `view`: A string that controls how the policy content is presented (for example, 'compact' vs 'full'). It is only used on the client side to determine which sections are expanded or collapsed. It is not sent to any backend interface. When the user toggles view mode, the page updates `view` in the URL (again via the History API), and the privacy-content-viewer re-renders the content accordingly (for example, by collapsing sections other than the active one in 'compact' view).

3. `hideTools`: A flag (for example, '1' to hide) that indicates whether the user-tools-summary-bar should be rendered. On initial load, if `hideTools` indicates hiding, the page controller will not call getUserToolSummaries and will omit the user-tools-summary-bar component. If `hideTools` indicates showing (or is absent), the controller calls getUserToolSummaries and passes the results to the summary bar. User interactions may change this parameter if there is a control to show/hide the bar.

The URL parameters are read on initial page load and on any subsequent `popstate` events so that the page remains in sync with browser navigation (back/forward). No interface calls are parameterized by these values because both getPrivacyPolicyContent and getUserToolSummaries are defined with no parameters. All dynamic content (policy text, last updated date, and user tool counts) comes from these interfaces, and the page never substitutes hardcoded data in their place.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter handling, and communication between the privacy content viewer, the table of contents, the user tools summary bar, and link-sharing controls.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Reads URL parameters, calls getPrivacyPolicyContent, and conditionally calls getUserToolSummaries based on URL.hideTools.
onUrlChange: Listens for browser popstate events, re-applies section highlighting/scrolling and view mode, and updates child components accordingly without re-calling interfaces unless necessary.
onPolicyContentLoaded: Receives notification from the content viewer that body_html is rendered, then applies scroll-to-section logic for the current URL.section.
onSectionChangeRequest: Called when the user selects a section via the TOC or content heading; updates URL.section using the History API and notifies the content viewer.
onViewModeToggle: Called when the user toggles between compact/full view; updates URL.view and notifies the content viewer.
onRetryPolicyLoad: Re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent after a previous failure and updates child components with new data.
onRetryToolsSummaryLoad: Re-invokes getUserToolSummaries after a previous failure and updates the user-tools-summary-bar.

components.functionality: Renders the privacy policy HTML body and last updated information, supports scroll-to-section behavior, optional compact/full display modes, print, and copy-section-link actions.
event_handlers: onInit: Receives body_html and last_updated from the page controller and renders them into the DOM without modification of the content itself.
onRenderComplete: Notifies the page controller that the DOM is ready so that scroll-to-section logic for URL.section can be applied.
onHeadingClick: When a heading is clicked (if headings are interactive), requests a section change from the page controller so that URL.section is updated and that heading becomes active.
onPrintClick: Calls window.print() (or equivalent) to print the current page without changing any state or data.

onCopySectionLinkClick: Determines the identifier for the current section (matching URL.section or a derived key), requests the page controller to update URL.section if needed, and then triggers the copy-to-clipboard behavior in the policy-link-share-control.
onViewModeToggleClick: When the user toggles compact/full mode from within the viewer, requests the page controller to update URL.view and re-renders sections based on the new mode.
onPolicyLoadErrorDisplay: Displays an error UI if getPrivacyPolicyContent failed and exposes a 'Retry' control that triggers onRetryPolicyLoad on the page controller.

components.functionality: Builds an in-page table of contents for the privacy policy using headings parsed from the rendered body_html and allows users to jump between sections while keeping the URL.section parameter in sync.
event_handlers: onInit: Receives body_html (or a reference to the rendered DOM) and parses headings to create a structured list of sections and section keys.
onTocItemClick: When a user clicks a TOC item, requests the page controller to change the active section (which updates URL.section and triggers scroll in the viewer).
onActiveSectionChange: Responds to changes in URL.section (via the page controller) and visually marks the corresponding TOC item as active.
onPolicyReload: Rebuilds the TOC if getPrivacyPolicyContent is re-fetched and body_html changes.

components.functionality: Displays dynamic counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, and cart items) based on getUserToolSummaries and provides navigation shortcuts to the corresponding tool pages, if visible.
event_handlers: onInit: If URL.hideTools does not indicate hiding and getUserToolSummaries has succeeded, renders the dynamic counts in the summary bar; if data is still loading, shows a loading state.

onToolLinkClick: When the user clicks on 'Favorites', 'Day plan', 'Watchlist', or 'Cart' sections of the bar, navigates to the corresponding page using standard links (for example, favorites.html) without additional API calls from this page.

onHideToggleClick: If a control is provided to hide the summary bar, requests the page controller to update URL.hideTools accordingly and hides the bar without calling getUserToolSummaries again in the same session.
onToolsSummaryLoadErrorDisplay: Displays an error message if getUserToolSummaries fails and exposes a 'Retry' control that triggers onRetryToolsSummaryLoad on the page controller.

components.functionality: Handles generation and copying of deep links to the privacy policy with a specific section parameter, working in coordination with the content viewer and the page controller.
event_handlers: onCopyLinkRequest: When invoked (for example, via a button in the content viewer), reads the current URL and URL.section from the page controller and constructs a full URL pointing to privacy.html with the appropriate `section` query parameter.
onClipboardCopy: Uses browser clipboard APIs to copy the constructed URL, and provides non-data-specific feedback to the user that the link has been copied.
onSectionChange: Updates internal state when URL.section changes so that any UI (for example, a small indicator of the link target) remains accurate.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the existing site header (logo → location → nav) intact at the top. Directly beneath it, center a single, prominent white content card controlled by the privacy-policy-page-controller. Inside this card, use a clearly defined top header row for the page title and policy-link-share-control. Below that, mirror the mockup’s two-column band: the privacy-content-viewer as the main left column (replacing the hero carousel) and the privacy-toc-sidebar as the right column (replacing the 'TODAY’S EVENTS' panel). At the bottom of the card, span the full width with the user-tools-summary-bar as a horizontal, editorial-style closing section explaining user tools and stored data.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the existing site header (logo → location → nav) intact at the top. Directly beneath it, center a single, prominent white content card controlled by the privacy-policy-page-controller. Inside this card, use a clearly defined top header row for the page title and policy-link-share-control. Below that, mirror the mockup’s two-column band: the privacy-content-viewer as the main left column (replacing the hero carousel) and the privacy-toc-sidebar as the right column (replacing the 'TODAY’S EVENTS' panel). At the bottom of the card, span the full width with the user-tools-summary-bar as a horizontal, editorial-style closing section explaining user tools and stored data.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered, fixed-width layout (max-width ~1200–1280px) with generous outer margins on a light gray page background. The header area above the card uses roughly 20–25% of the viewport height, while the main white card occupies the rest of the first viewport and extends downward as needed. Inside the card, allocate about 65–70% of the card width to the privacy-content-viewer on the left and 30–35% to the privacy-toc-sidebar on the right, with a 24–32px gutter between them. The page title and policy-link-share-control occupy a single full-width row at the top of the card, and the user-tools-summary-bar forms a low-height full-width band at the bottom.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use a moderately spacious density: the card has ~24px padding on all sides; the header row (title + utilities) has comfortable breathing room above and below (24–32px); the policy text inside the privacy-content-viewer is set with readable line length and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and subsections. The privacy-toc-sidebar is visually compact but readable, with tight but clear vertical separation between links. The user-tools-summary-bar is slightly denser than the main body text but still airy enough to scan easily, with clear grouping of each user tool.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a vertical editorial path: from the global logo/location/nav down to the 'Privacy Policy' title at the top of the card, then naturally into the main left column of policy content (privacy-content-viewer). Mirroring the mockup, a visually distinct right-hand band (privacy-toc-sidebar with a colored header) provides a strong secondary pull, encouraging users to scan section links after they recognize the main context. As users scroll down through the policy text, the sticky behavior of the right sidebar keeps orientation, and the flow concludes on the full-width user-tools-summary-bar at the bottom, which consolidates understanding of how favorites, day plan, watchlist, and cart relate to stored data. The policy-link-share-control sits at the top-right of the card as a tertiary focal point for advanced actions.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site header, the page opens into a calm, light gray canvas with a single, large white card centered on the screen. This card feels like a magazine feature: soft shadows, rounded corners (if used elsewhere in the system), and a generous margin from the viewport edges. At the top inside the card, a bold 'Privacy Policy' heading is left-aligned, while a small cluster of utility buttons (for printing, sharing, or copying the policy link) sits on the right, aligned along the same horizontal baseline. A subtle rule or extra spacing separates this header row from the main content band. The heart of the layout is a two-column arrangement: on the left, a wide column is dedicated to the policy text, structured as a readable article with clear subsections and comfortable line length; on the right, a narrower, card-like sidebar with a colored top bar echoes the mockup’s 'TODAY’S EVENTS' panel and lists the sections of the policy as a vertical table of contents. As the user scrolls down, the left column flows naturally, while the right table-of-contents panel stays visible (sticky) within the viewport on desktop, providing easy navigation. At the bottom of the main policy content, the layout returns to a single-column, full-width band: a lightly shaded user-tools-summary-bar that explains, in a compact but legible way, how each user tool (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) interacts with stored data, visually tying the abstract policy back to practical user features. The overall impression is editorial, clear, and friendly, with ample whitespace around each element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-page-controller acts as the overall framing container for the privacy policy card. It positions the content on a light gray page background, centering a single main white card both horizontally and within the vertical flow below the global header. The controller constrains the card to a max-width of approximately 1200–1280px and ensures generous outer margins (40–64px from viewport sides on desktop, 24–32px top and bottom). Inside this frame, it sets the internal padding of the card at roughly 24px on all sides and defines the vertical ordering: top header row (title + policy-link-share-control) → two-column content band (privacy-content-viewer left, privacy-toc-sidebar right) → bottom full-width user-tools-summary-bar. On narrower viewports, this controller reflows the layout to a single-column stack: header row at top, policy text below, then the table-of-contents block, then the user tools summary bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page. It establishes the centered, fixed-width card and the internal two-column grid on desktop, and handles responsive reflow to a stacked column layout on tablet and mobile.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-viewer occupies the left side of the main content band inside the card and functions as the primary reading area. On desktop, it spans roughly 65–70% of the card’s inner width, starting flush with the card’s left padding. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px beneath the header row before the first line of policy text begins, preserving the airy editorial feel. The text flows top to bottom with a comfortable line length (approximately 60–80 characters per line), 16–24px spacing between paragraphs, and clear visual separation between subsections (e.g., section headings with extra top margin and possibly light dividers). The right edge of this viewer aligns with the left edge of the vertical gutter (24–32px wide) separating it from the privacy-toc-sidebar. As users scroll, this column is the main scrollable content of the page; it extends downward as long as needed, stopping just above the user-tools-summary-bar, which follows it in the vertical flow. On smaller screens, this viewer becomes full-width within the card, sitting immediately below the header row and above the table-of-contents block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable column within the page; it does not have its own internal scroll container. It expands vertically with content and remains the central focus as users move down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-toc-sidebar visually echoes the mockup’s 'TODAY’S EVENTS' panel and sits to the right of the privacy-content-viewer within the same card. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the card’s inner width, separated from the main content by a 24–32px vertical gutter of empty space. The sidebar is vertically aligned so that its top edge sits just below the card’s header row, matching the top of the main policy text. It appears as a contained white panel or lightly elevated card inside the parent card, with its own internal padding of about 16–24px. A horizontal band across the top, using the brand’s pink (or analogous accent color), contains a concise label such as 'On this page' or 'Privacy Sections,' mimicking the strong colored header bar of the events panel. Below this bar, a vertical list of clickable section titles is presented with compact vertical spacing (8–12px between items) and clear typographic hierarchy to keep the list readable without feeling crowded. The overall height of the sidebar is determined by its content, but on long policies it is designed to remain visible as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the card on desktop: once the user scrolls past the header row, the sidebar’s top edge pins to a small offset from the top of the viewport (e.g., 24–32px) and remains in view while the main policy text scrolls alongside it. On tablets and mobile, it reflows below the main policy content as a full-width section (losing sticky behavior) or can optionally collapse into an expandable 'Jump to section' accordion above the policy text, depending on implementation detail.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user-tools-summary-bar is positioned at the bottom of the main card, directly beneath the last section of the privacy-content-viewer and spanning the full inner width of the card. It is separated from the policy text above by a distinct visual break—either extra vertical spacing (24–32px) and/or a light horizontal divider line—so it reads as a concluding, summary-focused section. The bar itself appears as a low-height, full-width band with a slightly tinted background (a very light gray or soft brand tint) to distinguish it from the pure white of the surrounding card, while maintaining the clean, editorial look. Inside, content is laid out horizontally on desktop where space permits: for example, a left-aligned heading like 'How your tools use your data' and then 2–4 columns or grouped rows, each representing a tool (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) with a short, 1–2 line explanation. Internal padding is consistent with other components (~24px), and elements are spaced so the bar feels compact but not cramped. On smaller screens, this bar stacks into a vertical list of tool explanations, still full-width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the end of the card’s content flow. It is not sticky; users reach it naturally as they finish scrolling through the policy. On very long policies, it serves as a visual and informational endpoint before the global footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-link-share-control is a compact utility cluster positioned in the top-right area inside the main card, sharing a horizontal row with the 'Privacy Policy' heading. The card’s header row has a full-width layout: the page title is aligned to the left, and this control is aligned to the right, both vertically centered within a row that is about 56–72px tall. The control may consist of a row of 2–4 small, pill-shaped buttons or icon+label buttons (e.g., 'Print,' 'Download PDF,' 'Copy link,' 'Share') with 8–12px horizontal spacing between them. It sits roughly 24px from the card’s right inner edge and at least 24px below the top inner edge, maintaining the established padding rhythm. Visually, it is understated—solid or outlined buttons with light weight—to avoid competing with the main title, but clear enough to be discoverable. As the user scrolls, this control leaves the viewport along with the header row, emphasizing its role as a start-of-page utility rather than a persistent tool.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the card’s header row; it scrolls away with the rest of the header as the user moves down the page. On smaller viewports, it may collapse into a single icon (e.g., a 'more' menu) aligned to the right of the title row or wrap onto a second line beneath the title while remaining within the top header area.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: This page retrieves and displays the website’s Terms & Conditions and shows summary counts for the user’s tools (favorites, today’s day plan, watchlist, cart). Users can deep-link to specific sections of the terms and navigate within the document.

core_features: Retrieve and render the latest Terms & Conditions content using getTermsContent, including the last updated timestamp.
Support deep-linking and in-page navigation to specific sections of the Terms & Conditions via URL parameters and anchors.
Provide an optional in-page text search/filter to help users locate relevant clauses within the Terms & Conditions content.

Fetch and display summary counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, cart items) using getUserToolSummaries to keep context about saved items while viewing legal terms.
Allow users to navigate from the Terms & Conditions page to other tool-related pages (favorites, day plan, watchlist, cart) using standard link navigation with URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View current Terms & Conditions:
1. User clicks the 'Terms & Conditions' link in the footer of any page.
2. Browser navigates to terms.html (optionally with URL parameters such as section or highlight).
3. On load, the page controller calls getTermsContent to retrieve body_html and last_updated.
4. The Terms Content component renders body_html as structured HTML and displays the last_updated value.
5. The Table of Contents component analyzes the rendered headings in body_html and builds an in-page navigation list.
6. If a section parameter or URL hash is present, the page scrolls to the corresponding section anchor.

Navigate to a specific section from another page:
1. Another page links to terms.html with a section query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=someSectionId) or a hash fragment.
2. On load, the page controller reads the URL parameter and/or hash.
3. After body_html is rendered, the controller locates the matching section anchor within the Terms Content.
4. The page scrolls to that section and optionally visually indicates the active section in the Table of Contents.
5. If the user clicks another section in the Table of Contents, the URL is updated (query parameter and/or hash), and the view scrolls accordingly.

Search within Terms & Conditions content:
1. User types a keyword or phrase into the in-page search field.
2. The search component filters or highlights matches in the rendered body_html without additional API calls.
3. If the highlight URL parameter is present on initial load, the search field is pre-filled and the content is highlighted accordingly.
4. User can clear the search field to remove any highlights and return to the default view.

View user tool summaries while reading terms:
1. On page load, the controller calls getUserToolSummaries.
2. The User Tools Summary Bar displays the counts for favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
3. User sees how many items they currently have in each tool while reviewing the terms related to events, memberships, and registrations.
4. User can click on a tool summary link (e.g., Favorites, Day Plan, Watchlist, Cart), which navigates to the corresponding page using URL-based navigation.

Refresh Terms & Conditions content if needed:
1. User triggers a manual refresh action (e.g., via a simple 'Reload' control or browser reload).
2. The controller re-invokes getTermsContent.
3. The Terms Content and last_updated display are updated with the latest returned values.
4. If a section parameter or search highlight parameter is present, they are reapplied after the new content is rendered.
interactions:
On page load, the initialization component:
- Parses the current URL to extract query parameters (e.g., section, highlight) and hash fragment.
- Invokes getTermsContent to retrieve body_html and last_updated.
- Invokes getUserToolSummaries to retrieve favorites_count, day_plan_today_items_count, watchlist_count, and cart_items_count.
- Passes the retrieved data to the respective components for rendering.

In-page navigation via Table of Contents:
- User clicks a Table of Contents item representing a section.
- The click handler updates the URL (query parameter and/or hash) to reflect the selected section.
- The page scrolls to the corresponding anchor within the rendered body_html.
- The active section is indicated in the Table of Contents based on scroll position or the current URL state.

URL-driven deep-link behavior:
- When the URL contains a section parameter, the Terms Content component scrolls to that anchor after body_html is rendered.
- When the URL contains a highlight parameter, the Search component pre-fills the search input and applies highlighting logic to the body_html.
- When the URL hash changes (e.g., user clicks a deep link or manually edits the hash), the page responds by scrolling to the matching anchor.

In-page search interaction:
- User types into the search field; on each input change or on explicit submit, the Search component scans the rendered body_html and applies a highlight or scroll-to-first-match behavior.
- Clearing the search input removes any highlighting and resets any search-related state.
- Optionally, when the search value changes, the component may update the highlight query parameter in the URL to preserve search state on reload or share links.

User tools summary bar interactions:
- After getUserToolSummaries returns, the User Tools Summary Bar binds the returned counts to their respective display elements.
- Each tool label (e.g., Favorites, Day Plan, Watchlist, Cart) is rendered as a clickable navigation element.
- When the user clicks one of these elements, the browser is navigated to the corresponding page (e.g., favorites.html, day-plan.html, watchlist.html, cart.html) using standard link navigation with relevant URL parameters for that target page if needed.

General link interactions inside Terms & Conditions content:
- Links within body_html (e.g., to privacy, contact, or external sites) are rendered as standard anchors.
- Clicking an internal link that references another section of the Terms updates the URL hash and triggers scrolling.
- Clicking a link to another page navigates away to that page via normal browser navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by URL parameters and the hash fragment, without use of localStorage. Supported URL-driven behaviors include:
- section (query parameter): A string identifier for a specific section within the Terms & Conditions. After getTermsContent returns and body_html is rendered, the page searches for an anchor or element whose id or data attribute matches this section value and scrolls it into view. When the user selects a section via the Table of Contents, the section parameter is updated to the corresponding identifier.
- highlight (query parameter): A string representing a search term to be highlighted within the Terms content. On initial load, if highlight is present, the Search component sets its internal search field value to this term and applies highlighting or scrolls to the first match within body_html. When the user updates the search term, the component may update the highlight parameter to match the current search term so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves this state.
- Hash fragment (#...): The hash may reference a section anchor embedded within the returned body_html. When the hash is present or changes, the page attempts to scroll to the corresponding anchor. The Table of Contents component can also update the hash when a user clicks a section. If both section and hash are present, the implementation can prioritize one consistently (e.g., section) and document that behavior.
- No terms-specific data, text, or identifiers are hardcoded in the page logic. All anchor identifiers used for section navigation are derived dynamically from the structure of body_html.
- The dynamic data returned by getTermsContent and getUserToolSummaries is treated as the single source of truth; the page does not cache or override any of these values using static defaults.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and URL-driven behavior for the Terms & Conditions page. On load, it calls getTermsContent and getUserToolSummaries, parses URL parameters (section, highlight) and hash, and passes resulting data and state to child components. It also listens for URL hash changes to trigger in-page navigation.

Renders the Terms & Conditions legal text using the body_html returned from getTermsContent, and shows the last_updated value. Exposes anchor points for each logical section of the content to support deep-linking and scrolling. Provides methods to scroll to a section when requested by other components or URL state.

Analyzes the rendered body_html structure (e.g., headings and anchors) to generate a dynamic list of sections. Displays these as clickable navigation items. When a section is clicked, it requests scrolling from the Terms Content Viewer and updates the URL parameters or hash to reflect the active section.

Provides an in-page search field for the Terms & Conditions content. Uses the search query to highlight or navigate to matching text within the rendered body_html. Reads the initial value from the highlight URL parameter (if present) and updates the URL parameter when the user changes the query.

Displays summary counts for user tools (favorites, today’s day plan items, watchlist items, cart items) based on the data returned by getUserToolSummaries. Each summary item is clickable and navigates to the corresponding tool page using standard link navigation and URL parameters as needed.

Provides a functional control (such as a link or button) that, when activated, triggers a re-fetch of the Terms & Conditions content via getTermsContent and re-renders the Terms Content Viewer and related components. This ensures users can manually request the most up-to-date version of the terms.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container. The hero content uses an approximate 8-column main area (carousel) and 4-column right sidebar (Today’s Events).
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the established global structure from the mockup: centered, fixed-width page with logo, location, and main navigation above. Beneath that, a single large white content card sits on a light gray background. Inside this card, preserve the two-column band: the left (roughly 8/12 columns) becomes the primary Terms & Conditions reading area, while the right (roughly 4/12 columns) becomes a functional sidebar combining the user tools summary and an in-page table of contents for the terms. A compact control row (search + refresh) runs along the top of the left content column, mirroring the utilitarian simplicity of the original navigation and carousel controls.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the established global structure from the mockup: centered, fixed-width page with logo, location, and main navigation above. Beneath that, a single large white content card sits on a light gray background. Inside this card, preserve the two-column band: the left (roughly 8/12 columns) becomes the primary Terms & Conditions reading area, while the right (roughly 4/12 columns) becomes a functional sidebar combining the user tools summary and an in-page table of contents for the terms. A compact control row (search + refresh) runs along the top of the left content column, mirroring the utilitarian simplicity of the original navigation and carousel controls.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main white terms card occupies about 70–80% of the viewport width, centered, with 48–64px of light gray margin around it on desktop. Inside the card, the left column (terms content + search) takes about 65–70% of the internal width; the right column (summary bar + table of contents) takes about 30–35%. Vertical spacing follows the 8px base scale: 24px internal padding for the card and major blocks, 16px spacing between functional rows, and 8–12px between list items in the table of contents. The header area above the card remains visually light and takes about the top 20–25% of the viewport, while the card holds most of the dense content below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density. The terms text area is readable and scrollable with comfortable line length and line height, not overly compressed. The search/filter and refresh controls share a compact row but are surrounded by breathing room above and below. The user tools summary bar uses compact but clearly separated metrics, while the table of contents is a simple stacked list with 8–12px vertical spacing per item. Overall, whitespace is used to separate functional zones (controls, reading area, sidebar) so nothing feels crowded, echoing the editorial, card-based feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a vertical-first, then lateral flow: logo and location at the very top, then the main navigation, then down into the large white terms card. Inside the card, the page title (implicit) and the search/filter at the top of the left column become the first focal point, analogous to the hero area. From there, the eye moves down into the scrolling terms content. The right sidebar creates a secondary attraction: the user tools summary bar at the top pulls the eye rightward, followed by the table of contents list which encourages a top-to-bottom scan. Deep linking behavior in the table of contents reinforces back-and-forth eye movement between right (section links) and left (section content) as the user navigates the document.

layout.overall_description: The Terms & Conditions page appears as a modern, editorial-style reading experience framed inside a generous, light gray background. At the top, the existing branding, location, and primary navigation occupy a clean band with ample vertical spacing, leading the eye naturally down to a large, centered white card that dominates the middle of the viewport. This card is padded with about 24px of internal space on all sides and is split into two columns: the left, wider column houses a compact control row (search/filter aligned left with a small refresh action aligned right) above a tall, scrollable Terms & Conditions content area; the right, narrower column stacks a concise user tools summary bar at the top and a vertically oriented table of contents below it, echoing the original ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ sidebar. The arrangement feels like a magazine spread: a strong primary reading area balanced by a functional, text-based sidebar, with generous margins and consistent spacing that keep the dense text of the terms readable and approachable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The init controller operates behind the scenes but manifests visually as a state layer for the entire main content card. When the page is loading or refreshing the Terms & Conditions, it presents a subtle overlay centered within the white content card: a semi-opaque white veil that covers both the left content column and the right sidebar, with a small spinner or progress indicator centered horizontally and vertically within the card. This overlay respects the card’s boundaries (it does not extend into the light gray page background or header area) and keeps the overall card silhouette intact. Once loading is complete, this layer disappears entirely, revealing the two-column layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-card overlay layer positioned above all other components within the main terms card, covering both columns while active, and otherwise remaining hidden. It is not scrollable; instead it temporarily disables interaction with underlying scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms content viewer occupies the primary left column within the main white card. It begins just below the search/filter and refresh control row, spanning the full width of the left 8-column area (approximately 65–70% of the card’s internal width). Its top edge sits about 16px beneath the controls, following the moderate spacing rhythm. The content viewer presents the Terms & Conditions as a vertically scrolling text region with generous line height and a comfortable line length suited to this width. Section headings in the terms align flush left with the column, creating clear visual anchors that correspond to the right-side table of contents. As the user scrolls, the viewer’s content extends downward inside the card while the card itself scrolls with the page; on taller terms, the content viewer will extend well below the initial viewport, maintaining its left-column alignment while the right sidebar (especially the table of contents) appears alongside it for as long as page height allows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the page flow. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float; any section anchoring is handled via jumps to anchored headings within this region when table of contents links are clicked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents is placed in the right-hand sidebar, directly beneath the user tools summary bar. It occupies the full width of the right 4-column area (about 30–35% of the card’s internal width) and extends vertically downwards, forming a tidy, stacked list of section links. At the top of this block, a simple textual heading (e.g., ‘TERMS SECTIONS’) is aligned flush left and may be paired with a subtle colored accent bar or underline, analogous to the bold pink ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ header in the mockup, helping it stand out. Each individual section link appears as a single text line with 8–12px vertical spacing between items, keeping the list compact but readable. Any nested subsections are indented slightly to the right to create a clear hierarchy while preserving the clean, text-first aesthetic. Visually, the table of contents forms a vertical spine that the eye can scan from top to bottom, with ample whitespace to the right (toward the card edge) and a consistent gutter to the left separating it from the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a right-column block that may use sticky behavior within the viewport: its top edge aligns under the user tools summary bar and can remain visible while the left content viewer scrolls, as long as space permits. It does not overlap the summary bar; both scroll together until the sticky threshold is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search/filter control is positioned at the top of the left main column inside the white card, immediately below any implicit page title. It is centered within the left column horizontally but left-aligned in terms of content, spanning roughly 70–80% of the left column’s width (so about 45–55% of the entire card width). The control is a single-line text field with 12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the input, matching the mockup’s soft, approachable form style. There is approximately 24px of vertical space above it (from the card’s top padding) and about 8–12px below it before the refresh control aligns in the same row or at the far right. On wider screens, the search field sits toward the left while the refresh control sits to the far right of the left column, creating a balanced horizontal bar reminiscent of the carousel controls in the mockup. On narrower screens, the search box can stack above the refresh control while maintaining 8–16px vertical separation, preserving the spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains fixed relative to the top of the terms content area; it scrolls away as the user moves down the document. It is not sticky, but when the page is first loaded or when the user scrolls back to the top of the card, it is one of the first interactive elements visible above the content viewer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user tools summary bar occupies the top portion of the right sidebar, aligning horizontally with the search/filter row in the left column. It spans the full width of the right 4-column area, with 24px internal padding on all sides, forming a compact card within the sidebar. Its top edge sits 24px below the card’s top interior edge, matching the vertical rhythm of the left column’s top padding. Inside, summary metrics for favorites, today’s plan, watchlist, and cart are laid out either in a 2×2 grid of small, pill-like tiles or as a row of neatly spaced items, each with minimal 8–12px horizontal gaps. The overall height of the summary bar is moderate—enough to be noticed at a glance without dominating the sidebar. This block gives a quick, at-a-glance status similar in visual weight to the ‘TODAY’S EVENTS’ header area in the mockup, anchoring the right column before the table of contents list begins below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column. It scrolls with the page but, if the sidebar adopts sticky behavior, the entire sidebar including this bar may remain pinned near the top of the viewport as the main terms content scrolls, ensuring persistent visibility of the user’s tools summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The refresh control is a small, clearly defined action placed in the same horizontal band as the search/filter at the top of the left column. It sits at the far right edge of the left 8-column area, horizontally aligned with the vertical center of the search input. This creates a single, cohesive control row: search on the left, refresh on the right, with a comfortable 16–24px gap between the right edge of the search field and the left edge of the refresh button. The control itself is compact—either an icon-only button or a small labeled button—with 8–12px internal padding, echoing the minimal navigation and arrow controls from the mockup’s carousel. There is 24px of top padding between this row and the card’s upper edge, and about 16px of vertical spacing beneath it before the terms content viewer begins, preserving the card’s hierarchy and breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the search/filter, non-sticky. It scrolls away with the rest of the control row when the user moves down into the terms content. When the page-init overlay is active, the refresh control is visually present but disabled and covered by the overlay layer.
